book.name: Acts
book.author: Luke, physician and traveling companion of Paul
book.writtenFrom: Traditionally Rome, though some scholars suggest Caesarea or Antioch
book.writtenTo: Theophilus, a Greek-speaking patron or official; by extension, Gentile believers and interested outsiders

explainer.overview: Acts is the sequel to the Gospel of Luke -- volume two of a two-part work. Where Luke told the story of what Jesus began to do and teach, Acts tells the story of what Jesus continued to do through his followers after the ascension. The book traces the explosive growth of the early church from a handful of frightened disciples in Jerusalem to a movement that reaches the capital of the Roman Empire. It is structured around Jesus' command in 1:8 -- 'You will be my witnesses in Jerusalem, and in all Judea and Samaria, and to the ends of the earth.' The narrative follows this geographic and ethnic expansion precisely: Jerusalem (chapters 1-7), Judea and Samaria (chapters 8-12), and the wider Gentile world (chapters 13-28). The Holy Spirit is the true main character, empowering ordinary people to do extraordinary things.
explainer.historicalContext: Acts covers roughly thirty years of history, from Jesus' ascension (c. 30-33 AD) to Paul's imprisonment in Rome (c. 60-62 AD). This was a period of enormous political and social upheaval. The Roman Empire was at its height under emperors Tiberius, Caligula, Claudius, and Nero. Judaism was fragmenting into competing sects -- Pharisees, Sadducees, Essenes, Zealots -- and tensions with Rome were building toward the catastrophic Jewish War of 66-70 AD. Christianity emerged in this volatile environment as a Jewish renewal movement that rapidly crossed ethnic boundaries. Luke writes as a careful historian, anchoring his narrative in verifiable details: named governors, specific cities, particular legal proceedings, and eyewitness accounts (the 'we' passages where Luke was personally present).
explainer.whyItMatters: Acts is the only historical account of how Christianity grew from a tiny Jewish sect into a worldwide movement. Without it, we would have no narrative bridge between the Gospels and Paul's letters. It shows how the early church navigated its most fundamental crisis -- whether Gentiles had to become Jews to follow Jesus -- and arrived at the revolutionary conclusion that the gospel was for everyone. The book also provides the template for what 'church' looks like: a Spirit-empowered community that shares resources, crosses social boundaries, endures persecution with joy, and keeps pushing outward to new people and places.
keyThemes: The Holy Spirit as the driving force behind the church's mission
The gospel expanding from Jews to Gentiles
Bold witness in the face of persecution
The sovereignty of God over human opposition
Community life -- sharing, prayer, and breaking of bread
The fulfillment of Old Testament prophecy in Jesus and the church

people.name: Peter (Simon Peter)
people.role: Lead apostle in the early Jerusalem church
people.bio: Peter dominates the first half of Acts (chapters 1-12). He preaches the first Christian sermon at Pentecost, performs the first apostolic healing, stands before the Sanhedrin with remarkable boldness, and -- most dramatically -- receives a vision that leads him to baptize the first Gentile converts. The fisherman who denied Jesus three times becomes the fearless leader of a revolutionary movement. His story in Acts shows what the Holy Spirit can do with a willing but imperfect person.
people.culturalContext: Peter was a Galilean fisherman with no formal education. The Jewish leaders noted that he was 'unschooled and ordinary' (4:13). Yet he became the primary spokesman for a movement that challenged both Jewish religious authority and Roman imperial claims. His willingness to enter a Gentile home (chapter 10) was a culture-shattering act for a devout Jew.

people.name: Paul (Saul of Tarsus)
people.role: Apostle to the Gentiles, missionary, theologian
people.bio: Paul dominates the second half of Acts (chapters 13-28). He begins as the church's most zealous persecutor -- present at Stephen's stoning, dragging believers from their homes. His encounter with the risen Jesus on the Damascus road transforms him into the church's greatest missionary. He undertakes three major missionary journeys across the Roman Empire, plants churches in major cities, argues successfully that Gentiles do not need to follow Jewish law, and ends the book under house arrest in Rome, still preaching the gospel. His life is a case study in radical transformation.
people.culturalContext: Paul had a foot in three worlds: a devout Pharisee trained by the famous rabbi Gamaliel, a Roman citizen with legal protections most Jews lacked, and a native of Tarsus -- a major center of Greek learning. This triple identity made him uniquely equipped to translate a Jewish message into terms the Greco-Roman world could understand.

people.name: Stephen
people.role: First Christian martyr, one of the seven deacons
people.bio: Stephen was chosen to serve tables (distribute food to widows) but quickly emerged as a powerful preacher and debater. His speech before the Sanhedrin in chapter 7 is the longest speech in Acts -- a masterful retelling of Israel's history that shows how Israel repeatedly rejected God's messengers. His stoning marks a turning point: the persecution that follows scatters believers beyond Jerusalem, inadvertently spreading the gospel wider.
people.culturalContext: Stephen was a Hellenistic Jew -- a Greek-speaking Jew from outside Palestine. His debate partners were from Hellenistic synagogues in Jerusalem. The tension between Hellenistic and Hebrew-speaking Jews was a fault line within early Christianity that Acts candidly addresses.

people.name: Barnabas
people.role: Encourager, missionary partner of Paul, bridge-builder
people.bio: Barnabas (meaning 'son of encouragement') was a Levite from Cyprus who sold a field and gave the money to the apostles. He vouched for the newly converted Paul when everyone else was afraid, partnered with Paul on the first missionary journey, and later parted ways with Paul over whether to give John Mark a second chance. Barnabas consistently championed people others had written off.
people.culturalContext: As a Levite from Cyprus, Barnabas bridged the worlds of Jewish tradition and Diaspora culture. His willingness to trust Paul when others would not, and to mentor the young Mark, shows the critical role of encouragers in any movement.

people.name: James (brother of Jesus)
people.bio: James was not a follower of Jesus during Jesus' ministry (Mark 3:21, John 7:5), but after the resurrection he became the leader of the Jerusalem church. At the Jerusalem Council (chapter 15), he delivered the deciding verdict that Gentile believers did not need to be circumcised. He represented the Jewish-Christian wing of the church and sought to balance faithfulness to Jewish tradition with openness to Gentile inclusion.
people.culturalContext: James was known as 'James the Just' for his strict personal piety. The Jewish historian Josephus records his execution in 62 AD. His leadership shows that the early church's structure was complex -- Peter was the apostolic leader, but James held authority in Jerusalem.

people.name: Silas (Silvanus)
people.role: Paul's missionary companion on the second journey
people.bio: Silas was a leader in the Jerusalem church and a Roman citizen. He was chosen to accompany Paul after the split with Barnabas. Together they were beaten and imprisoned in Philippi, where they famously sang hymns at midnight before an earthquake freed them. Silas combined prophetic gifting with the practical advantages of Roman citizenship.
people.culturalContext: Silas's Roman citizenship, like Paul's, provided legal protections that were crucial for the missionary enterprise. Roman citizens could not be beaten without a trial and could appeal to Caesar -- rights that Paul and Silas strategically exercised.

people.name: Timothy
people.bio: Timothy was the son of a Jewish mother and a Greek father, from Lystra. Paul chose him as a traveling companion and had him circumcised -- a pragmatic decision to avoid offending Jewish audiences. Timothy became one of Paul's most trusted coworkers, eventually leading the church in Ephesus. Two of Paul's letters are addressed to him.
people.culturalContext: Timothy's mixed heritage embodied the Jewish-Gentile bridge that the early church was building. His circumcision by Paul (after the Jerusalem Council decided it was not required for Gentiles) shows that Paul was willing to make cultural accommodations for the sake of the mission.

people.name: Lydia
people.role: First European convert, businesswoman, house church host
people.bio: Lydia was a dealer in purple cloth from the city of Thyatira, living in Philippi. She was a God-fearer -- a Gentile who worshiped the God of Israel without fully converting to Judaism. After hearing Paul speak by the river, she and her household were baptized, and she insisted that Paul and his companions stay at her home. Her house became the first church in Europe.
people.culturalContext: Purple dye was extremely expensive, extracted from murex sea snails. Lydia's trade made her a wealthy woman with her own household. Her story shows that women played significant leadership roles in the earliest churches -- she hosted and likely led the Philippian congregation.

people.name: Apollos
people.bio: Apollos was an educated Alexandrian Jew who knew the Scriptures thoroughly and spoke with great passion about Jesus. However, he only knew the baptism of John. Priscilla and Aquila took him aside and explained the full Christian message to him. He then became a powerful advocate for the faith, especially in Corinth.
people.culturalContext: Alexandria was the intellectual capital of the Jewish Diaspora, home to a major library and a tradition of sophisticated biblical interpretation. Apollos brought this intellectual tradition into early Christianity, complementing Paul's approach.

people.name: Priscilla and Aquila
people.bio: This married couple were Jewish Christians expelled from Rome under Claudius's edict (c. 49 AD). They met Paul in Corinth, shared his tentmaking trade, and became close ministry partners. They later instructed Apollos in Ephesus. Luke often names Priscilla first, which many scholars see as indicating she was the more prominent of the two in ministry.
people.culturalContext: The fact that Priscilla is frequently named before her husband was unusual in ancient literature and suggests she held a recognized teaching and leadership role in the early church.

people.name: Antonius Felix
people.role: Roman governor of Judea who imprisoned Paul
people.bio: Felix governed Judea from approximately 52-59 AD. He was a former slave who rose to power through his brother Pallas, a freedman of Emperor Claudius. He kept Paul imprisoned for two years, hoping for a bribe, and was eventually recalled to Rome. The Roman historian Tacitus described him as exercising royal power with the mind of a slave.
people.culturalContext: Felix's rule was marked by increasing violence between Jewish factions and Roman authorities. His willingness to keep an innocent man imprisoned to maintain political favor with the Jewish leaders illustrates the corruption of Roman provincial government.

people.name: Porcius Festus
people.role: Roman governor who succeeded Felix and heard Paul's case
people.bio: Festus replaced Felix as governor of Judea around 59 AD. He inherited Paul's case and, wanting to do the Jewish leaders a favor, suggested Paul be tried in Jerusalem. Paul, knowing this would be a death sentence, exercised his right as a Roman citizen to appeal to Caesar, setting in motion his journey to Rome.
people.culturalContext: Festus appears to have been a more competent administrator than Felix, but he was caught between Roman justice and Jewish political pressure -- the same dilemma Pontius Pilate faced with Jesus.

people.name: King Agrippa II
people.role: Last of the Herodian dynasty, heard Paul's defense
people.bio: Herod Agrippa II was the great-grandson of Herod the Great and the last king of the Herodian line. He heard Paul's defense in Caesarea and was moved enough to say, 'In a short time you will persuade me to become a Christian.' He agreed with Festus that Paul had done nothing deserving death or imprisonment.
people.culturalContext: Agrippa was an expert in Jewish customs and law, making him a valuable assessor for Festus. His near-conversion illustrates a recurring theme in Acts: the gospel is compelling even to those who ultimately resist it.

places.name: Jerusalem
places.description: The birthplace of the church. Acts opens here with the ascension, Pentecost, and the early community's explosive growth. It remains the center of authority for the mother church throughout the book. The Jerusalem Council (chapter 15) takes place here.
places.culturalSignificance: Jerusalem was the center of Jewish religious life, home to the Temple where God was believed to dwell. The church's birth at Pentecost in Jerusalem was theologically significant -- the Holy Spirit descended in the very city where Jesus was crucified, turning the site of defeat into the launch point for a global movement.

places.name: Antioch (of Syria)
places.description: The third-largest city in the Roman Empire and the launching pad for Gentile Christianity. Believers who fled persecution in Jerusalem brought the gospel here, and it was in Antioch that followers of Jesus were first called 'Christians.' This was Paul's home base for all three missionary journeys.
places.culturalSignificance: Antioch was a cosmopolitan city where Jews and Gentiles mixed more freely than in Jerusalem. Its church reflected this diversity -- the leadership team listed in 13:1 includes people from Africa, the Mediterranean, and the Middle East. Antioch became the model of what a multi-ethnic church could look like.

places.name: Damascus
places.description: The city where Saul was headed to persecute Christians when he encountered the risen Jesus on the road. After his conversion, he was baptized here by Ananias and began preaching in Damascus synagogues before escaping over the city wall in a basket.
places.culturalSignificance: Damascus was one of the oldest continuously inhabited cities in the world, with a significant Jewish population. Paul's conversion on the road to Damascus is one of the most pivotal events in Christian history -- the church's worst enemy became its greatest missionary.

places.name: Corinth
places.description: A major port city where Paul spent eighteen months establishing a church. He worked as a tentmaker with Priscilla and Aquila and was brought before the Roman proconsul Gallio, who dismissed the charges against him.
places.culturalSignificance: Corinth was famous (or infamous) for its wealth, diversity, and moral permissiveness. It had two harbors and controlled trade between the eastern and western Mediterranean. Planting a church in Corinth was strategically brilliant -- it was a crossroads city where the gospel could spread in every direction.
places.modernLocation: Near modern Corinth, Greece, about 80 km west of Athens.

places.name: Ephesus
places.description: A major city in the Roman province of Asia where Paul spent over two years. His ministry there was so successful that the local silversmiths -- who made their living crafting shrines to the goddess Artemis -- started a riot because their business was declining.
places.culturalSignificance: Ephesus was home to the Temple of Artemis, one of the Seven Wonders of the Ancient World. The riot in chapter 19 shows how the gospel could threaten not just religious beliefs but entire economic systems built on those beliefs.

places.name: Athens
places.description: The intellectual capital of the ancient world where Paul delivered his famous Areopagus speech, engaging Greek philosophy by starting with an altar 'To an Unknown God' and building a bridge to the gospel.
places.culturalSignificance: Athens was the birthplace of democracy, philosophy, and Western thought. Paul's speech there (chapter 17) is a masterclass in contextual communication -- he quotes Greek poets, engages Stoic and Epicurean thought, and presents the gospel without quoting a single Old Testament verse.

places.name: Philippi
places.description: A Roman colony in Macedonia where Paul planted the first church in Europe. Key events include the conversion of Lydia, the exorcism of a slave girl, and Paul and Silas singing in prison before an earthquake freed them.
places.culturalSignificance: Philippi was a Roman colony, meaning its citizens had the same rights as citizens of Rome itself. Many residents were retired Roman soldiers. Paul's assertion of Roman citizenship there carried special weight in a city proud of its Roman identity.
places.modernLocation: Archaeological site near modern Kavala, northeastern Greece.

places.name: Rome
places.description: The capital of the Roman Empire and the destination that drives the final section of Acts. Paul arrives there as a prisoner but is allowed to live under house arrest, where he spends two years freely preaching the gospel. The book ends here -- the gospel has reached the center of the known world.
places.culturalSignificance: Rome represented the pinnacle of human power and civilization. That the gospel reached Rome fulfilled Jesus' command in 1:8 to witness 'to the ends of the earth.' The book ending in Rome -- with Paul preaching boldly and unhindered -- is Luke's way of showing that no power can stop the spread of God's word.

places.name: Caesarea Maritima
places.description: The Roman administrative capital of Judea, built by Herod the Great. Peter baptized the centurion Cornelius here. Paul was imprisoned here for two years under Felix and Festus, and delivered his defense before King Agrippa here.
places.culturalSignificance: Caesarea was a thoroughly Roman city on the Jewish coast -- a symbol of Rome's presence in the Jewish homeland. It had a harbor, theater, hippodrome, and a temple dedicated to Caesar. The conversion of Cornelius here was symbolically potent: the gospel entered the Roman military establishment in Rome's own administrative center.
places.modernLocation: Archaeological site on the Mediterranean coast of Israel, between Tel Aviv and Haifa.

timeline.event: Jesus' crucifixion, resurrection, and ascension from Mount of Olives
Pentecost: Holy Spirit descends; Peter's sermon; 3,000 baptized
Stephen martyred; persecution scatters believers beyond Jerusalem
Philip preaches in Samaria and to the Ethiopian eunuch
Saul's conversion on the Damascus road
Peter's vision and the conversion of Cornelius at Caesarea
James (brother of John) executed by Herod Agrippa I; Peter imprisoned and freed
First missionary journey: Paul and Barnabas to Cyprus and southern Galatia
Jerusalem Council: Gentiles accepted without circumcision
Second missionary journey: Paul with Silas to Macedonia and Greece
Third missionary journey: Paul based in Ephesus, then travels to Greece
Paul arrested in the Jerusalem Temple
Paul imprisoned in Caesarea under Felix and Festus
Paul defends himself before King Agrippa; appeals to Caesar
Voyage to Rome; shipwreck on Malta
Paul under house arrest in Rome, preaching freely for two years
Romans destroy Jerusalem and the Temple

explainer.summary: Acts opens where Luke's Gospel ended -- with the risen Jesus spending forty days teaching his disciples about the kingdom of God. He commissions them to be his witnesses from Jerusalem to the ends of the earth, then ascends into heaven. The disciples return to Jerusalem, devote themselves to prayer, and replace Judas with Matthias to restore the number of apostles to twelve.
themes: The promise of the Holy Spirit
explainer.contextInBook: Chapter 1 is the hinge between Jesus' earthly ministry and the Spirit-empowered church. The command in 1:8 -- Jerusalem, Judea, Samaria, ends of the earth -- provides the structural outline for the entire book.

wordStudies.translatedAs: One who testifies to what they have seen and exper
wordStudies.meaning: One who testifies to what they have seen and experienced. The Greek word eventually gave us 'martyr,' because so many early witnesses to Jesus were killed for their testimony. In Acts, being a witness means both declaring what you know and being willing to pay the price for it.

wordStudies.translatedAs: An authorized representative, one sent with the fu
wordStudies.meaning: An authorized representative, one sent with the full authority of the sender. The qualifications for replacing Judas (1:21-22) show what 'apostle' meant: someone who had been with Jesus from his baptism through the resurrection, an eyewitness to the entire ministry.

verses.text: The first book I wrote, Theophilus, concerned all that Jesus began both to do and to teach,
verses.explainer: Luke addresses Theophilus directly, connecting Acts to his 'first book' -- the Gospel of Luke. He frames the Gospel as what Jesus 'began' to do and teach, implying that Acts will tell what Jesus continued to do through his Spirit and his people.

verses.text: until the day in which he was received up, after he had given commandment through the Holy Spirit to the apostles whom he had chosen.
verses.explainer: The 'commandment through the Holy Spirit' points to the commission Jesus gave his apostles before ascending. Luke emphasizes the Spirit's role from the very first verses -- the Holy Spirit is already active before Pentecost.

verses.text: To these he also showed himself alive after he suffered, by many proofs, appearing to them over a period of forty days, and speaking about God’s Kingdom.
verses.explainer: Jesus appeared to the apostles over forty days -- not a single dramatic appearance but an extended period of teaching and proof. The 'many proofs' likely included eating with them, showing his wounds, and explaining the Scriptures.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': 'Forty days', 'explanation': "Forty is a number of completion and testing in the Bible: Israel's 40 years in the wilderness, Moses' 40 days on Sinai, Jesus' 40 days of temptation. Jesus' 40 days of post-resurrection teaching is a period of preparing his followers for what comes next."}

verses.text:
Being assembled together with them, he commanded them,
“Don’t depart from Jerusalem, but wait for the promise of the Father, which you heard from me.
verses.explainer: Jesus tells them to wait in Jerusalem for 'the promise of the Father.' The disciples want to act; Jesus tells them to wait. The power they need is not something they can generate -- it must be given from above.

verses.text: For John indeed baptized in water, but you will be baptized in the Holy Spirit not many days from now.”
verses.explainer: Jesus distinguishes John's water baptism from the coming baptism 'in the Holy Spirit.' Water symbolizes repentance; the Spirit actually transforms. The upgrade from John to Jesus is the upgrade from symbol to reality.

verses.text: Therefore when they had come together, they asked him, “Lord, are you now restoring the kingdom to Israel?”
verses.explainer: The disciples ask if Jesus will now restore the kingdom to Israel. Even after forty days of teaching about God's kingdom, they still think in political terms -- a restored Jewish nation free from Rome. Their question reveals how deeply ingrained their expectations were.

thenVsNow.then: First-century Jews expected the Messiah to be a political liberator who would drive out the Romans and restore Israel's golden age under David and Solomon.
thenVsNow.now: Christians still debate the nature of God's kingdom -- whether it is primarily spiritual, political, future, or present. The disciples' confusion resonates with ongoing questions about what it means for God to reign.

verses.text:
He said to them,
“It isn’t for you to know times or seasons which the Father has set within his own authority.
verses.explainer: Jesus deflects their question about timing. 'It is not for you to know times or seasons' -- God's schedule is God's business. The disciples are not given a timetable; they are given a task.

verses.text: But you will receive power when the Holy Spirit has come upon you. You will be witnesses to me in Jerusalem, in all Judea and Samaria, and to the uttermost parts of the earth.”
verses.explainer: This is the programmatic verse for all of Acts: 'You will receive power when the Holy Spirit has come upon you, and you will be my witnesses in Jerusalem, in all Judea and Samaria, and to the uttermost parts of the earth.' The book follows this geographic expansion exactly.

verses.text: When he had said these things, as they were looking, he was taken up, and a cloud received him out of their sight.
verses.explainer: Jesus is 'taken up, and a cloud received him.' The cloud is not ordinary weather -- it is the shekinah glory cloud that represented God's presence in the Old Testament, the same cloud that filled the Tabernacle and Temple.

verses.text: While they were looking steadfastly into the sky as he went, behold, two men stood by them in white clothing,
verses.explainer: The disciples stand staring into the sky. Two men in white -- angels -- appear beside them. The scene has an almost comic element: the disciples are frozen, gaping upward, and the angels essentially say 'stop staring and get to work.'

verses.text: who also said, “You men of Galilee, why do you stand looking into the sky? This Jesus, who was received up from you into the sky, will come back in the same way as you saw him going into the sky.”
verses.explainer: The angels promise that Jesus will return 'in the same way' he went -- visibly, bodily, from heaven. This is the basis for the Christian hope of the Second Coming. But the immediate point is: do not stand here looking up. Go back to Jerusalem and wait for the Spirit.

thenVsNow.then: The ascension was a real departure -- Jesus was physically gone. The disciples had to learn to operate without his visible presence, relying instead on the Holy Spirit.
thenVsNow.now: Christians live in the same 'already but not yet' tension: Jesus has come, has ascended, and will return. The gap between ascension and return is the era of the church -- the age of mission.

verses.text: Then they returned to Jerusalem from the mountain called Olivet, which is near Jerusalem, a Sabbath day’s journey away.
verses.explainer: They return to Jerusalem from the Mount of Olives, 'a Sabbath day's journey away' -- about 2,000 cubits or roughly half a mile. Luke the historian provides precise geographic detail.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': "Sabbath day's journey", 'explanation': 'Rabbinic law set the maximum distance one could walk on the Sabbath at 2,000 cubits (about 3,000 feet or just over half a mile). Luke uses this Jewish measurement to help his readers understand the distance, even though the ascension did not occur on a Sabbath.'}

verses.text: When they had come in, they went up into the upper room, where they were staying; that is Peter, John, James, Andrew, Philip, Thomas, Bartholomew, Matthew, James the son of Alphaeus, Simon the Zealot, and Judas the son of James.
verses.explainer: The upper room roster lists the eleven remaining apostles. This is likely the same upper room where the Last Supper took place. These men have gone from Jesus' dinner table to his prayer meeting, waiting for the next chapter to begin.

verses.text: All these with one accord continued steadfastly in prayer and supplication, along with the women, and Mary the mother of Jesus, and with his brothers.
verses.explainer: The disciples devote themselves to prayer 'with one accord' -- a phrase Luke loves. The group includes women (probably including Mary Magdalene and others from Luke's Gospel), Mary the mother of Jesus, and Jesus' brothers -- who had been skeptics during his ministry but are now believers.

verses.text: In these days, Peter stood up in the middle of the disciples (and the number of names was about one hundred twenty), and said,
verses.explainer: Peter takes the lead, standing among about 120 believers. This is the total size of the early church before Pentecost -- small enough to fit in one room, large enough to constitute a community.

verses.text: “Brothers, it was necessary that this Scripture should be fulfilled, which the Holy Spirit spoke before by the mouth of David concerning Judas, who was guide to those who took Jesus.
verses.explainer: Peter interprets Judas's betrayal through Psalm 69 and Psalm 109, attributing the prophecy to the Holy Spirit speaking through David. The early church consistently read the Old Testament as pointing toward Jesus and the events surrounding him.

verses.text: For he was counted with us, and received his portion in this ministry.
verses.explainer: Judas 'was counted with us and received his portion in this ministry.' Peter acknowledges that Judas was a genuine member of the Twelve, not an outsider who infiltrated. Betrayal came from within, which made it worse.

verses.text: Now this man obtained a field with the reward for his wickedness, and falling headlong, his body burst open, and all his intestines gushed out.
verses.explainer: Luke provides a graphic account of Judas's death: he bought a field with his betrayal money, fell headlong, and his body burst open. This differs in detail from Matthew 27:5 (which says Judas hanged himself). Ancient readers were less troubled by such variations than modern ones -- the point is that Judas met a horrible end.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': 'Field of blood', 'explanation': 'The purchase of a field with blood money (the price of betraying an innocent man) made the land permanently contaminated in Jewish thinking. The name Akeldama stuck as a grim memorial.'}

verses.text: It became known to everyone who lived in Jerusalem that in their language that field was called ‘Akeldama,’ that is, ‘The field of blood.’
verses.explainer: The field became known as Akeldama -- 'Field of Blood' in Aramaic. Luke translates for his Greek-speaking audience, just as Mark translated Aramaic terms for his Roman readers.

verses.text:
For it is written in the book of Psalms,
‘Let his habitation be made desolate.
Let no one dwell therein;’
and,
‘Let another take his office.’
verses.explainer: Peter quotes two psalms to justify replacing Judas. The first (Psalm 69:25) explains why Judas's position is now vacant. The second (Psalm 109:8) commands that someone else take his office. Peter reads the Old Testament as a script that current events are fulfilling.

verses.text: “Of the men therefore who have accompanied us all the time that the Lord Jesus went in and out among us,
verses.explainer: The replacement must be someone who accompanied Jesus throughout his entire ministry -- from John's baptism to the ascension. This strict qualification shows that 'apostle' was not a general term for any Christian leader but a specific role requiring eyewitness experience.

verses.text: beginning from the baptism of John, to the day that he was received up from us, of these one must become a witness with us of his resurrection.”
verses.explainer: The core qualification for an apostle is being a witness to the resurrection. Everything else -- the teaching, the miracles, the ministry -- matters because it leads to and is validated by the resurrection. Without it, there is no gospel to preach.

verses.text: They put forward two, Joseph called Barsabbas, who was also called Justus, and Matthias.
verses.explainer: Two candidates are proposed: Joseph Barsabbas (also called Justus) and Matthias. We know nothing else about either man from the New Testament. Joseph's triple name suggests he moved in both Jewish and Roman circles.

verses.text: They prayed, and said, “You, Lord, who know the hearts of all men, show which one of these two you have chosen
verses.explainer: The prayer is simple and direct: 'Lord, you know the hearts of all -- show which one you have chosen.' They are not selecting a candidate by vote or preference but asking God to reveal his choice.

verses.text: to take part in this ministry and apostleship from which Judas fell away, that he might go to his own place.”
verses.explainer: Judas 'fell away' from the apostleship 'to go to his own place.' The euphemism is grimly suggestive. Judas chose his path, and the path led where it led.

verses.text: They drew lots for them, and the lot fell on Matthias, and he was counted with the eleven apostles.
verses.explainer: They cast lots, and Matthias is chosen. Casting lots was an accepted method of discerning God's will in the Old Testament (Proverbs 16:33). This is the last time lots are used in the New Testament -- after Pentecost, the Spirit provides direct guidance.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': 'Casting lots', 'explanation': "Lots were small stones or pieces of wood used like dice to determine God's will. Proverbs 16:33 says 'the lot is cast into the lap, but its every decision is from the Lord.' The practice had a long biblical history (Joshua, 1 Samuel) but disappears after the Spirit comes at Pentecost."}

thenVsNow.then: Before the Spirit came, believers used lots -- a form of sacred randomness -- to discern God's will. It was a legitimate practice rooted in the belief that God controlled the outcome.
thenVsNow.now: After Pentecost, the church shifted to prayer, Scripture, community discernment, and the Spirit's leading as the means of knowing God's will. Few Christian traditions still use lots for decision-making.

explainer.summary: Pentecost is the birthday of the church. The Holy Spirit descends with wind and fire, enabling the disciples to speak in languages they have never learned. Peter preaches the first Christian sermon, explaining that Jesus' death and resurrection fulfilled Old Testament prophecy. Three thousand people respond and are baptized. The chapter closes with a portrait of the early community: devoted to teaching, fellowship, breaking of bread, and prayer, sharing everything in common.
explainer.contextInBook: Pentecost reverses Babel (Genesis 11) -- where God confused languages to scatter humanity, now the Spirit enables communication across languages to gather humanity. This is the fulfillment of Jesus' promise in 1:8 and the launch of the 'Jerusalem' phase of the mission.

wordStudies.translatedAs: The Jewish festival of Shavuot, celebrated fifty d
wordStudies.meaning: The Jewish festival of Shavuot, celebrated fifty days after Passover. It commemorated the giving of the Torah at Sinai and the firstfruits of the wheat harvest. The Spirit descending on this day was deeply symbolic: just as God gave the Law at Sinai, now God gives the Spirit to write the law on human hearts.

wordStudies.translatedAs: To change one's mind, to turn around, to fundament
wordStudies.meaning: To change one's mind, to turn around, to fundamentally reorient one's thinking and behavior. It is more than feeling sorry -- it is a complete reversal of direction. Peter's call to repentance demands a total reappraisal of who Jesus is.

verses.text: Now when the day of Pentecost had come, they were all with one accord in one place.
verses.explainer: The day of Pentecost arrives, and they are 'all together in one place.' The Jewish festival that celebrated God giving the Torah at Sinai will now become the day God gives the Spirit to his new community.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': 'Day of Pentecost', 'explanation': "Shavuot (Weeks/Pentecost) was one of three pilgrimage festivals when Jews from across the known world traveled to Jerusalem. This guaranteed a massive, international audience for the Spirit's dramatic arrival -- God's timing was strategic."}

verses.text: Suddenly there came from the sky a sound like the rushing of a mighty wind, and it filled all the house where they were sitting.
verses.explainer: A sound 'like the rushing of a mighty wind' fills the house. The Greek word for spirit (pneuma) and wind (pneuma) are the same. The Spirit arrives not as a gentle breeze but as a violent force -- this is not comfortable religion.

verses.text: Tongues like fire appeared and were distributed to them, and one sat on each of them.
verses.explainer: Tongues 'like fire' appear and rest on each person. The fire is not destructive but empowering. In the Old Testament, fire marked God's presence -- the burning bush, the pillar of fire, the fire on Sinai. Now God's presence rests on individual people, not on sacred locations.

verses.text: They were all filled with the Holy Spirit, and began to speak with other languages, as the Spirit gave them the ability to speak.
verses.explainer: They speak 'in other languages' -- not ecstatic gibberish but actual foreign languages, as the following verses make clear. The Spirit enables cross-cultural communication. The first act of the Spirit-filled church is reaching across language barriers.

thenVsNow.then: The Pentecost miracle was the Spirit enabling ordinary Galileans to speak languages they had never learned, so that an international audience could hear the gospel in their own tongues.
thenVsNow.now: The debate about 'speaking in tongues' continues in churches today. Some traditions emphasize the Pentecost pattern (known languages for communication), while others focus on Paul's descriptions of ecstatic speech (1 Corinthians 12-14). The core point is the same: the Spirit empowers communication of God's message.

verses.text: Now there were dwelling in Jerusalem Jews, devout men, from every nation under the sky.
verses.explainer: Jerusalem is full of 'devout Jews from every nation under the sky' who have come for the festival. God chose the moment of maximum international presence to launch the church. The gospel will go global from day one.

verses.text: When this sound was heard, the multitude came together, and were bewildered, because everyone heard them speaking in his own language.
verses.explainer: The crowd is 'bewildered' because each person hears the disciples speaking in their own language. The miracle is one of hearing as much as speaking -- the Spirit bridges the gap from both sides.

verses.text: They were all amazed and marveled, saying to one another, “Behold, aren’t all these who speak Galileans?
verses.explainer: The crowd's amazement intensifies: 'Aren't all these who speak Galileans?' Galileans were known for their thick accents and limited education. They were the last people you would expect to be multilingual.

verses.text: How do we hear, everyone in our own native language?
verses.explainer: Each person hears in their own 'native language' -- the language of home, of childhood, of the heart. The Spirit does not require people to learn a sacred language; the gospel comes to people where they are.

verses.text: Parthians, Medes, Elamites, and people from Mesopotamia, Judea, Cappadocia, Pontus, Asia,
verses.explainer: Luke provides a detailed list of nations represented, starting with the east: Parthians, Medes, Elamites, and people from Mesopotamia. These represent the Jewish diaspora scattered across the ancient Near East since the Babylonian exile.

verses.text: Phrygia, Pamphylia, Egypt, the parts of Libya around Cyrene, visitors from Rome, both Jews and proselytes,
verses.explainer: The list continues westward through Asia Minor (Cappadocia, Pontus, Asia, Phrygia, Pamphylia) and into North Africa (Egypt, Libya near Cyrene) and Rome. The geographic sweep mirrors the spread of the gospel that Acts will narrate.

verses.text: Cretans and Arabians: we hear them speaking in our languages the mighty works of God!”
verses.explainer: The list concludes with Cretans and Arabians. Together, this catalog represents virtually the entire known world. The Spirit speaks to all nations -- the gospel is not a local product but a universal message.

verses.text: They were all amazed, and were perplexed, saying to one another, “What does this mean?”
verses.explainer: Some are amazed and perplexed, asking 'What does this mean?' This is the right question. The Spirit's arrival demands interpretation -- raw experience needs theological explanation, which Peter will provide.

verses.text: Others, mocking, said, “They are filled with new wine.”
verses.explainer: Others mock: 'They are filled with new wine.' The dismissive explanation -- they are just drunk -- shows that miraculous events do not compel belief. People can always find a naturalistic explanation if they want one.

verses.text: But Peter, standing up with the eleven, lifted up his voice, and spoke out to them, “You men of Judea, and all you who dwell at Jerusalem, let this be known to you, and listen to my words.
verses.explainer: Peter stands up 'with the eleven' -- a united front -- and addresses the crowd. The coward who denied Jesus three times is now the fearless preacher. The Spirit has transformed him. This is the first Christian sermon.

annotations.word: Peter, standing up with the eleven

verses.text: For these aren’t drunken, as you suppose, seeing it is only the third hour of the day.
verses.explainer: Peter's opening argument is practical: 'These men are not drunk -- it is only nine in the morning.' Ancient wine was typically mixed with water and consumed at meals later in the day. Drunkenness at 9 AM was implausible.

verses.text: But this is what has been spoken through the prophet Joel:
verses.explainer: Peter reaches for the prophet Joel to explain what is happening. His interpretive move is crucial: this is not random chaos but the fulfillment of ancient prophecy. What looks like disorder is actually God keeping a promise.

verses.text:
‘It will be in the last days, says God,
that I will pour out my Spirit on all flesh.
Your sons and your daughters will prophesy.
Your young men will see visions.
Your old men will dream dreams.
verses.explainer: The Joel quotation begins: 'In the last days, God says, I will pour out my Spirit on all flesh.' Peter modifies Joel's original 'afterward' to 'in the last days,' making the theological claim that Pentecost inaugurates the final era of God's plan.

thenVsNow.then: In the Old Testament, the Spirit came selectively -- on specific prophets, kings, and leaders for specific tasks. Joel's promise of the Spirit on 'all flesh' was a radical democratization of spiritual power.
thenVsNow.now: The belief that every believer has access to God's Spirit -- not just clergy or spiritual elites -- remains a foundational principle of Christianity. It empowers ordinary people to participate in God's work.

verses.text:
Yes, and on my servants and on my handmaidens in those days,
I will pour out my Spirit, and they will prophesy.
verses.explainer: Even servants -- the lowest social class -- will receive the Spirit and prophesy. The Spirit flattens social hierarchies. In God's economy, your social status does not determine your spiritual capacity.

Joel's prophecy includes cosmic signs: wonders in the sky, signs on the earth, blood, fire, and smoke. Peter includes these dramatic images as part of the package, even though Pentecost itself featured wind and fire, not blood and smoke. The 'last days' are a long period with multiple phases.

verses.text:
The sun will be turned into darkness,
and the moon into blood,
before the great and glorious day of the Lord comes.
verses.explainer: The sun turning to darkness and the moon to blood describe cosmic upheaval before 'the great and glorious day of the Lord.' Peter frames history as moving toward a climax -- and Pentecost is a major step in that direction.

verses.text: It will be that whoever will call on the name of the Lord will be saved.’
verses.explainer: The quotation climaxes with a promise of universal access: 'Everyone who calls on the name of the Lord will be saved.' This is the gospel in one sentence -- salvation is available to anyone who asks.

verses.text: “Men of Israel, hear these words! Jesus of Nazareth, a man approved by God to you by mighty works and wonders and signs which God did by him among you, even as you yourselves know,
verses.explainer: Peter pivots to Jesus of Nazareth, attested by 'mighty works and wonders and signs which God did through him among you.' He appeals to the crowd's own knowledge -- they saw these things. The evidence is public, not private.

verses.text: him, being delivered up by the determined counsel and foreknowledge of God, you have taken by the hand of lawless men, crucified and killed;
verses.explainer: The crucifixion was both God's predetermined plan and the crowd's moral responsibility. Peter holds both truths simultaneously without resolving the tension. Divine sovereignty and human accountability coexist.

thenVsNow.then: Peter tells the Jerusalem crowd that they handed Jesus over to 'lawless men' (Romans) to be crucified. This was not an accusation of a distant historical event but a confrontation with people who may have been in the crowd shouting 'Crucify him' weeks earlier.
thenVsNow.now: The theological point transcends the historical situation: human rebellion and divine purpose can operate simultaneously. The worst act in human history -- the murder of the Son of God -- was also the means of salvation.

verses.text: whom God raised up, having freed him from the agony of death, because it was not possible that he should be held by it.
verses.explainer: God raised Jesus from the dead because 'it was not possible for death to hold him.' Death had a grip on Jesus for three days, but it was an impossible grip -- like trying to hold the ocean in a jar. The resurrection was inevitable.

verses.text:
For David says concerning him,
‘I saw the Lord always before my face,
For he is on my right hand, that I should not be moved.
verses.explainer: Peter quotes Psalm 16, attributed to David, in which the speaker expresses confidence that God will not abandon him to death or let his 'Holy One see decay.'

verses.text:
Therefore my heart was glad, and my tongue rejoiced.
Moreover my flesh also will dwell in hope;
verses.explainer: The psalm continues: 'My heart was glad and my tongue rejoiced; moreover my flesh will live in hope.' Peter is building a scriptural case that the resurrection was prophesied centuries before it happened.

verses.text:
because you will not leave my soul in Hades,
neither will you allow your Holy One to see decay.
verses.explainer: The critical line: 'You will not abandon my soul to Hades or let your Holy One see decay.' Peter will argue that David could not have been talking about himself -- David's body did decay. So who was David talking about?

verses.text:
You made known to me the ways of life.
You will make me full of gladness with your presence.’
verses.explainer: The psalm concludes with joy in God's presence. Peter reads the entire psalm as a prophecy of Jesus' resurrection and subsequent exaltation at God's right hand.

verses.text: “Brothers, I may tell you freely of the patriarch David, that he both died and was buried, and his tomb is with us to this day.
verses.explainer: Peter's argument is simple: David died, was buried, and his tomb is 'with us to this day.' Everyone can visit it. David's body decayed. Therefore Psalm 16 cannot be about David himself.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': "David's tomb", 'explanation': "David's tomb in Jerusalem was a known and venerated site in the first century. Peter's audience could verify his claim -- David was dead and buried. The psalm must point to someone else."}

verses.text: Therefore, being a prophet, and knowing that God had sworn with an oath to him that of the fruit of his body, according to the flesh, he would raise up the Christ to sit on his throne,
verses.explainer: David was a prophet who knew God had promised that one of his descendants would sit on his throne. He spoke not about himself but about the future Messiah.

verses.text: he foreseeing this spoke about the resurrection of the Christ, that his soul wasn’t left in Hades, and his flesh didn’t see decay.
verses.explainer: David 'foresaw' the resurrection of the Christ -- the Messiah would not be abandoned to Hades and his body would not decay. Peter reads David as a prophet who saw beyond his own death to the Messiah's triumph over death.

verses.text: This Jesus God raised up, to which we all are witnesses.
verses.explainer: The pivot: 'This Jesus God raised up, and of that we all are witnesses.' The apostles are not offering theological speculation but eyewitness testimony. They saw the risen Jesus. Peter's argument rests on evidence, not philosophy.

verses.text: Being therefore exalted by the right hand of God, and having received from the Father the promise of the Holy Spirit, he has poured out this, which you now see and hear.
verses.explainer: Jesus has been exalted to God's right hand and has received the promised Holy Spirit from the Father, which he has now 'poured out' -- what the crowd is seeing and hearing. The Spirit at Pentecost is proof of Jesus' exaltation in heaven.

verses.text:
For David didn’t ascend into the heavens, but he says himself,
‘The Lord said to my Lord, “Sit by my right hand,
verses.explainer: Peter makes another argument from David: David did not ascend to heaven (Psalm 110:1), yet David wrote 'The Lord said to my Lord, sit at my right hand.' If David's Lord is seated at God's right hand, and David is not in heaven, then David was speaking about someone greater -- Jesus.

verses.text: until I make your enemies a footstool for your feet.”’
verses.explainer: The enemies will be made a 'footstool.' This is language of complete victory. The image comes from ancient near eastern practice where conquering kings literally placed their feet on the necks of defeated enemies.

verses.text: “Let all the house of Israel therefore know certainly that God has made him both Lord and Christ, this Jesus whom you crucified.”
verses.explainer: Peter's climactic declaration: 'God has made this Jesus, whom you crucified, both Lord and Christ.' The word 'Lord' (kyrios) is the Greek word used for God in the Old Testament. Peter is saying that the man you killed is both the Messiah and shares in God's own identity.

verses.text: Now when they heard this, they were cut to the heart, and said to Peter and the rest of the apostles, “Brothers, what shall we do?”
verses.explainer: The crowd is 'cut to the heart' and asks 'What shall we do?' Peter's sermon has achieved its purpose -- it has moved people from curiosity through understanding to conviction. They realize they participated in killing God's chosen one.

verses.text: Peter said to them, “Repent, and be baptized, every one of you, in the name of Jesus Christ for the forgiveness of sins, and you will receive the gift of the Holy Spirit.
verses.explainer: Peter's response: 'Repent, and be baptized, every one of you, in the name of Jesus Christ for the forgiveness of your sins, and you will receive the gift of the Holy Spirit.' Three commands (repent, be baptized, receive the Spirit) matched to three realities (forgiveness, belonging, empowerment).

thenVsNow.then: Peter's call to be baptized 'in the name of Jesus Christ' was a radical departure. Jewish baptism and John's baptism were about repentance; Christian baptism was about identification with a specific person -- Jesus.
thenVsNow.now: Baptism remains the universal initiation rite of Christianity, practiced by virtually every tradition (though the mode -- immersion, pouring, sprinkling -- and the age of the candidate vary widely). Peter's basic formula -- repentance, baptism, the Spirit -- still defines Christian conversion.

verses.text: For the promise is to you, and to your children, and to all who are far off, even as many as the Lord our God will call to himself.”
verses.explainer: The promise is 'for you and for your children and for all who are far off.' 'Far off' likely includes Gentiles -- those geographically and ethnically distant. Peter may not fully grasp the implications yet, but his words anticipate the Gentile mission.

verses.text: With many other words he testified, and exhorted them, saying, “Save yourselves from this crooked generation!”
verses.explainer: Peter continues to testify with 'many other words.' Luke summarizes a long sermon in a few verses, giving us the theological highlights rather than a transcript.

verses.text: Then those who gladly received his word were baptized. There were added that day about three thousand souls.
verses.explainer: About three thousand people are baptized that day. The church goes from 120 to over 3,000 in a single afternoon. The harvest at Pentecost (a harvest festival) is spectacular.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': 'Three thousand baptized', 'explanation': 'Mass baptism of 3,000 people in one day was logistically possible in Jerusalem because of the many ritual baths (mikvaot) near the Temple. Archaeologists have uncovered dozens of these pools. The infrastructure for ritual immersion already existed.'}

verses.text: They continued steadfastly in the apostles’ teaching and fellowship, in the breaking of bread, and prayer.
verses.explainer: The new community devotes itself to four things: the apostles' teaching, fellowship, breaking of bread, and prayer. This fourfold pattern became the template for Christian worship and community life.

thenVsNow.then: The four practices -- teaching, fellowship, breaking bread, prayer -- were not a formal liturgy but a rhythm of daily life. The early believers met in homes and at the Temple, learning, eating, and praying together.
thenVsNow.now: These four elements remain the core of Christian community across traditions: instruction in Scripture, mutual care and accountability, shared meals (including the Eucharist/Communion), and corporate prayer. Most church services are organized around some version of this pattern.

verses.text: Fear came on every soul, and many wonders and signs were done through the apostles.
verses.explainer: Awe falls on everyone as the apostles perform 'many wonders and signs.' The miracles validate the apostles' message and echo the 'signs and wonders' that God performed through Moses at the Exodus.

verses.text: All who believed were together, and had all things in common.
verses.explainer: All the believers are 'together' and have 'all things in common.' This is not state-enforced communism but voluntary generosity born of a shared spiritual experience. They had been transformed by the same Spirit and responded by sharing resources.

thenVsNow.then: The early church's sharing of possessions was voluntary and radical. In a society with no social safety net, the community itself became the safety net. Those with resources supported those without.
thenVsNow.now: The early church's economic sharing has inspired movements from monastic communities to modern intentional communities. Most churches practice some version of it through giving, benevolence funds, and mutual aid -- though the radical 'all things in common' model is rare.

verses.text: They sold their possessions and goods, and distributed them to all, according as anyone had need.
verses.explainer: They sell possessions and distribute to anyone in need. The selling is ongoing (the Greek tense implies repeated action, not a one-time liquidation). As needs arise, those with resources respond.

verses.text: Day by day, continuing steadfastly with one accord in the temple, and breaking bread at home, they took their food with gladness and singleness of heart,
verses.explainer: They meet daily in the Temple courts and break bread in homes. The early believers did not abandon Judaism -- they continued worshiping at the Temple while also gathering in homes for distinctly Christian fellowship.

verses.text: praising God, and having favor with all the people. The Lord added to the assembly day by day those who were being saved.
verses.explainer: The community enjoys 'favor with all the people' and 'the Lord added to their number daily those who were being saved.' Growth is organic, fueled by the attractiveness of the community's life together. People are drawn in by what they see, not just by what they hear.

explainer.summary: Peter and John heal a man who has been lame from birth at the Beautiful Gate of the Temple. The miracle draws a crowd, and Peter preaches his second sermon, explaining that the healing was done in the name of Jesus -- the one they crucified but God raised from the dead. He calls them to repentance, promising that God will send 'times of refreshing' and that Jesus is the prophet Moses predicted.
themes: Jesus as the fulfillment of Moses' prophecy
explainer.contextInBook: This healing is the first apostolic miracle in Acts and it follows the pattern of Jesus' own ministry: a dramatic healing leads to a sermon that interprets the healing as a sign pointing to Jesus. The healing also sets up the first confrontation with the Jewish authorities in chapter 4.

wordStudies.translatedAs: In the ancient world, a name was not just a label
wordStudies.meaning: In the ancient world, a name was not just a label but represented the person's character, authority, and power. Healing 'in the name of Jesus' means healing by his authority and power -- invoking not a magical formula but a personal relationship with the risen Christ.

wordStudies.translatedAs: A cooling, a recovery of breath, relief
wordStudies.meaning: A cooling, a recovery of breath, relief. Used only here in the New Testament. Peter promises 'times of refreshing from the presence of the Lord' -- a future restoration that begins now but will be completed when Jesus returns.

verses.text: Peter and John were going up into the temple at the hour of prayer, the ninth hour.
verses.explainer: Peter and John go to the Temple at 'the hour of prayer, the ninth hour' -- 3 PM, the time of the afternoon sacrifice. The apostles maintain Jewish worship patterns. Christianity has not yet separated from Judaism.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': 'Ninth hour of prayer', 'explanation': 'Jews observed three daily prayer times: morning (the third hour, 9 AM), afternoon (the sixth hour, noon), and evening (the ninth hour, 3 PM). The ninth hour coincided with the evening sacrifice in the Temple. Devout Jews oriented their entire day around these prayer rhythms.'}

verses.text: A certain man who was lame from his mother’s womb was being carried, whom they laid daily at the door of the temple which is called Beautiful, to ask gifts for the needy of those who entered into the temple.
verses.explainer: A man 'lame from his mother's womb' is carried daily to the Temple gate called Beautiful. He has never walked -- this is not an injury but a birth condition. He is placed at the gate to beg from those entering to worship.

verses.text: Seeing Peter and John about to go into the temple, he asked to receive gifts for the needy.
verses.explainer: The man asks Peter and John for money as they enter. This is his routine -- he does not know he is about to have the most important encounter of his life.

verses.text: Peter, fastening his eyes on him, with John, said, “Look at us.”
verses.explainer: Peter 'fastened his eyes on him' and says 'Look at us!' Peter demands the man's full attention before acting. This is not a casual encounter; it is a deliberate, personal confrontation.

verses.text: He listened to them, expecting to receive something from them.
verses.explainer: The man looks at them, 'expecting to receive something.' He expects money. He is about to receive something infinitely better.

verses.text: But Peter said, “I have no silver or gold, but what I have, that I give you. In the name of Jesus Christ of Nazareth, get up and walk!”
verses.explainer: Peter's famous words: 'Silver and gold I do not have, but what I do have, I give you. In the name of Jesus Christ of Nazareth, get up and walk!' Peter is poor in money but rich in spiritual power. The contrast is intentional.

thenVsNow.then: The early church had no financial resources, no buildings, no institutional power. What they had was the Holy Spirit and the authority of Jesus' name. Their poverty made the source of their power unmistakable.
thenVsNow.now: The church today often has significant financial and institutional resources. Peter's words challenge modern churches to ask whether they still possess the spiritual power that made the early church transformative, or whether they have traded spiritual authority for organizational comfort.

verses.text: He took him by the right hand, and raised him up. Immediately his feet and his ankle bones received strength.
verses.explainer: Peter takes him by the right hand and lifts him up. The healing is immediate -- his feet and ankles receive strength. The physical detail suggests an eyewitness account, possibly Peter's own recollection transmitted through Luke.

verses.text: Leaping up, he stood, and began to walk. He entered with them into the temple, walking, leaping, and praising God.
verses.explainer: The healed man leaps up, stands, walks, and enters the Temple 'walking and leaping and praising God.' For the first time in his life, he can enter the Temple on his own feet. His response is pure, uncontainable joy.

verses.text: All the people saw him walking and praising God.
verses.explainer: The people recognize him as the beggar who sat at the Beautiful Gate. This is not an anonymous healing -- everyone knows this man and knows his condition. The miracle is publicly verifiable.

verses.text: They recognized him, that it was he who used to sit begging for gifts for the needy at the Beautiful Gate of the temple. They were filled with wonder and amazement at what had happened to him.
verses.explainer: They are filled with wonder and amazement. The Greek words express stunned astonishment -- they are knocked off balance by what they are seeing.

verses.text: As the lame man who was healed held on to Peter and John, all the people ran together to them in the porch that is called Solomon’s, greatly wondering.
verses.explainer: The healed man clings to Peter and John as the crowd runs to them in Solomon's Portico -- a covered colonnade on the east side of the Temple. The crowd assumes Peter and John are the source of the power.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': "Solomon's Portico", 'explanation': "A large covered colonnade on the eastern side of the Temple courts, named for its traditional association with Solomon's original Temple. It was a common gathering place for teaching and discussion -- Jesus himself had taught here (John 10:23)."}

verses.text: When Peter saw it, he responded to the people, “You men of Israel, why do you marvel at this man? Why do you fasten your eyes on us, as though by our own power or godliness we had made him walk?
verses.explainer: Peter immediately redirects: 'Why do you stare at us, as though by our own power or godliness we made this man walk?' He refuses to take credit. The power is not theirs.

verses.text: The God of Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob, the God of our fathers, has glorified his Servant Jesus, whom you delivered up, and denied in the presence of Pilate, when he had determined to release him.
verses.explainer: Peter attributes the healing to 'the God of Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob' -- connecting the miracle to Israel's covenant God. This God has glorified his servant Jesus, whom they delivered up and denied before Pilate.

verses.text: But you denied the Holy and Righteous One, and asked for a murderer to be granted to you,
verses.explainer: Peter's accusation sharpens: 'You denied the Holy and Righteous One and asked for a murderer to be granted to you.' The contrast is devastating -- they chose Barabbas over Jesus.

verses.text: and killed the Prince of life, whom God raised from the dead, to which we are witnesses.
verses.explainer: They killed 'the Author of life, whom God raised from the dead.' The phrase 'Author of life' is paradoxical -- they killed the one who is the source of all life. Peter and the apostles are witnesses to the resurrection.

verses.text: By faith in his name, his name has made this man strong, whom you see and know. Yes, the faith which is through him has given him this perfect soundness in the presence of you all.
verses.explainer: Peter explains the healing: it is by faith in Jesus' name that this man stands before them whole. The name is not a magic spell -- it represents the living authority of the risen Jesus working through those who trust him.

verses.text: “Now, brothers, I know that you did this in ignorance, as did also your rulers.
verses.explainer: Peter softens his tone: 'I know that you acted in ignorance, as did also your rulers.' They did not know they were killing the Messiah. Ignorance does not excuse the act, but it opens the door to forgiveness.

verses.text: But the things which God announced by the mouth of all his prophets, that Christ should suffer, he thus fulfilled.
verses.explainer: God announced through all the prophets that his Christ would suffer. What they did in ignorance, God planned in advance. Human sin and divine sovereignty intersect at the cross.

verses.text: “Repent therefore, and turn again, that your sins may be blotted out, so that there may come times of refreshing from the presence of the Lord,
verses.explainer: Peter calls for repentance so that sins may be 'blotted out' and 'times of refreshing may come from the presence of the Lord.' The imagery is vivid: sins erased like wiping a slate clean, followed by a wave of divine renewal.

verses.text: and that he may send Christ Jesus, who was ordained for you before,
verses.explainer: God will send 'Jesus, who was appointed as the Christ for you.' Peter anticipates Jesus' return -- the second coming -- as the full realization of God's restoration plan.

verses.text: whom heaven must receive until the times of restoration of all things, which God spoke long ago by the mouth of his holy prophets.
verses.explainer: Heaven must receive Jesus 'until the times of restoration of all things, which God spoke by the mouth of his holy prophets.' The restoration is cosmic in scope -- not just individual salvation but the renewal of all creation.

verses.text: For Moses indeed said to the fathers, ‘The Lord God will raise up a prophet for you from among your brothers, like me. You shall listen to him in all things whatever he says to you.
verses.explainer: Peter quotes Moses: 'The Lord God will raise up a prophet for you from among your brothers, like me.' Jesus is the prophet Moses predicted -- the ultimate mediator between God and humanity.

verses.text: It will be that every soul that will not listen to that prophet will be utterly destroyed from among the people.’
verses.explainer: Anyone who does not listen to this prophet will be 'utterly destroyed from among the people.' The stakes are ultimate. Rejecting Jesus is not a minor religious disagreement -- it is rejecting God's chosen spokesman.

verses.text: Yes, and all the prophets from Samuel and those who followed after, as many as have spoken, they also told of these days.
verses.explainer: All the prophets from Samuel onward spoke about 'these days.' Peter presents the entire prophetic tradition as pointing to the present moment -- what is happening now is what the prophets saw from afar.

verses.text: You are the children of the prophets, and of the covenant which God made with our fathers, saying to Abraham, ‘In your offspring will all the families of the earth be blessed.’
verses.explainer: Peter reminds them: 'You are the sons of the prophets and of the covenant that God made with your fathers.' They have first claim on these promises. The gospel goes to the Jews first, not because others are excluded but because God keeps his covenant commitments.

verses.text: God, having raised up his servant Jesus, sent him to you first to bless you, in turning away every one of you from your wickedness.”
verses.explainer: God sent Jesus 'first to you' -- to Israel, to bless them 'in turning every one of you from your wickedness.' The blessing is not wealth or power but transformation -- being freed from the patterns of sin that destroy life.

explainer.summary: The healing at the Temple brings the apostles into direct conflict with the religious authorities. Peter and John are arrested, brought before the Sanhedrin, and ordered to stop speaking about Jesus. They refuse, declaring 'we cannot help speaking about what we have seen and heard.' Released with threats, they return to the community, which responds with bold prayer asking not for safety but for more courage to speak. The chapter closes with a renewed description of the community's radical generosity.
themes: The name of Jesus as the only salvation
explainer.contextInBook: Chapter 4 introduces the pattern that will recur throughout Acts: preaching leads to opposition, opposition leads to trial, trial becomes an opportunity for witness, and persecution strengthens rather than silences the church.

wordStudies.translatedAs: Fearless, frank, open speech
wordStudies.meaning: Fearless, frank, open speech. It was a political term in Greek democracy -- the right of a free citizen to speak openly in the public assembly. The apostles claim this right before the highest Jewish court, asserting that their message cannot be silenced by any human authority.

wordStudies.translatedAs: Literally 'head of the corner
wordStudies.meaning: Literally 'head of the corner.' The cornerstone was the most important stone in a building -- it determined the alignment of the entire structure. Peter says Jesus is the cornerstone that the builders (Israel's leaders) rejected.

verses.text: As they spoke to the people, the priests and the captain of the temple and the Sadducees came to them,
verses.explainer: The priests, the captain of the Temple guard, and the Sadducees confront Peter and John. The Sadducees are particularly disturbed because the apostles are teaching about resurrection -- a doctrine the Sadducees reject.

verses.text: being upset because they taught the people and proclaimed in Jesus the resurrection from the dead.
verses.explainer: The Sadducees are 'greatly annoyed' because the apostles are proclaiming 'in Jesus the resurrection from the dead.' The healing is secondary to them -- what they cannot tolerate is the theology. The resurrection threatens their worldview and their authority.

verses.text: They laid hands on them, and put them in custody until the next day, for it was now evening.
verses.explainer: Peter and John are arrested and put in custody overnight. It is evening, so a trial cannot happen until morning. The apostles spend the night in jail -- their first imprisonment.

verses.text: But many of those who heard the word believed, and the number of the men came to be about five thousand.
verses.explainer: Despite the arrest, 'many of those who heard the word believed, and the number of the men came to be about five thousand.' The church keeps growing even as opposition intensifies. Persecution cannot contain the gospel -- it only spreads it.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': 'Five thousand men', 'explanation': "Luke specifies 'men' (andres), which in ancient counting typically excluded women and children. The total community may have been 15,000 or more. In a city of perhaps 40,000-80,000 permanent residents, this was an enormous and disruptive social movement."}

verses.text: In the morning, their rulers, elders, and scribes were gathered together in Jerusalem.
verses.explainer: The next day, the rulers, elders, and scribes gather in Jerusalem -- this is essentially the Sanhedrin, the highest Jewish court, the same body that condemned Jesus weeks earlier.

verses.text: Annas the high priest was there, with Caiaphas, John, Alexander, and as many as were relatives of the high priest.
verses.explainer: Annas the high priest, Caiaphas, John, and Alexander are named. Annas was the power behind the high priesthood (he had been deposed by Rome but still wielded enormous influence). Caiaphas was his son-in-law and the sitting high priest -- the same one who presided over Jesus' trial.

verses.text: When they had stood Peter and John in the middle of them, they inquired, “By what power, or in what name, have you done this?”
verses.explainer: They ask: 'By what power or by what name did you do this?' The question inadvertently gives Peter the opening he needs. They have asked about the name -- and Peter is about to tell them.

verses.text: Then Peter, filled with the Holy Spirit, said to them, “You rulers of the people, and elders of Israel,
verses.explainer: Peter is 'filled with the Holy Spirit' and addresses them. The Spirit is specifically credited with Peter's boldness before hostile authorities -- this fulfills Jesus' promise that the Spirit would give them words when they stood before rulers.

verses.text: if we are examined today concerning a good deed done to a crippled man, by what means this man has been healed,
verses.explainer: Peter reframes the situation with devastating irony: 'If we are being examined today for a good deed done to a crippled man -- by what means this man has been healed...' He highlights the absurdity: they are on trial for healing someone.

verses.text: be it known to you all, and to all the people of Israel, that in the name of Jesus Christ of Nazareth, whom you crucified, whom God raised from the dead, in him does this man stand here before you whole.
verses.explainer: Peter declares plainly: 'It is by the name of Jesus Christ of Nazareth, whom you crucified, whom God raised from the dead -- by him this man stands before you healthy.' The indictment is direct: you killed Jesus; God reversed your verdict.

verses.text: He is ‘the stone which was regarded as worthless by you, the builders, which has become the head of the corner.’
verses.explainer: Peter quotes Psalm 118:22: Jesus is 'the stone that was rejected by you, the builders, which has become the cornerstone.' The religious leaders (the 'builders' of Israel) rejected the most important piece of God's plan.

verses.text: There is salvation in none other, for neither is there any other name under heaven, that is given among men, by which we must be saved!”
verses.explainer: Peter makes the most exclusive claim in the New Testament: 'There is salvation in no one else, for there is no other name under heaven given among men by which we must be saved.' The healing of the lame man becomes a parable -- physical healing points to spiritual salvation, and both come only through Jesus.

thenVsNow.then: In a world of many gods and many religious options, Peter's claim that salvation comes only through Jesus was provocative. The Roman Empire was tolerant of diverse religions as long as they did not claim exclusivity.
thenVsNow.now: This verse remains one of the most debated in Christianity. In a pluralistic world, the claim that Jesus is the only way to salvation is either the most important truth or the most arrogant claim, depending on one's perspective. Mainstream Christianity has historically affirmed Peter's statement.

verses.text: Now when they saw the boldness of Peter and John, and had perceived that they were unlearned and ignorant men, they marveled. They recognized that they had been with Jesus.
verses.explainer: The Sanhedrin observes that Peter and John are 'unschooled, ordinary men' and is amazed. Then they recognize that 'they had been with Jesus.' The disciples' boldness cannot be explained by education or status -- it can only be explained by their relationship with Jesus.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': 'Unschooled and ordinary', 'explanation': "The Greek terms (agrammatoi and idiotai) mean 'illiterate' and 'laymen.' Peter and John had no formal rabbinical training. Their ability to argue Scripture and speak with authority before the highest court baffled the educated elite."}

verses.text: Seeing the man who was healed standing with them, they could say nothing against it.
verses.explainer: The healed man is standing right there beside them. The evidence is irrefutable -- a man who was lame for over forty years is now standing in court. The Sanhedrin has no counter-argument.

verses.text: But when they had commanded them to go aside out of the council, they conferred among themselves,
verses.explainer: The Sanhedrin confers privately. They have a problem: the miracle is public and undeniable. They cannot deny what happened; they can only try to limit its impact.

verses.text: saying, “What shall we do to these men? Because indeed a notable miracle has been done through them, as can be plainly seen by all who dwell in Jerusalem, and we can’t deny it.
verses.explainer: They admit to each other: 'A notable sign has been performed through them, and we cannot deny it.' Even the opponents acknowledge the miracle. Their problem is not with the evidence but with the implications.

verses.text: But so that this spreads no further among the people, let’s threaten them, that from now on they don’t speak to anyone in this name.”
verses.explainer: Their strategy: threaten the apostles to stop them from speaking. They hope intimidation will accomplish what argument cannot.

verses.text: They called them, and commanded them not to speak at all nor teach in the name of Jesus.
verses.explainer: They command Peter and John 'not to speak or teach at all in the name of Jesus.' This is an official ruling from the highest Jewish authority -- a legally binding gag order.

verses.text: But Peter and John answered them, “Whether it is right in the sight of God to listen to you rather than to God, judge for yourselves,
verses.explainer: Peter and John's response is a landmark statement of civil disobedience: 'Whether it is right in the sight of God to listen to you rather than to God, you judge.' They place God's authority above the Sanhedrin's.

thenVsNow.then: Peter and John's refusal to obey the Sanhedrin was a radical act. The Sanhedrin had genuine legal authority over Jewish religious life. Defying them was not just brave; it was a declaration that a higher authority had overruled them.
thenVsNow.now: This passage has been cited throughout Christian history to justify civil disobedience when human laws contradict divine commands -- from the early church martyrs to the abolitionists to the civil rights movement. The principle that God's authority supersedes human authority remains foundational.

verses.text: for we can’t help telling the things which we saw and heard.”
verses.explainer: 'We cannot help speaking about what we have seen and heard.' Witness is not optional for the apostles -- it is compulsive. They have seen the risen Jesus; silence is impossible.

verses.text: When they had further threatened them, they let them go, finding no way to punish them, because of the people; for everyone glorified God for that which was done.
verses.explainer: The Sanhedrin threatens them further but releases them. They cannot punish Peter and John without provoking the people, who are all praising God for the healing.

verses.text: For the man on whom this miracle of healing was performed was more than forty years old.
verses.explainer: The healed man was over forty years old. Luke provides this detail to emphasize how established and well-known his condition was -- this was not a recent injury but a lifelong disability.
