verses.text: Being let go, they came to their own company, and reported all that the chief priests and the elders had said to them.
verses.explainer: Peter and John return to 'their own' -- the believing community. In the face of official opposition, they go back to the people who will pray with them.

verses.text: When they heard it, they lifted up their voice to God with one accord, and said, “O Lord, you are God, who made the heaven, the earth, the sea, and all that is in them;
verses.explainer: The community responds by praying together, beginning with a confession of God's sovereignty: 'Sovereign Lord, who made the heaven and the earth and the sea and everything in them.' They start with God's power, not their problem.

verses.text:
who by the mouth of your servant, David, said,
‘Why do the nations rage,
and the peoples plot a vain thing?
verses.explainer: They quote Psalm 2, which describes the nations raging and plotting against the Lord and his Anointed One. The early church reads current events through the lens of ancient Scripture.

verses.text:
The kings of the earth take a stand,
and the rulers take council together,
against the Lord, and against his Christ.’
verses.explainer: The psalm continues: kings and rulers 'gathered together against the Lord and against his Christ.' The community interprets their situation as the continuation of the same opposition that killed Jesus.

verses.text: “For truly, in this city against your holy servant, Jesus, whom you anointed, both Herod and Pontius Pilate, with the Gentiles and the people of Israel, were gathered together
verses.explainer: They name the conspirators: Herod (Antipas), Pontius Pilate, the Gentiles, and the peoples of Israel. The crucifixion was a collaboration between all human power structures -- Jewish and Roman, political and religious. Yet they acted within God's predetermined plan.

verses.text: to do whatever your hand and your council foreordained to happen.
verses.explainer: Everything the conspirators did was 'whatever your hand and your plan had predestined to take place.' Human evil operates within divine sovereignty. The worst thing humans did became the best thing God did.

verses.text: Now, Lord, look at their threats, and grant to your servants to speak your word with all boldness,
verses.explainer: Their prayer request is remarkable: they do not ask for safety or deliverance from persecution. They ask for 'boldness to speak your word.' They want more courage, not less trouble.

thenVsNow.then: The early church's prayer for boldness rather than safety reveals their priorities. They expected opposition and considered it normal. The question was not how to avoid persecution but how to speak faithfully through it.
thenVsNow.now: Many modern Christians pray primarily for comfort, safety, and deliverance from difficulty. The early church's prayer challenges this instinct. What if the appropriate prayer is not 'protect me from opposition' but 'give me courage to speak truth despite opposition'?

verses.text: while you stretch out your hand to heal; and that signs and wonders may be done through the name of your holy Servant Jesus.”
verses.explainer: They ask God to stretch out his hand to heal and perform signs and wonders 'through the name of your holy servant Jesus.' They want more miracles, not fewer -- more confrontation with the powers that oppose God.

verses.text: When they had prayed, the place was shaken where they were gathered together. They were all filled with the Holy Spirit, and they spoke the word of God with boldness.
verses.explainer: The place where they are meeting is shaken -- a physical manifestation of God's response. They are 'all filled with the Holy Spirit and spoke the word of God with boldness.' God answers their prayer immediately and exactly.

verses.text: The multitude of those who believed were of one heart and soul. Not one of them claimed that anything of the things which he possessed was his own, but they had all things in common.
verses.explainer: The community is described again: 'one heart and soul,' no one claiming private ownership, sharing everything. The portrait is idealized but not fabricated -- Luke will show the cracks in chapter 5.

verses.text: With great power, the apostles gave their testimony of the resurrection of the Lord Jesus. Great grace was on them all.
verses.explainer: The apostles continue to give powerful testimony to the resurrection of Jesus, and 'great grace' is upon them all. The resurrection remains the central message -- everything else flows from it.

verses.text: For neither was there among them any who lacked, for as many as were owners of lands or houses sold them, and brought the proceeds of the things that were sold,
verses.explainer: No one is needy because those who own land or houses sell them and bring the proceeds to the apostles for distribution. This voluntary communal sharing creates a community with no poverty.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': 'Selling property', 'explanation': "In the ancient Mediterranean world, land was the primary form of wealth and family security. Selling land was an enormous sacrifice -- it meant giving up your family's inheritance and your social safety net. The early believers were willing to liquidate their most valuable assets for the sake of the community."}

verses.text: and laid them at the apostles’ feet, and distribution was made to each, according as anyone had need.
verses.explainer: The proceeds are laid 'at the apostles' feet' -- a gesture of complete trust and surrender. The apostles distribute to each person as they have need. The community functions as a family where resources flow to those who need them most.

verses.text: Joses, who by the apostles was also called Barnabas (which is, being interpreted, Son of Encouragement), a Levite, a man of Cyprus by race,
verses.explainer: Barnabas is introduced as a positive example: a Levite from Cyprus who sells a field and gives the money to the apostles. His name means 'Son of Encouragement,' and his generous act lives up to it.

verses.text: having a field, sold it, and brought the money and laid it at the apostles’ feet.
verses.explainer: Barnabas's act of generosity is highlighted as exemplary. It also sets up the contrast with Ananias and Sapphira in the next chapter, who try to fake the same kind of sacrifice.

explainer.summary: Chapter 5 opens with the shocking deaths of Ananias and Sapphira, who lied about the price of their property to appear more generous than they were. The incident sends a wave of fear through the community. Meanwhile, the apostles continue performing signs and wonders, leading to another arrest by the Sadducees. An angel frees them from prison, and they resume preaching in the Temple. Brought before the Sanhedrin again, they are rescued from execution by the wise counsel of Gamaliel, who argues that if this movement is from God, no one can stop it.
themes: Obedience to God over human authorities
explainer.contextInBook: After the idealized portrait of the community in chapters 2 and 4, chapter 5 introduces the first internal threat (Ananias and Sapphira) alongside the continuing external threat (the Sanhedrin). The church must deal with corruption within as well as opposition without.

wordStudies.translatedAs: To put to the test, to try
wordStudies.meaning: To put to the test, to try. When Peter asks Sapphira why she agreed to 'test the Spirit of the Lord,' he means she was testing whether God could be deceived. The answer is devastating.

wordStudies.translatedAs: Gamaliel I was one of the most respected rabbis in
wordStudies.meaning: Gamaliel I was one of the most respected rabbis in Jewish history, a grandson of the famous Hillel. He was known for moderate, pragmatic views. Paul studied under him (22:3). His counsel to leave the Christians alone reveals a broader perspective than his fellow Sanhedrin members.

verses.text: But a certain man named Ananias, with Sapphira, his wife, sold a possession,
verses.explainer: Ananias and his wife Sapphira sell a piece of property. Their names are ironic: Ananias means 'God is gracious' and Sapphira means 'beautiful.' The story will show neither grace nor beauty in their actions.

verses.text: and kept back part of the price, his wife also being aware of it, and brought a certain part, and laid it at the apostles’ feet.
verses.explainer: They keep back part of the price 'with his wife's knowledge' -- this is a joint conspiracy. They bring only a portion to the apostles, presumably presenting it as the full amount. The sin is not in keeping some money but in lying about it.

thenVsNow.then: Barnabas had just sold a field and given everything. Ananias and Sapphira wanted the same reputation without the same sacrifice. They wanted to look generous without being generous.
thenVsNow.now: The temptation to perform generosity for public approval while privately cutting corners remains as real today as in the first century. Social media has only amplified the incentive to curate a more virtuous public image than reality warrants.

verses.text: But Peter said, “Ananias, why has Satan filled your heart to lie to the Holy Spirit, and to keep back part of the price of the land?
verses.explainer: Peter asks Ananias why Satan has 'filled your heart to lie to the Holy Spirit.' The language echoes the indwelling of the Spirit at Pentecost -- but instead of the Spirit filling Ananias, Satan has filled him. It is a grotesque parody of Pentecost.

verses.text: While you kept it, didn’t it remain your own? After it was sold, wasn’t it in your power? How is it that you have conceived this thing in your heart? You haven’t lied to men, but to God.”
verses.explainer: Peter makes clear: the property was theirs to keep, and the proceeds were theirs to do with as they wished. No one required them to give anything. The sin is not holding back money -- it is lying about it. They lied 'not to men but to God.'

verses.text: Ananias, hearing these words, fell down and died. Great fear came on all who heard these things.
verses.explainer: Ananias hears these words and falls down dead. Great fear seizes everyone who hears. The judgment is immediate and shocking. God takes the integrity of the new community with deadly seriousness.

verses.text: The young men arose and wrapped him up, and they carried him out and buried him.
verses.explainer: Young men wrap Ananias's body and carry him out for burial. The speed of the burial reflects the ancient urgency of handling the dead and the community's stunned response.

verses.text: About three hours later, his wife, not knowing what had happened, came in.
verses.explainer: About three hours later, Sapphira arrives, not knowing what happened to her husband. The gap creates dramatic tension -- she has one chance to tell the truth.

verses.text:
Peter answered her, “Tell me whether you sold the land for so much.”
She said, “Yes, for so much.”
verses.explainer: Peter gives Sapphira the opportunity to come clean: 'Tell me whether you sold the land for this much.' She confirms the lie. The door to honesty was open; she walked past it.

verses.text: But Peter asked her, “How is it that you have agreed together to tempt the Spirit of the Lord? Behold, the feet of those who have buried your husband are at the door, and they will carry you out.”
verses.explainer: Peter pronounces judgment: 'How is it that you have agreed together to test the Spirit of the Lord? The feet of those who buried your husband are at the door, and they will carry you out.' Sapphira has tested whether God's Spirit can be fooled. The answer destroys her.

verses.text: She fell down immediately at his feet, and died. The young men came in and found her dead, and they carried her out and buried her by her husband.
verses.explainer: Sapphira falls dead at Peter's feet. The young men carry her out and bury her beside her husband. The community is purified, but at a terrible cost.

verses.text: Great fear came on the whole assembly, and on all who heard these things.
verses.explainer: Great fear comes upon 'the whole church' -- this is the first time the word 'church' (ekklesia) appears in Acts -- and upon all who hear about it. The fear is appropriate: God is present in this community, and his presence is not tame.

verses.text: By the hands of the apostles many signs and wonders were done among the people. They were all with one accord in Solomon’s porch.
verses.explainer: The apostles continue performing 'many signs and wonders among the people.' The believers meet together in Solomon's Portico -- the same place where Peter preached after healing the lame man.

verses.text: None of the rest dared to join them, however the people honored them.
verses.explainer: No outsider 'dared to join them' -- the fear from the Ananias episode has made the community formidable. But the people hold them in high esteem. The church has become an object of both fear and admiration.

verses.text: More believers were added to the Lord, multitudes of both men and women.
verses.explainer: Despite the fear, believers continue to be added 'multitudes of both men and women.' The pattern of Acts: opposition and even internal judgment do not stop the growth of the church. They intensify it.

verses.text: They even carried out the sick into the streets, and laid them on cots and mattresses, so that as Peter came by, at the least his shadow might overshadow some of them.
verses.explainer: People carry the sick into the streets, hoping that at least Peter's shadow might fall on some of them as he passes. This is folk religion meeting apostolic power -- the people attribute healing to Peter's very shadow.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': "Peter's shadow", 'explanation': "The belief that a person's shadow carried their power was common in the ancient Mediterranean world. Luke does not say the shadow actually healed anyone -- he records the popular expectation. The point is the extraordinary faith (or superstition) that Peter's ministry inspired."}

verses.text: The multitude also came together from the cities around Jerusalem, bringing sick people, and those who were tormented by unclean spirits: and they were all healed.
verses.explainer: People come from the towns around Jerusalem, bringing the sick and those afflicted by unclean spirits, 'and they were all healed.' The ministry that began at the Beautiful Gate has become a regional phenomenon.

verses.text: But the high priest rose up, and all those who were with him (which is the sect of the Sadducees), and they were filled with jealousy,
verses.explainer: The high priest and the Sadducees are 'filled with jealousy.' Luke uses the same word for 'filled' that he used for the Spirit filling the believers. The Sadducees are filled with jealousy as the church is filled with the Spirit -- two competing fillings.

verses.text: and laid hands on the apostles, and put them in public custody.
verses.explainer: They arrest the apostles and put them in public prison. This is the second arrest -- the opposition is escalating from threats to incarceration.

verses.text: But an angel of the Lord opened the prison doors by night, and brought them out, and said,
verses.explainer: An angel of the Lord opens the prison doors at night and brings them out. The first of several miraculous prison breaks in Acts (Peter in chapter 12, Paul and Silas in chapter 16). God is not constrained by locks and guards.

verses.text: “Go stand and speak in the temple to the people all the words of this life.”
verses.explainer: The angel's instruction: 'Go, stand in the Temple and speak to the people all the words of this Life.' They are not told to hide or flee but to go right back to the place where they were arrested and keep preaching.

verses.text: When they heard this, they entered into the temple about daybreak, and taught. But the high priest came, and those who were with him, and called the council together, and all the senate of the children of Israel, and sent to the prison to have them brought.
verses.explainer: At daybreak, they enter the Temple and teach. Meanwhile, the Sanhedrin convenes, unaware that the prisoners have escaped. The dramatic irony is rich -- the court is assembling to judge men who are already free and preaching.

verses.text: But the officers who came didn’t find them in the prison. They returned and reported,
verses.explainer: The officers go to the prison and find it empty. The guards are at their posts, the doors are locked -- but the cells are empty. The scene recalls the empty tomb.

verses.text: “We found the prison shut and locked, and the guards standing before the doors, but when we opened them, we found no one inside!”
verses.explainer: The officers report: 'We found the prison locked securely and the guards standing at the doors, but when we opened them, we found no one inside.' Maximum security, zero prisoners.

verses.text: Now when the high priest, the captain of the temple, and the chief priests heard these words, they were very perplexed about them and what might become of this.
verses.explainer: The captain of the Temple guard and the chief priests are 'greatly perplexed, wondering what this would come to.' They cannot explain the escape, and they sense that events are spiraling beyond their control.

verses.text: One came and told them, “Behold, the men whom you put in prison are in the temple, standing and teaching the people.”
verses.explainer: Someone reports: the men you put in prison are 'standing in the Temple and teaching the people.' The apostles have not fled the city or gone underground. They are doing exactly what they were arrested for, in the most public place possible.

verses.text: Then the captain went with the officers, and brought them without violence, for they were afraid that the people might stone them.
verses.explainer: The captain and officers bring the apostles back 'without violence, for they were afraid of the people' -- the crowd might stone the officers for arresting the apostles. Popular support is now protecting the church.

verses.text: When they had brought them, they set them before the council. The high priest questioned them,
verses.explainer: They set the apostles before the Sanhedrin. The high priest confronts them with an accusation that is also an unwitting compliment.

verses.text: saying, “Didn’t we strictly command you not to teach in this name? Behold, you have filled Jerusalem with your teaching, and intend to bring this man’s blood on us.”
verses.explainer: The high priest says: 'You have filled Jerusalem with your teaching and intend to bring this man's blood upon us.' The gospel has saturated the city. And the accusation about 'bringing this man's blood upon us' is ironic -- the crowd at Jesus' trial said 'His blood be on us!' (Matthew 27:25).

verses.text: But Peter and the apostles answered, “We must obey God rather than men.
verses.explainer: Peter and the apostles deliver one of the most quoted lines in Acts: 'We must obey God rather than men.' This is the second statement of the principle of divine civil disobedience (see 4:19).

thenVsNow.then: The Sanhedrin had legitimate religious authority over Jewish life. The apostles were not anti-authority in principle -- they simply recognized a higher authority. When the two conflicted, God won.
thenVsNow.now: This verse has been invoked throughout history to justify standing against unjust authority -- from early Christian martyrs to Martin Luther to Dietrich Bonhoeffer to civil rights leaders. The principle is straightforward but applying it requires wisdom.

verses.text: The God of our fathers raised up Jesus, whom you killed, hanging him on a tree.
verses.explainer: Peter summarizes the gospel: 'The God of our fathers raised up Jesus, whom you killed by hanging him on a tree.' The phrase 'hanging on a tree' evokes Deuteronomy 21:23, which says anyone hung on a tree is cursed by God.

verses.text: God exalted him with his right hand to be a Prince and a Savior, to give repentance to Israel, and remission of sins.
verses.explainer: God exalted Jesus to his right hand as 'Leader and Savior, to give repentance to Israel and forgiveness of sins.' The one they killed is now in the position of ultimate authority, offering the very thing they need most -- forgiveness.

verses.text: We are His witnesses of these things; and so also is the Holy Spirit, whom God has given to those who obey him.”
verses.explainer: 'We are witnesses of these things, and so is the Holy Spirit, whom God has given to those who obey him.' The apostles and the Spirit bear joint testimony. The evidence is both human (eyewitness) and divine (the Spirit's work).

verses.text: But they, when they heard this, were cut to the heart, and were determined to kill them.
verses.explainer: The Sanhedrin members are 'enraged and wanted to kill them.' The opposition has escalated from annoyance (4:2) to threats (4:17) to imprisonment (5:18) to wanting execution. The trajectory is exactly what happened with Jesus.

verses.text: But one stood up in the council, a Pharisee named Gamaliel, a teacher of the law, honored by all the people, and commanded to put the apostles out for a little while.
verses.explainer: Gamaliel intervenes. He is 'honored by all the people' -- a Pharisee, not a Sadducee, and the most respected rabbi of his generation. His intervention saves the apostles' lives.

verses.text: He said to them, “You men of Israel, be careful concerning these men, what you are about to do.
verses.explainer: Gamaliel tells the Sanhedrin to be careful about what they are about to do. He is counseling restraint and patience -- the opposite of their emotional reaction.

verses.text: For before these days Theudas rose up, making himself out to be somebody; to whom a number of men, about four hundred, joined themselves: who was slain; and all, as many as obeyed him, were dispersed, and came to nothing.
verses.explainer: Gamaliel cites the example of Theudas, a revolutionary who claimed to be somebody, gathered about four hundred followers, and was killed. His movement dissolved. False movements self-destruct.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': 'Theudas', 'explanation': "The historical details of this Theudas are debated. Josephus mentions a Theudas who led a movement around 44-46 AD, but that would be after Gamaliel's speech. Either Gamaliel refers to an earlier Theudas, or Luke has compressed the chronology. The point remains: failed movements collapse."}

verses.text: After this man, Judas of Galilee rose up in the days of the enrollment, and drew away some people after him. He also perished, and all, as many as obeyed him, were scattered abroad.
verses.explainer: He cites another example: Judas the Galilean, who led a revolt during the census of 6-7 AD. He was killed and his followers scattered. The pattern holds: human movements that lack divine backing eventually fail.

verses.text: Now I tell you, withdraw from these men, and leave them alone. For if this counsel or this work is of men, it will be overthrown.
verses.explainer: Gamaliel's conclusion: 'If this plan or this undertaking is of man, it will fail.' Leave them alone and let history judge. If it is merely human, it will collapse on its own.

verses.text: But if it is of God, you will not be able to overthrow it, and you would be found even to be fighting against God!”
verses.explainer: 'But if it is of God, you will not be able to overthrow them. You might even be found opposing God!' This is the most prophetic statement from a non-believer in Acts. Two thousand years later, the movement has not collapsed.

thenVsNow.then: Gamaliel's pragmatic advice saved the apostles' lives, but it was also theologically astute: if God is behind something, fighting it is futile and dangerous. His counsel gave the church time to grow.
thenVsNow.now: Gamaliel's test -- does a movement endure? -- is sometimes cited to validate Christianity's longevity. It is worth noting that the test has limits (other religions have also endured), but the principle of humility before God's purposes remains wise.

verses.text: They agreed with him. Summoning the apostles, they beat them and commanded them not to speak in the name of Jesus, and let them go.
verses.explainer: The Sanhedrin is persuaded by Gamaliel. They beat the apostles, order them again not to speak in Jesus' name, and release them. The beating is a compromise -- less than execution but more than a warning.

verses.text: They therefore departed from the presence of the council, rejoicing that they were counted worthy to suffer dishonor for Jesus’ name.
verses.explainer: The apostles leave 'rejoicing that they were counted worthy to suffer dishonor for the Name.' They are not masochists; they view suffering as validation. If the world hated Jesus, being hated for him is an honor.

thenVsNow.then: Rejoicing in suffering was counterintuitive in a culture that valued honor above almost everything. The apostles' response inverted the honor-shame system: what the world called shameful, they called glorious.
thenVsNow.now: Suffering for one's beliefs is still a reality for Christians in many parts of the world. The apostles' example challenges comfortable Western Christians to consider whether they would still follow Jesus if it cost them something.

verses.text: Every day, in the temple and at home, they never stopped teaching and preaching Jesus, the Christ.
verses.explainer: Every day, in the Temple and from house to house, they 'did not cease teaching and preaching that the Christ is Jesus.' The ban has no effect. The beatings have no effect. They keep going, in public and in private, without stopping.

explainer.summary: As the church grows, a complaint arises: Greek-speaking Jewish widows are being overlooked in the daily food distribution while Hebrew-speaking widows are cared for. The apostles respond by appointing seven men, led by Stephen, to oversee the practical needs of the community. This frees the apostles to focus on prayer and preaching. Stephen, however, proves to be not just an administrator but a powerful preacher and miracle worker. His effectiveness draws fierce opposition, leading to false charges and his arraignment before the Sanhedrin.
themes: Ethnic tension in the early church
explainer.contextInBook: Chapter 6 is a pivotal transition. The church's first internal crisis is also an ethnic one -- Greek-speaking vs. Hebrew-speaking believers. The resolution introduces Stephen, whose martyrdom in chapter 7 will scatter the church beyond Jerusalem and set the stage for the Gentile mission.

wordStudies.translatedAs: Greek-speaking Jews, often from the Diaspora (Jewi
wordStudies.meaning: Greek-speaking Jews, often from the Diaspora (Jewish communities outside Palestine). They spoke Greek as their primary language and may have attended Greek-language synagogues. Their cultural perspective differed from the Hebrew-speaking Palestinian Jews, creating tension even within the believing community.

wordStudies.translatedAs: Literally 'servant' or 'table waiter
wordStudies.meaning: Literally 'servant' or 'table waiter.' The seven men chosen in this chapter are traditionally called the first deacons, though Acts does not use that title. Their role was practical service -- ensuring fair food distribution -- but several of them (Stephen and Philip) quickly became prominent preachers as well.

verses.text: Now in those days, when the number of the disciples was multiplying, a complaint arose from the Hellenists against the Hebrews, because their widows were neglected in the daily service.
verses.explainer: A complaint arises from the Hellenists (Greek-speaking Jewish believers) against the Hebrews (Aramaic-speaking Palestinian Jewish believers): their widows are being 'neglected in the daily distribution' of food. The first church conflict is about equity, not theology.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': 'Hellenists vs. Hebrews', 'explanation': "The early church included Jews from two cultural worlds. 'Hebrews' spoke Aramaic and had grown up in Palestine. 'Hellenists' spoke Greek and came from the Jewish Diaspora. Language, customs, and cultural assumptions differed. The food distribution bias likely reflected unconscious cultural favoritism, not deliberate policy."}

thenVsNow.then: Widows in the ancient world had no social safety net. Without a husband or sons to provide for them, they depended on community charity. The daily distribution of food was literally a matter of survival.
thenVsNow.now: Ethnic and cultural tensions within religious communities remain a persistent challenge. The early church's willingness to name the problem and restructure to address it is a model for how communities can respond to inequity -- not by ignoring it but by creating systems of accountability.

verses.text: The twelve summoned the multitude of the disciples and said, “It is not appropriate for us to forsake the word of God and serve tables.
verses.explainer: The twelve apostles gather the community and say: 'It is not right that we should give up preaching the word of God to serve tables.' This is not arrogance -- it is an acknowledgment that different gifts require different roles. The solution is delegation, not neglect.

verses.text: Therefore select from among you, brothers, seven men of good report, full of the Holy Spirit and of wisdom, whom we may appoint over this business.
verses.explainer: They ask the community to choose 'seven men of good reputation, full of the Spirit and of wisdom.' The qualifications are character-based: reputation, spiritual maturity, and practical wisdom. The community selects; the apostles install.

verses.text: But we will continue steadfastly in prayer and in the ministry of the word.”
verses.explainer: The apostles will devote themselves to 'prayer and the ministry of the word.' Prayer comes first -- before preaching, before planning, before anything. This priority ordering is intentional.

verses.text: These words pleased the whole multitude. They chose Stephen, a man full of faith and of the Holy Spirit, Philip, Prochorus, Nicanor, Timon, Parmenas, and Nicolaus, a proselyte of Antioch;
verses.explainer: The community chooses seven men, all with Greek names: Stephen, Philip, Prochorus, Nicanor, Timon, Parmenas, and Nicolaus (a proselyte from Antioch). The Greek names suggest these are Hellenists -- the church appoints people from the aggrieved group to solve the problem.

annotations.word: Stephen, a man full of faith and of the Holy Spirit

verses.text: whom they set before the apostles. When they had prayed, they laid their hands on them.
verses.explainer: The apostles pray and lay hands on the seven. Laying on of hands was a Jewish practice of conferring authority and blessing. It symbolizes the transfer of responsibility and the community's commissioning.

verses.text: The word of God increased and the number of the disciples multiplied in Jerusalem exceedingly. A great company of the priests were obedient to the faith.
verses.explainer: The result: 'The word of God increased, and the number of the disciples multiplied greatly in Jerusalem, and a great many of the priests became obedient to the faith.' Even Temple priests are converting -- the gospel is penetrating the religious establishment.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': 'Priests becoming believers', 'explanation': 'There were thousands of priests in Jerusalem, organized into 24 divisions that took turns serving in the Temple. Most were not wealthy or powerful -- they were ordinary men fulfilling hereditary duties. Their conversion was significant because it showed the gospel reaching inside the very institution that was opposing the apostles.'}

verses.text: Stephen, full of faith and power, performed great wonders and signs among the people.
verses.explainer: Stephen, 'full of grace and power,' performs great wonders and signs among the people. He was appointed to serve tables but quickly proves to be a formidable preacher and miracle worker. The Spirit does not respect job descriptions.

verses.text: But some of those who were of the synagogue called “The Libertines”, and of the Cyrenians, of the Alexandrians, and of those of Cilicia and Asia arose, disputing with Stephen.
verses.explainer: Opposition arises from members of 'the Synagogue of the Freedmen' -- Jews from Cyrene, Alexandria, Cilicia, and Asia. These are Hellenistic Jews like Stephen himself. His most dangerous opponents share his cultural background.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': 'Synagogue of the Freedmen', 'explanation': "Freedmen (Libertinoi) were Jews or their descendants who had been enslaved by Rome and subsequently freed. They formed their own synagogue in Jerusalem. The members from Cilicia may have included Saul of Tarsus, who was from Cilicia -- this could be Paul's first encounter with the Christian movement."}

verses.text: They weren’t able to withstand the wisdom and the Spirit by which he spoke.
verses.explainer: They 'were not able to withstand the wisdom and the Spirit by which he spoke.' Stephen wins every argument. His opponents cannot match his logic or his spiritual authority.

verses.text: Then they secretly induced men to say, “We have heard him speak blasphemous words against Moses and God.”
verses.explainer: Unable to defeat Stephen in debate, they 'secretly induced men' to make false accusations: 'We have heard him speak blasphemous words against Moses and God.' The pattern is identical to Jesus' trial -- when you cannot refute the message, destroy the messenger.

verses.text: They stirred up the people, the elders, and the scribes, and came against him and seized him, and brought him in to the council,
verses.explainer: They stir up the people, the elders, and the scribes, who seize Stephen and bring him before the Sanhedrin. The entire power structure is mobilized against one man.

verses.text: and set up false witnesses who said, “This man never stops speaking blasphemous words against this holy place and the law.
verses.explainer: False witnesses testify: 'This man never stops speaking against this holy place and the law.' The charges are not entirely invented -- Stephen probably did say things about the Temple and the law that his opponents found threatening. But the witnesses distort his meaning.

verses.text: For we have heard him say that this Jesus of Nazareth will destroy this place, and will change the customs which Moses delivered to us.”
verses.explainer: They claim Stephen said 'Jesus of Nazareth will destroy this place and change the customs that Moses delivered to us.' This echoes the false charges against Jesus at his trial (Mark 14:58). The same playbook is being used against Jesus' followers.

verses.text: All who sat in the council, fastening their eyes on him, saw his face like it was the face of an angel.
verses.explainer: Everyone in the Sanhedrin looks at Stephen and sees 'his face was like the face of an angel.' In the midst of false accusations and mortal danger, Stephen radiates something supernatural. The scene recalls Moses' shining face after encountering God on Sinai.

explainer.summary: Stephen delivers the longest speech in Acts -- a sweeping retelling of Israel's history from Abraham to Solomon. His point is devastating: Israel has a pattern of rejecting the messengers God sends. They rejected Joseph, resisted Moses, persecuted the prophets, and now they have betrayed and murdered the Righteous One. The speech enrages the Sanhedrin, and Stephen is dragged out and stoned to death, becoming the first Christian martyr. As he dies, he sees Jesus standing at God's right hand and prays for his killers' forgiveness. A young man named Saul watches approvingly.
themes: Israel's pattern of rejecting God's messengers
The Temple is not God's dwelling
Stephen as a parallel to Jesus
explainer.contextInBook: Stephen's martyrdom is the hinge point of Acts. The persecution that follows scatters believers beyond Jerusalem (8:1), fulfilling the next phase of Jesus' commission in 1:8. Stephen's death plants the seed that will eventually bear fruit in Paul's conversion -- the blood of the martyr becomes the seed of the apostle.

wordStudies.translatedAs: Literally 'hard-necked
wordStudies.meaning: Literally 'hard-necked.' An image from farming -- a stubborn ox that refuses to turn when the yoke is pulled. Stephen applies this classic Old Testament accusation (Exodus 33:3) to the current generation, saying they are as resistant to God as their ancestors were.

wordStudies.translatedAs: Stephen sees Jesus 'standing' at the right hand of
wordStudies.meaning: Stephen sees Jesus 'standing' at the right hand of God. Elsewhere in the New Testament, Jesus is described as 'seated' at God's right hand (Psalm 110:1). The fact that Jesus is standing has been interpreted as rising to welcome his first martyr, standing as a witness in Stephen's defense, or standing in readiness to return.

verses.text: The high priest said, “Are these things so?”
verses.explainer: The high priest asks Stephen: 'Are these things so?' It is a formal legal question. Stephen's response will not be a simple denial but a comprehensive reinterpretation of Israel's entire history.

verses.text: He said, “Brothers and fathers, listen. The God of glory appeared to our father Abraham, when he was in Mesopotamia, before he lived in Haran,
verses.explainer: Stephen begins: 'Brothers and fathers, listen. The God of glory appeared to our father Abraham when he was in Mesopotamia, before he lived in Haran.' He starts at the very beginning of Israel's story, with Abraham's call from pagan territory.

verses.text: and said to him, ‘Get out of your land, and from your relatives, and come into a land which I will show you.’
verses.explainer: God told Abraham to leave his land and relatives and go to a new land. Stephen emphasizes that God's call required Abraham to leave everything familiar. The people of God have always been a pilgrim people, not settled and comfortable.

verses.text: Then he came out of the land of the Chaldaeans, and lived in Haran. From there, when his father was dead, God moved him into this land, where you are now living.
verses.explainer: Abraham left Chaldea and settled in Haran, then moved to the current land after his father died. Stephen tracks the geography of obedience -- Abraham kept moving as God directed.

verses.text: He gave him no inheritance in it, no, not so much as to set his foot on. He promised that he would give it to him for a possession, and to his offspring after him, when he still had no child.
verses.explainer: God gave Abraham no inheritance in the land -- 'not even enough to set his foot on.' But he promised it to his descendants. Abraham lived his entire life in the Promised Land without owning any of it. Faith meant trusting a promise, not holding a deed.

verses.text: God spoke in this way: that his offspring would live as aliens in a strange land, and that they would be enslaved and mistreated for four hundred years.
verses.explainer: God told Abraham his descendants would be 'sojourners in a land belonging to others' who would 'enslave and mistreat them for four hundred years.' The promise came with a warning: before the blessing, there would be suffering.

verses.text: ‘I will judge the nation to which they will be in bondage,’ said God, ‘and after that will they come out, and serve me in this place.’
verses.explainer: God promised to judge the enslaving nation and bring his people out to worship him 'in this place.' The pattern is established: suffering, then deliverance, then worship. Stephen's audience should recognize themselves in this pattern.

verses.text: He gave him the covenant of circumcision. So Abraham became the father of Isaac, and circumcised him the eighth day. Isaac became the father of Jacob, and Jacob became the father of the twelve patriarchs.
verses.explainer: God gave Abraham the covenant of circumcision. Abraham fathered Isaac, Isaac fathered Jacob, and Jacob fathered the twelve patriarchs. Stephen is building toward the Joseph story, where the pattern of rejection begins.

verses.text: “The patriarchs, moved with jealousy against Joseph, sold him into Egypt. God was with him,
verses.explainer: The patriarchs were 'jealous of Joseph' and sold him into Egypt. 'But God was with him.' Here is Stephen's thesis: Israel's own ancestors rejected the one God had chosen to save them. Joseph's brothers rejected Joseph -- just as the current generation rejected Jesus.

thenVsNow.then: Joseph's brothers sold him into slavery out of jealousy, not realizing he was God's instrument for their own survival during the coming famine. They rejected their deliverer.
thenVsNow.now: Stephen's parallel is devastating: just as the patriarchs rejected Joseph, the current Jewish leaders rejected Jesus. In both cases, the rejected one turned out to be the God-appointed savior.

verses.text: and delivered him out of all his afflictions, and gave him favor and wisdom before Pharaoh, king of Egypt. He made him governor over Egypt and all his house.
verses.explainer: God rescued Joseph from all his afflictions and gave him favor and wisdom before Pharaoh, who made him ruler over Egypt. The rejected one became the ruler -- a pattern Stephen sees fulfilled in Jesus.

verses.text: Now a famine came over all the land of Egypt and Canaan, and great affliction. Our fathers found no food.
verses.explainer: A famine came over all Egypt and Canaan, bringing 'great affliction.' The brothers who rejected Joseph now desperately needed him. Their survival depended on the very person they had thrown away.

verses.text: But when Jacob heard that there was grain in Egypt, he sent out our fathers the first time.
verses.explainer: Jacob heard there was grain in Egypt and sent the brothers on their first visit. They did not yet recognize Joseph. The parallel: Israel encounters Jesus but does not recognize him.

verses.text: On the second time Joseph was made known to his brothers, and Joseph’s race was revealed to Pharaoh.
verses.explainer: On the second visit, Joseph revealed himself to his brothers. Stephen's pattern: the deliverer is rejected on the first encounter but revealed on the second. The early church saw Jesus' second coming in this light.

verses.text: Joseph sent, and summoned Jacob, his father, and all his relatives, seventy-five souls.
verses.explainer: Joseph sent for his father Jacob and all his relatives, seventy-five people in all. Stephen follows the Greek Old Testament (Septuagint) count of 75 rather than the Hebrew text's 70. This is natural for a Hellenistic speaker.

verses.text: Jacob went down into Egypt, and he died, himself and our fathers,
verses.explainer: Jacob went down to Egypt, where he and the patriarchs eventually died. Stephen moves the story along -- the patriarchs' sojourn in Egypt is a prelude to the next phase of the pattern.

verses.text: and they were brought back to Shechem, and laid in the tomb that Abraham bought for a price in silver from the children of Hamor of Shechem.
verses.explainer: Their bodies were carried back to Shechem and buried in the tomb Abraham purchased. Stephen compresses some geographical details -- the point is continuity between the promises to Abraham and the unfolding story.

verses.text: “But as the time of the promise came close which God had sworn to Abraham, the people grew and multiplied in Egypt,
verses.explainer: As the time of God's promise to Abraham drew near, 'the people increased and multiplied in Egypt.' Growth often precedes crisis in the biblical narrative -- prosperity leads to oppression.

verses.text: until there arose a different king, who didn’t know Joseph.
verses.explainer: A new king arose 'who did not know Joseph.' The memory of Joseph's service was erased, and the Egyptians began to fear the Israelites' numbers. The savior is forgotten, and the saved become slaves.

verses.text: The same took advantage of our race, and mistreated our fathers, and forced them to throw out their babies, so that they wouldn’t stay alive.
verses.explainer: This king 'dealt treacherously with our people' and forced them to abandon their babies. The oppression escalates to infanticide -- the worst abuse of power.

verses.text: At that time Moses was born, and was exceedingly handsome. He was nourished three months in his father’s house.
verses.explainer: Moses is born 'at that time' -- divine timing again. He is 'beautiful before God' -- the Greek phrase suggests something more than physical attractiveness; Moses was marked by God from birth.

verses.text: When he was thrown out, Pharaoh’s daughter took him up, and reared him as her own son.
verses.explainer: When Moses was exposed, Pharaoh's daughter adopted him and raised him as her own son. The deliverer grows up in the household of the oppressor -- another pattern that resonates with God's surprising methods.

verses.text: Moses was instructed in all the wisdom of the Egyptians. He was mighty in his words and works.
verses.explainer: Moses was 'educated in all the wisdom of the Egyptians' and was 'mighty in his words and deeds.' Egypt's own education system trained the man who would lead Israel out of Egypt. God uses the enemy's resources for his own purposes.

verses.text: But when he was forty years old, it came into his heart to visit his brothers, the children of Israel.
verses.explainer: At forty years old, Moses visits his own people. Stephen divides Moses' life into three forty-year periods (40 in Egypt, 40 in Midian, 40 leading Israel). This was a common Jewish tradition.

verses.text: Seeing one of them suffer wrong, he defended him, and avenged him who was oppressed, striking the Egyptian.
verses.explainer: Moses sees an Israelite being mistreated and kills the Egyptian. He 'avenged the oppressed and struck down the Egyptian.' Stephen does not condemn the act -- he presents Moses as acting on behalf of his people.

verses.text: He supposed that his brothers understood that God, by his hand, was giving them deliverance; but they didn’t understand.
verses.explainer: Moses 'supposed that his brothers would understand that God was giving them salvation by his hand.' But they did not understand. Here is the critical point: Moses was God's deliverer, but his own people did not recognize him.

verses.text: “The day following, he appeared to them as they fought, and urged them to be at peace again, saying, ‘Sirs, you are brothers. Why do you wrong one another?’
verses.explainer: The next day, Moses tries to reconcile two Israelites who are fighting. He intervenes as a peacemaker, but he is rejected.

verses.text: But he who did his neighbor wrong pushed him away, saying, ‘Who made you a ruler and a judge over us?
verses.explainer: The one doing wrong 'pushed Moses aside' and said: 'Who made you a ruler and a judge over us?' The deliverer is rejected by his own people. Stephen is driving home the parallel with Jesus.

thenVsNow.then: Moses was rejected as deliverer by the very people he was trying to save. He had to flee to Midian for forty years before God sent him back. The rejection delayed but did not prevent God's plan.
thenVsNow.now: Stephen's pattern -- the deliverer is rejected before being accepted -- is his central argument. Jesus was rejected and killed, but that does not mean he is not the Messiah. It means he is following the pattern of every deliverer God has ever sent.

verses.text: Do you want to kill me, as you killed the Egyptian yesterday?’
verses.explainer: The man asks Moses: 'Do you want to kill me as you killed the Egyptian yesterday?' Moses' act of violence has been exposed. He is vulnerable and rejected.

verses.text: Moses fled at this saying, and became a stranger in the land of Midian, where he became the father of two sons.
verses.explainer: Moses flees to Midian, where he becomes a foreigner with two sons. The deliverer is in exile -- rejected by his own people, living as an alien in a foreign land.

verses.text: “When forty years were fulfilled, an angel of the Lord appeared to him in the wilderness of Mount Sinai, in a flame of fire in a bush.
verses.explainer: After forty years in Midian, an angel appears to Moses in the flame of a burning bush in the wilderness near Mount Sinai. God has not forgotten his promise or his deliverer.

verses.text: When Moses saw it, he wondered at the sight. As he came close to see, a voice of the Lord came to him,
verses.explainer: Moses is amazed at the sight of the burning bush. As he approaches, God speaks. The bush that burns without being consumed represents God's presence -- consuming fire that does not destroy.

verses.text: ‘I am the God of your fathers, the God of Abraham, the God of Isaac, and the God of Jacob.’ Moses trembled, and dared not look.
verses.explainer: God identifies himself: 'I am the God of your fathers -- the God of Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob.' Moses trembles and does not dare to look. The encounter is terrifying because God is real, present, and speaking.

verses.text: The Lord said to him, ‘Take off your sandals, for the place where you stand is holy ground.
verses.explainer: God tells Moses to remove his sandals because 'the place where you are standing is holy ground.' Stephen's theological point is crucial: this is the wilderness, not the Temple. God makes ground holy by his presence. Holiness is not limited to buildings or locations.

thenVsNow.then: God declared a patch of wilderness 'holy ground' simply because he was there. This challenges the assumption that sacred spaces are more real than ordinary ones -- any place God shows up becomes holy.
thenVsNow.now: Stephen's point directly challenges the Sanhedrin's Temple-centered theology. If God appeared in a burning bush in the wilderness, he does not need a building. This idea -- that God is not confined to sacred spaces -- remains transformative.

verses.text: I have surely seen the affliction of my people that is in Egypt, and have heard their groaning. I have come down to deliver them. Now come, I will send you into Egypt.’
verses.explainer: God has seen the affliction of his people in Egypt, heard their groaning, and come down to deliver them. The language echoes Exodus 3:7-8. God is moved by suffering and acts to end it.

verses.text: “This Moses, whom they refused, saying, ‘Who made you a ruler and a judge?’—God has sent him as both a ruler and a deliverer by the hand of the angel who appeared to him in the bush.
verses.explainer: Now Stephen drives the point home: 'This Moses whom they rejected, saying, Who made you a ruler and a judge? -- this man God sent as both ruler and redeemer.' The rejected one becomes the chosen one. The pattern is unmistakable.

verses.text: This man led them out, having worked wonders and signs in Egypt, in the Red Sea, and in the wilderness for forty years.
verses.explainer: Moses led them out with 'wonders and signs in the land of Egypt, at the Red Sea, and in the wilderness for forty years.' Stephen lists three arenas of God's power through the rejected-then-vindicated deliverer.

verses.text: This is that Moses, who said to the children of Israel, ‘The Lord our God will raise up a prophet for you from among your brothers, like me.’
verses.explainer: Moses himself prophesied: 'God will raise up a prophet for you from among your brothers, like me.' Stephen quotes the same Deuteronomy passage Peter cited in 3:22. Jesus is the prophet like Moses -- and they have treated him exactly as they treated Moses.

verses.text: This is he who was in the assembly in the wilderness with the angel that spoke to him on Mount Sinai, and with our fathers, who received living revelations to give to us,
verses.explainer: Moses was 'in the assembly in the wilderness' with the angel who spoke on Mount Sinai. He received 'living words' to give to them. Stephen calls the Torah 'living oracles' -- he is not attacking the law; he reveres it.

verses.text: to whom our fathers wouldn’t be obedient, but rejected him, and turned back in their hearts to Egypt,
verses.explainer: Yet 'our fathers refused to obey him' and 'in their hearts turned back to Egypt.' Again the pattern: God sends a deliverer, the people reject him. Moses received God's word; the people wanted to go back to slavery.

verses.text: saying to Aaron, ‘Make us gods that will go before us, for as for this Moses, who led us out of the land of Egypt, we don’t know what has become of him.’
verses.explainer: They told Aaron: 'Make us gods to go before us, because this Moses who led us out of Egypt -- we do not know what has become of him.' When the deliverer is absent, the people create substitutes. The golden calf was born from impatience and unbelief.

verses.text: They made a calf in those days, and brought a sacrifice to the idol, and rejoiced in the works of their hands.
verses.explainer: They made a calf idol, offered sacrifice to it, and 'rejoiced in the works of their hands.' Idolatry is worshiping what you have made instead of the God who made you. Stephen will connect this to the Temple.

verses.text:
But God turned, and gave them up to serve the army of the sky, as it is written in the book of the prophets,
‘Did you offer to me slain animals and sacrifices
forty years in the wilderness, O house of Israel?
verses.explainer: God 'turned and gave them over to worship the host of heaven.' When people persist in rejecting God, God sometimes withdraws his restraint and lets them experience the consequences of their choices.

verses.text:
You took up the tabernacle of Moloch,
the star of your god Rephan,
the figures which you made to worship.
I will carry you away beyond Babylon.’
verses.explainer: Stephen quotes Amos: they carried the tent of Moloch and the star of the god Rephan -- idolatrous images. Therefore God sent them into exile beyond Babylon. Idolatry led to judgment and exile.

verses.text: “Our fathers had the tabernacle of the testimony in the wilderness, even as he who spoke to Moses commanded him to make it according to the pattern that he had seen;
verses.explainer: Stephen shifts to the tabernacle: 'Our fathers had the tent of witness in the wilderness.' This portable worship structure was built according to God's pattern -- it went where the people went. God was mobile, not stationary.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': 'Tent of witness', 'explanation': "The tabernacle -- a portable tent-shrine that housed the Ark of the Covenant. It traveled with Israel through the wilderness. Stephen's point: God's original dwelling was mobile and temporary, not a permanent building. The tabernacle was adequate for God's purposes."}

verses.text: which also our fathers, in their turn, brought in with Joshua when they entered into the possession of the nations, whom God drove out before the face of our fathers, to the days of David,
verses.explainer: The tabernacle was brought into the land under Joshua and remained until the days of David. For centuries, God was content with a tent. The tabernacle worked.

verses.text: who found favor in the sight of God, and asked to find a habitation for the God of Jacob.
verses.explainer: David 'found favor before God and asked to find a dwelling place for the God of Jacob.' David wanted to build God a permanent house. God's response was complex -- he honored the desire but gave the task to Solomon.

verses.text: But Solomon built him a house.
verses.explainer: Solomon built the house. Stephen states this plainly but immediately qualifies it.

verses.text: However, the Most High doesn’t dwell in temples made with hands, as the prophet says,
verses.explainer: Here is the theological bombshell: 'The Most High does not dwell in houses made by human hands.' The Temple -- the center of Jewish identity and the Sanhedrin's power base -- cannot contain God.

thenVsNow.then: Stephen is not saying the Temple was wrong but that it was never meant to be the final word on God's presence. Treating the Temple as if God were trapped inside it was idolatry -- worshiping the building instead of the God the building pointed to.
thenVsNow.now: The principle that God cannot be contained in any human-made structure continues to challenge religious institutions. Church buildings, denominations, and traditions are all useful but none can contain God. The moment any institution claims exclusive access to God, it has repeated the error Stephen identified.

verses.text:
‘heaven is my throne,
and the earth a footstool for my feet.
What kind of house will you build me?’ says the Lord.
‘Or what is the place of my rest?
verses.explainer: Stephen quotes Isaiah: 'Heaven is my throne and the earth is my footstool. What house will you build for me, says the Lord?' If heaven is God's throne and earth is his footstool, no building on earth can be his home.

verses.text: Didn’t my hand make all these things?’
verses.explainer: God asks: 'Did not my hand make all these things?' The Creator of the cosmos is not looking for a building. He is looking for hearts that respond to him.

verses.text: “You stiff-necked and uncircumcised in heart and ears, you always resist the Holy Spirit! As your fathers did, so you do.
verses.explainer: Stephen's tone shifts to direct accusation: 'You stiff-necked people, uncircumcised in heart and ears, you always resist the Holy Spirit.' He turns the language of Gentile outsiders ('uncircumcised') against the Jewish leaders. They may be circumcised in body but not in heart.

verses.text: Which of the prophets didn’t your fathers persecute? They killed those who foretold the coming of the Righteous One, of whom you have now become betrayers and murderers.
verses.explainer: He asks: 'Which of the prophets did your fathers not persecute?' Israel killed the prophets who announced the coming of the Righteous One, and now this generation has betrayed and murdered the Righteous One himself. The pattern of rejection reaches its climax in Jesus.

verses.text: You received the law as it was ordained by angels, and didn’t keep it!”
verses.explainer: Stephen concludes: 'You received the law as delivered by angels and did not keep it.' The ultimate irony -- they accuse Stephen of opposing the law, but they are the ones who have consistently broken it. The prosecutors are the real lawbreakers.

verses.text: Now when they heard these things, they were cut to the heart, and they gnashed at him with their teeth.
verses.explainer: The Sanhedrin is 'cut to the heart' -- the same phrase used at Pentecost (2:37). But where the Pentecost crowd responded with 'What shall we do?', the Sanhedrin responds by grinding their teeth at Stephen. Same conviction, opposite response.

verses.text: But he, being full of the Holy Spirit, looked up steadfastly into heaven, and saw the glory of God, and Jesus standing on the right hand of God,
verses.explainer: Stephen, 'full of the Holy Spirit,' looks up and sees 'the glory of God, and Jesus standing at the right hand of God.' While the Sanhedrin rages, Stephen gets a vision of heaven. The contrast between earthly hostility and heavenly glory is absolute.

verses.text: and said, “Behold, I see the heavens opened, and the Son of Man standing at the right hand of God!”
verses.explainer: Stephen declares: 'I see the heavens opened and the Son of Man standing at the right hand of God!' Jesus is standing -- elsewhere he is seated. Many interpret this as Jesus rising to welcome his first martyr home.

verses.text: But they cried out with a loud voice, and stopped their ears, and rushed at him with one accord.
verses.explainer: They cry out with a loud voice, cover their ears, and rush at him together. The covering of ears is a prescribed Jewish response to blasphemy -- they will not listen to another word. The mob mentality takes over.

verses.text: They threw him out of the city, and stoned him. The witnesses placed their garments at the feet of a young man named Saul.
verses.explainer: They drag Stephen out of the city and stone him. The witnesses -- whose testimony had condemned him -- lay their coats at the feet of 'a young man named Saul.' This is Luke's understated introduction of the most important convert in church history.

verses.text: They stoned Stephen as he called out, saying, “Lord Jesus, receive my spirit!”
verses.explainer: As he is being stoned, Stephen prays: 'Lord Jesus, receive my spirit.' He addresses Jesus in the same way one would address God -- committing his spirit to Jesus just as Jesus committed his spirit to the Father (Luke 23:46).

verses.text: He kneeled down, and cried with a loud voice, “Lord, don’t hold this sin against them!” When he had said this, he fell asleep.
verses.explainer: Stephen falls to his knees and cries out: 'Lord, do not hold this sin against them.' His final words echo Jesus' prayer from the cross: 'Father, forgive them.' Then he 'fell asleep' -- a euphemism for death. The first Christian martyr dies praying for his killers' forgiveness.

thenVsNow.then: Stephen's death established the template for Christian martyrdom: bold witness, trust in God, and forgiveness toward persecutors. The word 'martyr' comes from the Greek for 'witness' -- Stephen was both.
thenVsNow.now: Stephen's example of praying for his killers remains one of the most challenging ideals in Christianity. It has inspired countless believers facing persecution to respond with forgiveness rather than vengeance. His prayer for Saul may have been the seed that eventually led to Paul's conversion.

explainer.summary: The persecution triggered by Stephen's death scatters believers throughout Judea and Samaria -- exactly the regions Jesus named in 1:8. Philip, one of the seven deacons, brings the gospel to Samaria with dramatic results. Simon the sorcerer believes and is baptized but later reveals corrupt motives. The chapter climaxes with Philip's encounter with an Ethiopian eunuch on the desert road to Gaza -- a story of Scripture, guidance, and baptism that extends the gospel across ethnic and geographic boundaries.
themes: Persecution as the engine of mission
Simon Magus and the danger of counterfeit faith
explainer.contextInBook: Chapter 8 fulfills the second phase of Jesus' commission in 1:8: 'Judea and Samaria.' The scattering from Jerusalem is not a defeat but a strategic advance. The gospel now crosses ethnic lines that Jews had maintained for centuries.

wordStudies.translatedAs: The buying or selling of spiritual privileges or e
wordStudies.meaning: The buying or selling of spiritual privileges or ecclesiastical positions. Named after Simon the sorcerer who tried to buy the power to confer the Holy Spirit. The term entered church law and remained a major issue throughout medieval Christianity.

wordStudies.translatedAs: A castrated male, often serving in royal courts as
wordStudies.meaning: A castrated male, often serving in royal courts as a trusted official. Under Deuteronomy 23:1, eunuchs were excluded from the assembly of the Lord. Isaiah 56:3-5 promised that this exclusion would be overturned in the messianic age. The Ethiopian eunuch's baptism fulfills that promise.

verses.text: Saul was consenting to his death. A great persecution arose against the assembly which was in Jerusalem in that day. They were all scattered abroad throughout the regions of Judea and Samaria, except for the apostles.
verses.explainer: Saul approves of Stephen's execution. A great persecution breaks out against the church in Jerusalem, scattering everyone except the apostles throughout Judea and Samaria. The irony is thick: persecution designed to destroy the movement becomes the mechanism that spreads it.

thenVsNow.then: The Jerusalem authorities thought they could contain the Jesus movement by killing its leaders and scattering its members. Instead, every scattered believer became a missionary, carrying the message to new territories.
thenVsNow.now: The pattern of persecution fueling expansion has repeated throughout church history -- from the Roman Empire to modern China. Movements built on genuine conviction tend to spread when pressured, because you cannot scatter fire without spreading it.

verses.text: Devout men buried Stephen, and lamented greatly over him.
verses.explainer: Devout men bury Stephen and mourn deeply for him. Even amid persecution, the community honors its dead. Stephen's burial parallels Jesus' -- faithful people care for the body of the martyr.

verses.text: But Saul ravaged the assembly, entering into every house, and dragged both men and women off to prison.
verses.explainer: Saul ravages the church, going house to house, dragging off men and women and committing them to prison. The language is violent -- 'ravaging' (Greek: lumainomai) means to destroy or ruin, like a wild animal tearing its prey. Saul is systematic and ruthless.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': 'House to house persecution', 'explanation': "The early church met in homes, not public buildings. Saul's house-to-house campaign shows that the authorities knew where believers gathered. There was no anonymity in the early church -- to be known as a follower of Jesus was to be vulnerable."}

verses.text: Therefore those who were scattered abroad went around preaching the word.
verses.explainer: Those who were scattered 'went about preaching the word.' The Greek word for 'preaching' here (euangelizomenoi) means 'gospeling' -- sharing the good news wherever they went. They did not flee as silent refugees but as active missionaries.

verses.text: Philip went down to the city of Samaria, and proclaimed to them the Christ.
verses.explainer: Philip goes down to 'the city of Samaria' and proclaims the Christ to them. This is a stunning development -- Jews and Samaritans had been hostile neighbors for centuries. Philip, a Hellenistic Jew, crosses one of the deepest ethnic divides in the ancient world.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': 'Samaritans', 'explanation': "The Samaritans were descendants of the northern Israelite tribes who intermarried with Assyrian settlers after 722 BC. Jews considered them racial and religious half-breeds. They had their own temple on Mount Gerizim and their own version of the Torah. John 4:9 notes: 'Jews do not associate with Samaritans.' Philip's mission there was revolutionary."}

verses.text: The multitudes listened with one accord to the things that were spoken by Philip, when they heard and saw the signs which he did.
verses.explainer: The crowds pay close attention to Philip, hearing his words and seeing the signs he performs. The combination of proclamation and demonstration convinces them. The message and the miracles work together.

verses.text: For unclean spirits came out of many of those who had them. They came out, crying with a loud voice. Many who had been paralyzed and lame were healed.
verses.explainer: Unclean spirits come out of many people with loud cries, and many who are paralyzed or lame are healed. Philip's ministry mirrors Jesus' own -- casting out demons and healing the disabled. The kingdom is advancing through a deacon, not just through apostles.

verses.text: There was great joy in that city.
verses.explainer: There is 'much joy in that city.' The gospel produces joy -- a repeated theme in Acts. The Samaritans, long despised and excluded, discover that God's favor extends to them.

verses.text: But there was a certain man, Simon by name, who used to practice sorcery in the city, and amazed the people of Samaria, making himself out to be some great one,
verses.explainer: A man named Simon had previously practiced sorcery in Samaria, amazing the people and claiming to be 'someone great.' He had built a following through magical displays and self-promotion.

verses.text: to whom they all listened, from the least to the greatest, saying, “This man is that great power of God.”
verses.explainer: The people, from the least to the greatest, called Simon 'the Great Power of God.' He had successfully positioned himself as a divine figure. His fame was city-wide and deep-rooted.

verses.text: They listened to him, because for a long time he had amazed them with his sorceries.
verses.explainer: They followed Simon because 'for a long time he had amazed them with his sorceries.' His influence was not recent or shallow -- it had been built over years. Philip is confronting an entrenched spiritual system.

verses.text: But when they believed Philip preaching good news concerning God’s Kingdom and the name of Jesus Christ, they were baptized, both men and women.
verses.explainer: But when Philip preaches about the kingdom of God and the name of Jesus Christ, both men and women are baptized. The gospel displaces Simon's sorcery. The people recognize the difference between tricks and genuine spiritual power.

verses.text: Simon himself also believed. Being baptized, he continued with Philip. Seeing signs and great miracles occurring, he was amazed.
verses.explainer: Simon himself believes and is baptized. He follows Philip around, astonished by the signs and great miracles he sees. Whether Simon's faith is genuine or merely impressed by superior power remains an open question -- one the next verses will answer.

thenVsNow.then: Simon was used to being the most powerful spiritual figure in Samaria. When he encountered Philip's miracles, he recognized something greater than his own abilities. But his response reveals the difference between being impressed by power and being transformed by it.
thenVsNow.now: Simon's story raises an enduring question: Is it possible to be fascinated by Christianity's effects without being changed by its message? People can be attracted to the church's community, music, or charismatic leaders while remaining fundamentally self-centered.

verses.text: Now when the apostles who were at Jerusalem heard that Samaria had received the word of God, they sent Peter and John to them,
verses.explainer: The apostles in Jerusalem hear that Samaria has received the word of God and send Peter and John. The mother church sends its senior leaders to verify and affirm what has happened. This is not suspicion but pastoral oversight.

verses.text: who, when they had come down, prayed for them, that they might receive the Holy Spirit;
verses.explainer: Peter and John pray that the Samaritans may receive the Holy Spirit, because the Spirit had not yet fallen on any of them -- they had only been baptized in the name of Jesus. The separation between baptism and the Spirit's coming is unusual and debated.

thenVsNow.then: The delay of the Spirit until the apostles arrived may have served a specific historical purpose: it required the Jerusalem church to officially validate the Samaritan believers, preventing the new movement from immediately splitting along Jewish-Samaritan lines.
thenVsNow.now: The relationship between water baptism and the Holy Spirit's coming remains one of the most debated topics in Christian theology. Different traditions emphasize different aspects of the Samarian experience.

verses.text: for as yet he had fallen on none of them. They had only been baptized in the name of Christ Jesus.
verses.explainer: Luke reiterates: the Spirit had not yet come upon any of them; they had only been baptized into the name of the Lord Jesus. The repetition underscores that something was missing despite their genuine faith and baptism.

verses.text: Then they laid their hands on them, and they received the Holy Spirit.
verses.explainer: Peter and John lay their hands on them, and they receive the Holy Spirit. The apostolic laying on of hands confirms the Samaritan believers as full members of the same community, breaking centuries of division.

verses.text: Now when Simon saw that the Holy Spirit was given through the laying on of the apostles’ hands, he offered them money,
verses.explainer: Simon sees that the Spirit is given through the laying on of the apostles' hands and offers them money: 'Give me this power too.' He sees the Spirit as a commodity to be purchased -- a spiritual technology to add to his repertoire.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': 'Purchasing spiritual power', 'explanation': 'In the ancient world, magical knowledge and spiritual techniques were routinely bought and sold. Traveling sorcerers paid for new spells and incantations. Simon applied the same commercial logic to the Holy Spirit, not understanding that the Spirit is a gift, not a product.'}

verses.text: saying, “Give me also this power, that whomever I lay my hands on may receive the Holy Spirit.”
verses.explainer: Simon asks: 'Give me this power so that anyone on whom I lay my hands may receive the Holy Spirit.' His request reveals his heart -- he wants power over others, not transformation of himself.

verses.text: But Peter said to him, “May your silver perish with you, because you thought you could obtain the gift of God with money!
verses.explainer: Peter's rebuke is fierce: 'May your silver perish with you, because you thought you could buy the gift of God with money!' The Spirit is a gift (Greek: dorea) -- free, unearned, and not for sale. Simon's offer is not just mistaken; it is fundamentally corrupt.

verses.text: You have neither part nor lot in this matter, for your heart isn’t right before God.
verses.explainer: Peter tells Simon: 'You have neither part nor lot in this matter, for your heart is not right before God.' Despite his baptism, Simon's heart has not changed. External religious acts without internal transformation are empty.

verses.text: Repent therefore of this, your wickedness, and ask God if perhaps the thought of your heart may be forgiven you.
verses.explainer: Peter urges Simon to repent and pray that God might forgive 'the intent of your heart.' The door is not closed -- repentance is still possible. Peter diagnoses the problem (a corrupted heart) and prescribes the cure (repentance and prayer).

verses.text: For I see that you are in the poison of bitterness and in the bondage of iniquity.”
verses.explainer: Peter sees that Simon is 'in the gall of bitterness and in the bond of iniquity.' The language echoes Deuteronomy 29:18 -- Simon is poisoned by bitterness and chained by sin. His sorcery background has left deep roots.

verses.text: Simon answered, “Pray for me to the Lord, that none of the things which you have spoken happen to me.”
verses.explainer: Simon responds: 'Pray for me to the Lord, that nothing of what you have said may come upon me.' Some see this as genuine repentance; others see Simon still trying to manipulate spiritual power through others' prayers. Luke leaves his fate ambiguous.

verses.text: They therefore, when they had testified and spoken the word of the Lord, returned to Jerusalem, and preached the Good News to many villages of the Samaritans.
verses.explainer: Peter and John testify and speak the word of the Lord, then return to Jerusalem, preaching the gospel in many villages of the Samaritans along the way. The apostles who came to verify now become missionaries themselves.

verses.text: But an angel of the Lord spoke to Philip, saying, “Arise, and go toward the south to the way that goes down from Jerusalem to Gaza. This is a desert.”
verses.explainer: An angel tells Philip: 'Rise and go south to the road that goes down from Jerusalem to Gaza.' This is a desert road -- an unlikely place for evangelism. Philip obeys without asking why.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': 'The road to Gaza', 'explanation': 'Gaza was the last major city before the Sinai desert and the road to Egypt and Africa. The old city of Gaza had been destroyed and the road through it was desolate. Sending Philip to a desert road seemed irrational, but God was arranging a divine appointment.'}

verses.text: He arose and went; and behold, there was a man of Ethiopia, a eunuch of great authority under Candace, queen of the Ethiopians, who was over all her treasure, who had come to Jerusalem to worship.
verses.explainer: Philip meets an Ethiopian eunuch, a court official of Candace, queen of the Ethiopians, who was in charge of all her treasury. He had come to Jerusalem to worship and was returning home, reading the prophet Isaiah in his chariot.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': 'Candace, queen of the Ethiopians', 'explanation': "Candace (Kandake) was the title for the queen mothers who ruled the kingdom of Meroe in Nubia (modern Sudan), not the Ethiopia of today. This was a powerful African kingdom with its own pyramids, writing system, and iron-working technology. The eunuch's high position shows the gospel reaching the corridors of African royal power."}

verses.text: He was returning and sitting in his chariot, and was reading the prophet Isaiah.
verses.explainer: The eunuch is reading the prophet Isaiah while riding in his chariot. He has traveled over a thousand miles to worship in Jerusalem and is now studying Scripture on his journey home. His devotion is extraordinary.

thenVsNow.then: An Ethiopian official reading an Isaiah scroll in his chariot represented enormous wealth and devotion. Scrolls were hand-copied and extremely expensive. He had invested heavily in both the journey and the text.
thenVsNow.now: The eunuch's hunger for Scripture despite limited access contrasts sharply with modern believers who have multiple Bible translations on their phones but rarely read them. Access to Scripture has never been easier; engagement with it has rarely been more superficial.

verses.text: The Spirit said to Philip, “Go near, and join yourself to this chariot.”
verses.explainer: The Spirit tells Philip: 'Go over and join this chariot.' The Spirit provides specific, real-time guidance. Philip does not design an evangelism strategy -- he simply follows the Spirit's direction to one person.

verses.text: Philip ran to him, and heard him reading Isaiah the prophet, and said, “Do you understand what you are reading?”
verses.explainer: Philip runs to the chariot and hears the eunuch reading Isaiah aloud. Ancient reading was almost always done aloud, even when reading alone. Philip asks: 'Do you understand what you are reading?'

verses.text: He said, “How can I, unless someone explains it to me?” He begged Philip to come up and sit with him.
verses.explainer: The eunuch responds: 'How can I, unless someone guides me?' He invites Philip to sit with him. His humility is striking -- a powerful court official admits his need for help understanding Scripture and welcomes a stranger as his teacher.

verses.text:
Now the passage of the Scripture which he was reading was this,
“He was led as a sheep to the slaughter.
As a lamb before his shearer is silent,
so he doesn’t open his mouth.
verses.explainer: The passage the eunuch is reading is Isaiah 53:7-8: 'Like a sheep he was led to the slaughter, and like a lamb before its shearer is silent, so he opens not his mouth.' This is the Suffering Servant passage -- one of the most debated texts in Judaism.

verses.text:
In his humiliation, his judgment was taken away.
Who will declare His generation?
For his life is taken from the earth.”
verses.explainer: The passage continues: 'In his humiliation, justice was denied him. Who can describe his generation? For his life is taken away from the earth.' The eunuch is reading about unjust suffering, silenced innocence, and a life cut short.

verses.text: The eunuch answered Philip, “Who is the prophet talking about? About himself, or about someone else?”
