wordStudies.translatedAs: To declare righteous, to acquit
wordStudies.meaning: To declare righteous, to acquit. Paul introduces this term at the end of his Pisidian Antioch sermon: everyone who believes in Jesus is 'justified' -- declared right with God -- in a way that the law of Moses could never accomplish. This is a preview of the theology Paul will develop fully in his letter to the Romans.

verses.text: Now in the assembly that was at Antioch there were some prophets and teachers: Barnabas, Simeon who was called Niger, Lucius of Cyrene, Manaen the foster brother of Herod the tetrarch, and Saul.
verses.explainer: The Antioch church leadership includes Barnabas, Simeon called Niger (a Latin name meaning 'black'), Lucius of Cyrene (North Africa), Manaen (who grew up with Herod Antipas), and Saul. This leadership team spans Africa, the Middle East, and the Mediterranean -- a diverse group leading a diverse church.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': 'Antioch church leadership', 'explanation': "The diversity of this leadership team is remarkable: Barnabas (a Levite from Cyprus), Simeon Niger (likely African), Lucius of Cyrene (from modern Libya), Manaen (raised in Herod's court, Greek: syntrophos, meaning 'foster brother' or childhood companion of the tetrarch), and Saul (a Pharisee from Tarsus). No single ethnicity or social class dominates."}

verses.text: As they served the Lord and fasted, the Holy Spirit said, “Separate Barnabas and Saul for me, for the work to which I have called them.”
verses.explainer: While they are worshiping and fasting, the Holy Spirit says: 'Set apart for me Barnabas and Saul for the work to which I have called them.' The mission originates not from human strategic planning but from the Spirit's direct command during worship.

verses.text: Then, when they had fasted and prayed and laid their hands on them, they sent them away.
verses.explainer: After fasting and praying, they lay hands on Barnabas and Saul and send them off. The laying on of hands is both commissioning and blessing. The Antioch church releases its best leaders for a wider mission -- generosity that will transform the world.

thenVsNow.then: The Antioch church sent out its two most gifted leaders -- Barnabas and Saul -- rather than keeping them for local ministry. This required extraordinary faith and generosity. They prioritized the mission beyond their walls over their own institutional needs.
thenVsNow.now: Churches that invest their best people and resources in outward mission rather than internal programming follow the Antioch model. The instinct to hoard talent for the home congregation works against the pattern established here.

verses.text: So, being sent out by the Holy Spirit, they went down to Seleucia. From there they sailed to Cyprus.
verses.explainer: Sent out by the Holy Spirit, they go to Seleucia (the port of Antioch) and sail to Cyprus. Barnabas is from Cyprus, so they begin with familiar territory. The mission strategy combines spiritual leading with practical wisdom.

verses.text: When they were at Salamis, they proclaimed God’s word in the Jewish synagogues. They had also John as their attendant.
verses.explainer: In Salamis, on the eastern coast of Cyprus, they proclaim the word of God in the Jewish synagogues. This becomes Paul's standard pattern: start at the synagogue, where the Old Testament is read and discussed, and show how Jesus fulfills it. John Mark accompanies them as an assistant.

verses.text: When they had gone through the island to Paphos, they found a certain sorcerer, a false prophet, a Jew, whose name was Bar Jesus,
verses.explainer: They travel through the entire island to Paphos on the western coast. There they encounter Bar-Jesus, a Jewish false prophet and sorcerer who is an attendant of the proconsul, Sergius Paulus.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': 'Paphos and Sergius Paulus', 'explanation': "Paphos was the capital of the Roman province of Cyprus, seat of the proconsul. Sergius Paulus was the governor of the province. Inscriptional evidence from Antioch of Pisidia mentions a family named Sergius Paullus, possibly confirming Luke's account. Roman officials sometimes kept court astrologers and magicians as advisors."}

verses.text: who was with the proconsul, Sergius Paulus, a man of understanding. This man summoned Barnabas and Saul, and sought to hear the word of God.
verses.explainer: The proconsul Sergius Paulus, described as 'an intelligent man,' summons Barnabas and Saul because he wants to hear the word of God. A Roman governor seeks out the gospel. The mission is already reaching centers of power.

verses.text: But Elymas the sorcerer (for so is his name by interpretation) withstood them, seeking to turn the proconsul away from the faith.
verses.explainer: Elymas the sorcerer (the Greek form of his name) opposes them, trying to turn the proconsul away from the faith. He perceives the missionaries as a threat to his influence. The confrontation is a power encounter between the Spirit and the occult.

verses.text: But Saul, who is also called Paul, filled with the Holy Spirit, fastened his eyes on him,
verses.explainer: Saul, 'also called Paul,' is filled with the Holy Spirit and looks directly at Elymas. This is the first use of the name 'Paul' in Acts. From this point, Luke consistently uses the Roman name. The shift may reflect Paul's increasing engagement with the Gentile world.

verses.text: and said, “Full of all deceit and all cunning, you son of the devil, you enemy of all righteousness, will you not cease to pervert the right ways of the Lord?
verses.explainer: Paul calls Elymas 'son of the devil, enemy of all righteousness, full of all deceit and villainy.' The language is severe because the stakes are high -- Elymas is attempting to prevent a Roman governor from hearing the gospel.

verses.text:
Now, behold, the hand of the Lord is on you, and you will be blind, not seeing the sun for a season!”
Immediately a mist and darkness fell on him. He went around seeking someone to lead him by the hand.
verses.explainer: Paul pronounces temporary blindness on Elymas. The sorcerer gropes for someone to lead him by the hand. The irony echoes Paul's own experience on the Damascus road -- the man who opposes the gospel is blinded, just as Paul himself was before his conversion.

verses.text: Then the proconsul, when he saw what was done, believed, being astonished at the teaching of the Lord.
verses.explainer: The proconsul sees what happened and believes, 'being astonished at the teaching of the Lord.' Luke notes that it was the teaching, not just the miracle, that converted Sergius Paulus. Power demonstrations without proclamation are insufficient.

verses.text: Now Paul and his company set sail from Paphos, and came to Perga in Pamphylia. John departed from them and returned to Jerusalem.
verses.explainer: Paul and his companions sail from Paphos to Perga in Pamphylia. Here John Mark leaves them and returns to Jerusalem. Luke does not explain why, but the desertion will later cause a sharp disagreement between Paul and Barnabas.

thenVsNow.then: John Mark's departure may have been due to homesickness, fear of the dangerous interior journey, or disagreement with the mission's direction (the increasing focus on Gentiles). Whatever the reason, Paul viewed it as a serious failure of commitment.
thenVsNow.now: Mark's failure and later restoration (2 Timothy 4:11) is one of the most encouraging stories in the New Testament. The young man who quit became the author of a Gospel. Failure in ministry does not have to be the final word.

verses.text: But they, passing on from Perga, came to Antioch of Pisidia. They went into the synagogue on the Sabbath day, and sat down.
verses.explainer: Paul and Barnabas travel from Perga to Pisidian Antioch and enter the synagogue on the Sabbath. Pisidian Antioch was a Roman colony in the highlands of south-central Turkey -- a significant administrative center.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': 'Pisidian Antioch', 'explanation': "Not to be confused with Antioch of Syria. This was a Roman colony in the province of Galatia, situated on a major east-west trade route. As a colony, many residents were Roman military veterans. The city had a Jewish community large enough to support a synagogue, which became Paul's initial contact point."}

verses.text: After the reading of the law and the prophets, the rulers of the synagogue sent to them, saying, “Brothers, if you have any word of exhortation for the people, speak.”
verses.explainer: After the reading of the Law and the Prophets, the synagogue rulers invite them: 'Brothers, if you have any word of encouragement for the people, say it.' Visitors were routinely invited to speak. Paul seizes the opportunity.

verses.text: Paul stood up, and beckoning with his hand said, “Men of Israel, and you who fear God, listen.
verses.explainer: Paul stands, motions with his hand, and begins: 'Men of Israel, and you who fear God, listen.' He addresses two audiences: ethnic Jews and God-fearing Gentiles. Both groups are present in the synagogue.

verses.text: The God of this people chose our fathers, and exalted the people when they stayed as aliens in the land of Egypt, and with an uplifted arm, he led them out of it.
verses.explainer: Paul begins with the exodus: 'The God of this people Israel chose our fathers and made the people great during their stay in the land of Egypt, and with uplifted arm he led them out.' Like Stephen's speech, Paul's sermon retells Israel's history, building toward Jesus.

verses.text: For a period of about forty years he put up with them in the wilderness.
verses.explainer: God 'put up with them' in the wilderness for about forty years. The Greek word can also mean 'carried them' or 'bore with them.' God endured Israel's wilderness rebellions with patient perseverance.

verses.text: When he had destroyed seven nations in the land of Canaan, he gave them their land for an inheritance, for about four hundred fifty years.
verses.explainer: God destroyed seven nations in Canaan and gave their land to Israel as an inheritance. Paul compresses centuries of history in a few words, moving quickly toward the point of the sermon.

verses.text: After these things he gave them judges until Samuel the prophet.
verses.explainer: God gave them judges until Samuel the prophet. The period of the judges was chaotic -- 'everyone did what was right in their own eyes.' Paul passes over this era quickly.

verses.text: Afterward they asked for a king, and God gave to them Saul the son of Kish, a man of the tribe of Benjamin, for forty years.
verses.explainer: The people asked for a king, and God gave them Saul the son of Kish, from the tribe of Benjamin, for forty years. Paul mentions his namesake -- the first king of Israel, who started well but ended badly.

verses.text: When he had removed him, he raised up David to be their king, to whom he also testified, ‘I have found David the son of Jesse, a man after my heart, who will do all my will.’
verses.explainer: God removed Saul and raised up David, of whom he testified: 'I have found in David the son of Jesse a man after my heart, who will do all my will.' David is the pivot point -- the king through whom God's promises flow to Jesus.

verses.text: From this man’s offspring, God has brought salvation to Israel according to his promise,
verses.explainer: 'Of this man's offspring God has brought to Israel a Savior, Jesus, as he promised.' The entire historical survey has been building to this moment: Israel's story finds its climax and purpose in Jesus.

verses.text: before his coming, when John had first preached the baptism of repentance to Israel.
verses.explainer: Before Jesus' coming, John the Baptist proclaimed a baptism of repentance to all the people of Israel. John is the forerunner -- the last prophet who points to the Messiah.

verses.text: As John was fulfilling his course, he said, ‘What do you suppose that I am? I am not he. But behold, one comes after me, the sandals of whose feet I am not worthy to untie.’
verses.explainer: As John was finishing his course, he said: 'What do you suppose that I am? I am not he. But after me one is coming whose sandals I am not worthy to untie.' John deflects attention from himself to Jesus.

verses.text: Brothers, children of the stock of Abraham, and those among you who fear God, the word of this salvation is sent out to you.
verses.explainer: Paul addresses the audience directly: 'Brothers, sons of the family of Abraham, and those among you who fear God, to us has been sent the message of this salvation.' The message is for both Jews and God-fearers.

verses.text: For those who dwell in Jerusalem, and their rulers, because they didn’t know him, nor the voices of the prophets which are read every Sabbath, fulfilled them by condemning him.
verses.explainer: The residents of Jerusalem and their rulers did not recognize Jesus or understand the prophets read every Sabbath, and they fulfilled the prophecies by condemning him. Ignorance, not malice, drove the crucifixion -- but ignorance does not nullify responsibility.

verses.text: Though they found no cause for death, they still asked Pilate to have him killed.
verses.explainer: Though they found no charge worthy of death, they asked Pilate to have him executed. The injustice of the proceedings is emphasized -- Jesus was killed without legitimate cause.

verses.text: When they had fulfilled all things that were written about him, they took him down from the tree, and laid him in a tomb.
verses.explainer: When they had carried out everything written about him, they took him down from the tree and laid him in a tomb. Even the burial fulfills prophetic expectations. 'The tree' again invokes Deuteronomy 21:23.

verses.text: But God raised him from the dead,
verses.explainer: 'But God raised him from the dead.' The three-word reversal that defines the gospel. They killed him; God raised him. Human verdict: guilty. Divine verdict: alive.

verses.text: and he was seen for many days by those who came up with him from Galilee to Jerusalem, who are his witnesses to the people.
verses.explainer: For many days he appeared to those who had come with him from Galilee to Jerusalem, and they are now his witnesses to the people. The resurrection is supported by multiple witnesses over an extended period, not a single fleeting appearance.

verses.text: We bring you good news of the promise made to the fathers,
verses.explainer: 'We bring you the good news that what God promised to the fathers, he has fulfilled to us their children by raising Jesus.' The resurrection is not just a miracle -- it is the fulfillment of God's ancient promises. Everything in Israel's story pointed here.

verses.text:
that God has fulfilled the same to us, their children, in that he raised up Jesus. As it is also written in the second psalm,
‘You are my Son.
Today I have become your father.’
verses.explainer: Paul quotes Psalm 2:7: 'You are my Son; today I have begotten you.' The resurrection is the moment when Jesus is publicly declared to be God's Son with power. The psalm of royal enthronement finds its ultimate fulfillment.

verses.text: “Concerning that he raised him up from the dead, now no more to return to corruption, he has spoken thus: ‘I will give you the holy and sure blessings of David.’
verses.explainer: God raised Jesus 'no more to return to corruption.' Paul distinguishes Jesus' resurrection from resuscitations like Lazarus -- Jesus was raised to a new kind of life that does not decay. He quotes Isaiah: 'I will give you the holy and sure blessings of David.'

verses.text: Therefore he says also in another psalm, ‘You will not allow your Holy One to see decay.’
verses.explainer: Paul cites Psalm 16:10: 'You will not let your Holy One see corruption.' This is the same passage Peter used at Pentecost. Paul and Peter independently arrive at the same scriptural argument for the resurrection.

verses.text: For David, after he had in his own generation served the counsel of God, fell asleep, and was laid with his fathers, and saw decay.
verses.explainer: David, after serving God's purpose in his own generation, died and was buried and 'saw corruption.' David's body decayed. The psalm cannot refer to David.

verses.text: But he whom God raised up saw no decay.
verses.explainer: 'But he whom God raised up did not see corruption.' Jesus' body did not decay because he was raised. The psalm finds its fulfillment not in David but in David's greater descendant.

verses.text: Be it known to you therefore, brothers, that through this man is proclaimed to you remission of sins,
verses.explainer: 'Through this man forgiveness of sins is proclaimed to you.' The sermon pivots from history to application. The resurrection is not just a fact to be believed but a reality that offers forgiveness.

verses.text: and by him everyone who believes is justified from all things, from which you could not be justified by the law of Moses.
verses.explainer: 'By him everyone who believes is freed from everything from which you could not be freed by the law of Moses.' This is Paul's doctrine of justification by faith in embryonic form. The law could not free people from sin; Jesus can.

thenVsNow.then: Paul's claim that the law of Moses could not fully justify was radical in a synagogue setting. Faithful Jews believed that Torah observance was the path to right standing with God. Paul argues that the law was good but insufficient -- only Jesus provides complete freedom.
thenVsNow.now: The relationship between law-keeping and grace remains a central tension in Christianity. Paul's insistence that faith in Jesus accomplishes what moral effort alone cannot is both liberating and controversial -- it challenges every system that measures acceptance by performance.

verses.text: Beware therefore, lest that come on you which is spoken in the prophets:
verses.explainer: Paul warns: 'Beware, lest what is said in the prophets should come about.' The opportunity carries a warning -- ignoring the gospel has consequences.

He quotes Habakkuk 1:5: 'Look, you scoffers, be astounded and perish; for I am doing a work in your days, a work that you will not believe, even if one tells it to you.' God's work in Jesus is so extraordinary that some will refuse to believe it even when confronted with the evidence.

verses.text: So when the Jews went out of the synagogue, the Gentiles begged that these words might be preached to them the next Sabbath.
verses.explainer: As Paul and Barnabas leave the synagogue, the people beg them to return next Sabbath to speak again. The initial response is enthusiastic.

verses.text: Now when the synagogue broke up, many of the Jews and of the devout proselytes followed Paul and Barnabas; who, speaking to them, urged them to continue in the grace of God.
verses.explainer: After the meeting, many Jews and devout converts follow Paul and Barnabas, who urge them to continue in the grace of God. The word 'grace' is central -- it is God's unmerited favor, not human achievement.

verses.text: The next Sabbath, almost the whole city was gathered together to hear the word of God.
verses.explainer: The next Sabbath, almost the entire city gathers to hear the word of the Lord. Paul's sermon has created a sensation. The turnout is massive -- Jews, God-fearers, and curious pagans alike.

verses.text: But when the Jews saw the multitudes, they were filled with jealousy, and contradicted the things which were spoken by Paul, and blasphemed.
verses.explainer: When the Jewish leaders see the crowds, they are 'filled with jealousy' and begin contradicting Paul, reviling him. The opposition is driven not by theology but by jealousy -- the same motivation that sold Joseph into Egypt and killed Jesus.

thenVsNow.then: The synagogue leaders' jealousy was provoked by the massive Gentile response. As long as Paul was attracting Jews, they tolerated him. When the whole city came -- including non-Jews -- the synagogue's exclusive claim to God's message was threatened.
thenVsNow.now: Religious jealousy -- the resentment that arises when God appears to bless people outside our group -- remains a persistent problem. Churches that celebrate growth in other congregations or traditions practice a generosity that the Pisidian Antioch synagogue could not muster.

verses.text: Paul and Barnabas spoke out boldly, and said, “It was necessary that God’s word should be spoken to you first. Since indeed you thrust it from you, and judge yourselves unworthy of eternal life, behold, we turn to the Gentiles.
verses.explainer: Paul and Barnabas speak boldly: 'It was necessary that the word of God be spoken first to you. Since you thrust it aside and judge yourselves unworthy of eternal life, behold, we are turning to the Gentiles.' This is a watershed moment -- the explicit turn from Jewish to Gentile mission.

verses.text:
For so has the Lord commanded us, saying,
‘I have set you as a light for the Gentiles,
that you should bring salvation to the uttermost parts of the earth.’”
verses.explainer: They cite Isaiah 49:6: 'I have made you a light for the Gentiles, that you may bring salvation to the ends of the earth.' The prophets always intended for Israel's blessing to reach all nations. Paul is not abandoning the Jewish mission but fulfilling its original purpose.

verses.text: As the Gentiles heard this, they were glad, and glorified the word of God. As many as were appointed to eternal life believed.
verses.explainer: When the Gentiles hear this, they are glad and glorify the word of the Lord, 'and as many as were appointed to eternal life believed.' Luke attributes conversion to divine appointment while recording human joy and response.

verses.text: The Lord’s word was spread abroad throughout all the region.
verses.explainer: The word of the Lord spreads through the whole region. The message does not stay in the synagogue or even in the city -- it fans out across the surrounding territory.

verses.text: But the Jews stirred up the devout and prominent women and the chief men of the city, and stirred up a persecution against Paul and Barnabas, and threw them out of their borders.
verses.explainer: The Jewish leaders incite 'devout women of high standing and the leading men of the city' and stir up persecution. They use social and political connections to drive Paul and Barnabas out. The opposition comes through established power networks.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': 'Devout women of high standing', 'explanation': "Many wealthy and prominent women in Roman cities were attracted to Judaism as God-fearers. The synagogue leaders leveraged these women's social influence and their connections to male civic leaders to create a political coalition against the missionaries. The persecution was orchestrated through existing power structures."}

verses.text: But they shook off the dust of their feet against them, and came to Iconium.
verses.explainer: Paul and Barnabas shake the dust off their feet against them and go to Iconium. Shaking off dust was a Jewish gesture of disassociation -- treating a place as pagan territory. Jesus instructed his disciples to do this when rejected.

verses.text: The disciples were filled with joy and with the Holy Spirit.
verses.explainer: The disciples in Pisidian Antioch are 'filled with joy and with the Holy Spirit.' The missionaries leave, but the church remains and thrives. The departure of the founders does not destroy the community -- the Spirit sustains it.

explainer.summary: Paul and Barnabas continue through Iconium, Lystra, and Derbe, completing the first missionary journey. In Iconium they face opposition and flee. In Lystra, Paul heals a lame man, and the crowd tries to worship the missionaries as the gods Hermes and Zeus. Paul barely restrains them. Then Jewish opponents from Pisidian Antioch and Iconium arrive, turn the crowd against Paul, stone him, and leave him for dead. He survives, goes to Derbe, then retraces his steps, strengthening new churches and appointing elders. The team returns to Antioch of Syria and reports that God has 'opened a door of faith to the Gentiles.'
themes: Suffering as part of the mission
Reporting back to the sending church
explainer.contextInBook: Chapter 14 completes the first missionary journey and establishes the pattern for Paul's ministry: preach, plant, suffer, strengthen, and move on. The appointment of elders shows Paul's concern for sustainable, local leadership rather than permanent dependence on traveling missionaries.

wordStudies.translatedAs: Mature community leaders appointed to oversee loca
wordStudies.meaning: Mature community leaders appointed to oversee local churches. The title comes from Jewish synagogue governance. Paul and Barnabas appoint elders in every new church, creating a leadership structure that can function without apostolic presence. These are not young leaders but tested, experienced believers.

verses.text: In Iconium, they entered together into the synagogue of the Jews, and so spoke that a great multitude both of Jews and of Greeks believed.
verses.explainer: In Iconium, Paul and Barnabas enter the Jewish synagogue and speak so effectively that 'a great number of both Jews and Greeks believed.' The pattern holds: start at the synagogue, preach persuasively, and see both Jews and Gentiles respond.

verses.text: But the disbelieving Jews stirred up and embittered the souls of the Gentiles against the brothers.
verses.explainer: But the 'unbelieving Jews stirred up the Gentiles and poisoned their minds against the brothers.' The opposition follows the same playbook as in Pisidian Antioch -- influencing the wider population through social networks.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': 'Iconium', 'explanation': "Modern Konya in central Turkey, Iconium was a prosperous city on the major Roman road (Via Sebaste) connecting the coast to the interior. Its population was a mix of native Phrygians, Greeks, Romans, and Jews. The city's mixed demographics made it fertile ground for both the gospel and opposition."}

verses.text: Therefore they stayed there a long time, speaking boldly in the Lord, who testified to the word of his grace, granting signs and wonders to be done by their hands.
verses.explainer: Paul and Barnabas remain 'a long time, speaking boldly for the Lord,' who confirms their message with 'signs and wonders.' They do not flee at the first sign of trouble. They stay, and God validates their perseverance with miraculous confirmation.

verses.text: But the multitude of the city was divided. Part sided with the Jews, and part with the apostles.
verses.explainer: The city is divided: some side with the Jews, some with the apostles. Luke calls Paul and Barnabas 'apostles' -- the only place outside the Twelve where this title is clearly applied. Their missionary commissioning gives them apostolic authority.

verses.text: When some of both the Gentiles and the Jews, with their rulers, made a violent attempt to mistreat and stone them,
verses.explainer: A plot develops among both Gentiles and Jews, together with their rulers, to abuse and stone the missionaries. The opposition has become a coalition crossing ethnic lines -- Jews and Gentiles unite against the gospel.

verses.text: they became aware of it, and fled to the cities of Lycaonia, Lystra, Derbe, and the surrounding region.
verses.explainer: Learning of the plot, Paul and Barnabas flee to Lystra and Derbe, cities of Lycaonia, and the surrounding region. Retreat when facing certain death is not cowardice -- it is strategic. Jesus himself told his disciples to flee persecution when necessary.

verses.text: There they preached the Good News.
verses.explainer: They continue to preach the gospel in Lycaonia. Flight does not silence them -- it simply moves the preaching to a new location. The pattern of Acts: persecution scatters the gospel, it does not suppress it.

verses.text: At Lystra a certain man sat, impotent in his feet, a cripple from his mother’s womb, who never had walked.
verses.explainer: In Lystra there is a man crippled from birth who has never walked. The scene deliberately echoes the healing at the Beautiful Gate in chapter 3 -- same condition, different city, different apostle.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': 'Lystra', 'explanation': "A small Roman colony in the Lycaonian region of southern Galatia. Unlike Pisidian Antioch and Iconium, Lystra had no synagogue (at least none is mentioned). Paul is preaching to a thoroughly pagan audience. Timothy's hometown (16:1) -- his mother Eunice and grandmother Lois may have been among Paul's converts here."}

verses.text: He was listening to Paul speaking, who, fastening eyes on him, and seeing that he had faith to be made whole,
verses.explainer: The man listens to Paul speaking. Paul looks directly at him and sees that he has faith to be healed. Paul perceives something in the man -- an openness, a readiness -- that signals the moment is right.

verses.text: said with a loud voice, “Stand upright on your feet!” He leaped up and walked.
verses.explainer: Paul calls out in a loud voice: 'Stand upright on your feet!' The man springs up and begins walking. The healing is instantaneous and complete, just like the Beautiful Gate miracle. The gospel is authenticated by the same power in pagan Lystra as in Jewish Jerusalem.

verses.text: When the multitude saw what Paul had done, they lifted up their voice, saying in the language of Lycaonia, “The gods have come down to us in the likeness of men!”
verses.explainer: When the crowds see what Paul has done, they shout in the Lycaonian language: 'The gods have come down to us in the likeness of men!' They interpret the miracle through their own religious framework -- not the God of Israel but the Greek pantheon.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': 'Lycaonian language', 'explanation': "The local population spoke Lycaonian -- an indigenous Anatolian language distinct from Greek or Latin. Paul and Barnabas may not have immediately understood what the crowd was saying. The use of the local language suggests the crowd's reaction was spontaneous and deeply rooted in local religious tradition."}

verses.text: They called Barnabas “Jupiter”, and Paul “Mercury”, because he was the chief speaker.
verses.explainer: They call Barnabas 'Zeus' and Paul 'Hermes,' because Paul was the chief speaker. Zeus was the king of the gods; Hermes was his messenger and the god of eloquence. The identification follows Greek religious logic perfectly.

thenVsNow.then: Local legend in Lystra told of Zeus and Hermes visiting the region disguised as humans (recorded by the Roman poet Ovid in the story of Philemon and Baucis). The townspeople, remembering that legend, were terrified of failing to honor the 'gods' again and being punished for it.
thenVsNow.now: The Lystra episode illustrates how people filter the gospel through their existing categories. Every culture has frameworks that can distort the message -- in Lystra it was Greek mythology; in other contexts it might be political ideology, consumerism, or therapeutic self-help.

verses.text: The priest of Jupiter, whose temple was in front of their city, brought oxen and garlands to the gates, and would have made a sacrifice along with the multitudes.
verses.explainer: The priest of the temple of Zeus, which stood just outside the city, brings oxen and garlands to the gates, preparing to offer sacrifices to Paul and Barnabas. The attempted worship is organized and official -- not just crowd enthusiasm but priestly ritual.

verses.text: But when the apostles, Barnabas and Paul, heard of it, they tore their clothes, and sprang into the multitude, crying out,
verses.explainer: When the apostles Barnabas and Paul hear of it, they tear their garments and rush out into the crowd. Tearing garments was the Jewish expression of horror at blasphemy. They are physically distressed by the crowd's misidentification.

verses.text: “Men, why are you doing these things? We also are men of like passions with you, and bring you good news, that you should turn from these vain things to the living God, who made the sky, the earth, the sea, and all that is in them;
verses.explainer: They cry out: 'Men, why are you doing these things? We are also men, of like nature with you, and we bring you good news, that you should turn from these vain things to a living God, who made the heaven and the earth and the sea and everything in them.' This is Paul's first recorded sermon to a purely pagan audience -- no Old Testament citations, no Jewish history, just the Creator God.

thenVsNow.then: Paul's approach at Lystra differs completely from his synagogue sermons. With no shared Scripture, he starts with natural theology -- God the Creator. He meets his audience where they are, not where he would like them to be.
thenVsNow.now: The shift from scriptural argument (in synagogues) to natural theology (in pagan settings) demonstrates contextual communication. The content of the gospel does not change, but the starting point and the language do. Effective communication requires understanding the audience's existing framework.

verses.text: who in the generations gone by allowed all the nations to walk in their own ways.
verses.explainer: In past generations, God 'allowed all the nations to walk in their own ways.' Paul acknowledges that Gentile ignorance of the true God has been tolerated until now. The age of ignorance is ending; the gospel calls for a new response.

verses.text: Yet he didn’t leave himself without witness, in that he did good and gave you rains from the sky and fruitful seasons, filling our hearts with food and gladness.”
verses.explainer: Yet God 'did not leave himself without witness, for he did good by giving you rains from heaven and fruitful seasons, satisfying your hearts with food and gladness.' Even without Scripture, creation testifies to God's goodness. The rains and harvests are silent sermons.

verses.text: Even saying these things, they hardly stopped the multitudes from making a sacrifice to them.
verses.explainer: Even with these words, they 'scarcely restrained the people from offering sacrifice to them.' The crowd's desire to worship Paul and Barnabas is almost unstoppable. The cultural momentum toward polytheistic worship is powerful.

verses.text: But some Jews from Antioch and Iconium came there, and having persuaded the multitudes, they stoned Paul, and dragged him out of the city, supposing that he was dead.
verses.explainer: Jews arrive from Pisidian Antioch and Iconium and win over the crowds. They stone Paul and drag him out of the city, supposing he is dead. The same crowd that wanted to worship Paul as a god now tries to kill him. The swing from adulation to violence is breathtaking.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': 'Stoning', 'explanation': 'Stoning was the Jewish method of execution for blasphemy and other capital offenses. The Jews from Pisidian Antioch and Iconium traveled over 100 miles to pursue Paul, demonstrating the intensity of their opposition. They convinced a pagan crowd to use a Jewish execution method -- a remarkable coalition of hostility.'}

verses.text: But as the disciples stood around him, he rose up, and entered into the city. On the next day he went out with Barnabas to Derbe.
verses.explainer: The disciples gather around Paul, and he rises up and enters the city. The next day he goes with Barnabas to Derbe. Whether Paul's survival is miraculous or simply resilient, it is extraordinary. A man left for dead gets up and continues the mission.

verses.text: When they had preached the Good News to that city, and had made many disciples, they returned to Lystra, Iconium, and Antioch,
verses.explainer: They preach the gospel in Derbe and make 'many disciples.' Then, instead of continuing east to Tarsus (Paul's hometown and a shorter route), they retrace their steps through hostile territory -- Lystra, Iconium, and Pisidian Antioch -- to strengthen the new churches.

thenVsNow.then: Paul and Barnabas chose the dangerous route back through cities where they had been persecuted rather than the safe route home. They prioritized the new believers' discipleship over their own safety. Leadership in the early church meant returning to places that wanted you dead.
thenVsNow.now: The decision to retrace their steps through hostile territory demonstrates a pastoral commitment that challenges easy discipleship. Church planting without follow-up produces orphan congregations. Paul models the principle that new believers need ongoing investment.

verses.text: confirming the souls of the disciples, exhorting them to continue in the faith, and that through many afflictions we must enter into God’s Kingdom.
verses.explainer: They strengthen the disciples' souls, encouraging them to continue in the faith, and saying: 'Through many tribulations we must enter the kingdom of God.' Paul does not promise an easy road. He tells new converts that suffering is normative, not exceptional.

verses.text: When they had appointed elders for them in every assembly, and had prayed with fasting, they commended them to the Lord, on whom they had believed.
verses.explainer: They appoint elders in every church, pray with fasting, and commit them to the Lord in whom they have believed. The missionary team creates sustainable local leadership before leaving. The churches will be led by their own members, not by visiting apostles.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': 'Appointing elders', 'explanation': "The Greek word cheirotoneo originally meant 'to stretch out the hand' -- possibly indicating election by show of hands. The elder system adapted Jewish synagogue governance for the new Christian communities. Multiple elders (plural) in each church ensured collective leadership rather than one-person rule."}

verses.text: They passed through Pisidia, and came to Pamphylia.
verses.explainer: They pass through Pisidia and come to Pamphylia. The journey retraces the outbound route in reverse, returning toward the coast.

verses.text: When they had spoken the word in Perga, they went down to Attalia.
verses.explainer: They speak the word in Perga -- the city where John Mark left them -- then go down to Attalia, the main port of Pamphylia.

verses.text: From there they sailed to Antioch, from where they had been committed to the grace of God for the work which they had fulfilled.
verses.explainer: From Attalia they sail to Antioch of Syria, 'where they had been commended to the grace of God for the work that they had fulfilled.' They return to the church that sent them. The mission began and ends with the sending community.

verses.text: When they had arrived, and had gathered the assembly together, they reported all the things that God had done with them, and that he had opened a door of faith to the nations.
verses.explainer: They gather the church and report 'all that God had done with them, and how he had opened a door of faith to the Gentiles.' The phrase is carefully chosen: God opened the door. The missionaries walked through it, but God was the one who unlocked it.

thenVsNow.then: The missionary report to the Antioch church established a pattern of accountability and celebration. The missionaries were not freelancers; they were sent by and accountable to a local church. Their report emphasizes what God did, not their own accomplishments.
thenVsNow.now: The practice of missionaries reporting back to their sending church remains standard in most mission organizations. Paul's model -- sent by a church, accountable to a church, reporting to a church -- provides the organizational framework that sustains long-term mission work.

verses.text: They stayed there with the disciples for a long time.
verses.explainer: They stay in Antioch 'no little time' with the disciples. After the intensity of the journey -- stoning, opposition, miraculous healings, near-death experiences -- they rest and reintegrate with their home community. Even apostles need recovery.

explainer.summary: The Jerusalem Council is the most important church meeting in the New Testament. The question: Must Gentile believers be circumcised and follow the law of Moses to be saved? After fierce debate, Peter recounts his experience with Cornelius, Paul and Barnabas report what God has done among the Gentiles, and James renders the decisive verdict: Gentiles do not need to become Jews. The council sends a letter with minimal requirements. Afterward, Paul and Barnabas split over John Mark -- Barnabas takes Mark to Cyprus while Paul chooses Silas and begins the second missionary journey.
themes: The unity of Jewish and Gentile believers
explainer.contextInBook: The Jerusalem Council resolves the fundamental identity crisis of early Christianity: Is this a Jewish sect or a universal movement? The answer -- universal, with Jewish roots but not Jewish requirements -- sets the trajectory for the rest of Acts and the entire future of Christianity.

wordStudies.translatedAs: A wooden frame placed on the necks of oxen for plo
wordStudies.meaning: A wooden frame placed on the necks of oxen for plowing. Peter uses it metaphorically for the burden of the law -- a weight that 'neither our fathers nor we have been able to bear.' The image suggests that requiring Gentiles to follow the entire Torah would be oppressive rather than liberating.

wordStudies.translatedAs: To hold oneself away from, to refrain
wordStudies.meaning: To hold oneself away from, to refrain. The council's letter asks Gentile believers to abstain from four things associated with pagan worship. These are not salvation requirements but practical guidelines for maintaining fellowship between Jewish and Gentile believers.

verses.text: Some men came down from Judea and taught the brothers, “Unless you are circumcised after the custom of Moses, you can’t be saved.”
verses.explainer: Some men come from Judea to Antioch and teach the brothers: 'Unless you are circumcised according to the custom of Moses, you cannot be saved.' This is the crisis that triggers the council -- salvation itself is at stake.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': 'Circumcision controversy', 'explanation': "Circumcision was the sign of God's covenant with Abraham (Genesis 17). For centuries, it was the non-negotiable entry requirement for male converts to Judaism. Requiring it of Gentile believers would effectively mean they must become Jews before they could be Christians. The controversy was about the very nature of salvation: Is it by grace through faith, or by grace plus cultural compliance?"}

verses.text: Therefore when Paul and Barnabas had no small discord and discussion with them, they appointed Paul and Barnabas, and some others of them, to go up to Jerusalem to the apostles and elders about this question.
verses.explainer: Paul and Barnabas have 'no small dissension and debate' with these men. The Antioch church appoints Paul, Barnabas, and others to go to Jerusalem to resolve the question with the apostles and elders. The dispute is too significant for a local church to settle alone.

verses.text: They, being sent on their way by the assembly, passed through both Phoenicia and Samaria, declaring the conversion of the Gentiles. They caused great joy to all the brothers.
verses.explainer: On the way to Jerusalem, they pass through Phoenicia and Samaria, reporting the conversion of the Gentiles and causing 'great joy among all the brothers.' The Gentile mission is celebrated outside Jerusalem even as it is contested within it.

verses.text: When they had come to Jerusalem, they were received by the assembly and the apostles and the elders, and they reported all things that God had done with them.
verses.explainer: In Jerusalem, the church, apostles, and elders welcome them, and Paul and Barnabas report 'all that God had done with them.' They frame the Gentile mission as God's work, not their own initiative.

verses.text: But some of the sect of the Pharisees who believed rose up, saying, “It is necessary to circumcise them, and to command them to keep the law of Moses.”
verses.explainer: Some believers who belong to the 'party of the Pharisees' insist: 'It is necessary to circumcise them and to order them to keep the law of Moses.' The opposition comes from within the church, from sincere believers with Pharisaic backgrounds who believe Torah observance is essential.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': 'Pharisaic Christians', 'explanation': "The Pharisees were the most popular and influential Jewish party, known for strict Torah observance and oral tradition. That Pharisees had become Christians shows the gospel's penetration into every Jewish faction. Their insistence on circumcision was theologically consistent with their pre-conversion worldview -- they had accepted Jesus as Messiah but had not yet rethought the role of the law."}

verses.text: The apostles and the elders were gathered together to see about this matter.
verses.explainer: The apostles and elders meet to consider the matter. This is a formal deliberative assembly -- the first church council. The decision reached here will have authority across all churches.

verses.text: When there had been much discussion, Peter rose up and said to them, “Brothers, you know that a good while ago God made a choice among you, that by my mouth the nations should hear the word of the Good News, and believe.
verses.explainer: After 'much debate,' Peter rises and reminds them of the Cornelius episode: God chose to give the gospel to the Gentiles through Peter, and the Gentiles believed. Peter's argument is based on what God has already done, not on what should be done theoretically.

thenVsNow.then: Peter's appeal to personal experience was not anti-intellectual -- it was a claim that God had already settled the question before the council met. When God gave the Spirit to uncircumcised Gentiles, he rendered the debate moot.
thenVsNow.now: The Jerusalem Council established a pattern for resolving theological disputes: appeal to Scripture, to apostolic testimony, and to the evidence of God's work in the world. Most Christian traditions still use some version of this threefold approach.

verses.text: God, who knows the heart, testified about them, giving them the Holy Spirit, just like he did to us.
verses.explainer: God, who knows the heart, 'bore witness to them, by giving them the Holy Spirit just as he did to us.' God looked at the Gentiles' hearts, not their foreskins. The Spirit is the evidence of acceptance, not any physical marker.

verses.text: He made no distinction between us and them, cleansing their hearts by faith.
verses.explainer: God 'made no distinction between us and them, having cleansed their hearts by faith.' No distinction -- this phrase demolishes the Jewish-Gentile boundary at its foundation. If God makes no distinction, neither can the church.

verses.text: Now therefore why do you tempt God, that you should put a yoke on the neck of the disciples which neither our fathers nor we were able to bear?
verses.explainer: Peter asks: 'Why are you putting God to the test by placing a yoke on the neck of the disciples that neither our fathers nor we have been able to bear?' Peter admits that even Jews could not fully keep the law. Requiring Gentiles to do so is testing God by adding to what he requires.

verses.text:
But we believe that we are saved through the grace of the Lord Jesus,
 just as they are.”
verses.explainer: 'We believe that we will be saved through the grace of the Lord Jesus, just as they will.' Peter puts Jews and Gentiles on exactly the same footing. Jews are not saved by the law plus Jesus; they are saved by grace alone, just like the Gentiles.

verses.text: All the multitude kept silence, and they listened to Barnabas and Paul reporting what signs and wonders God had done among the nations through them.
verses.explainer: The assembly falls silent and listens to Barnabas and Paul narrate 'the signs and wonders God had done through them among the Gentiles.' The silence indicates respect and attention. The evidence is compelling.

thenVsNow.then: The narrative evidence of God's work among the Gentiles was as important as the scriptural argument. The early church made decisions based on three inputs: Scripture, apostolic testimony, and the observable work of the Spirit. All three converged at the Jerusalem Council.
thenVsNow.now: The interplay of Scripture, tradition, and experience in theological decision-making continues to shape Christian debate. The Jerusalem Council modeled a process that takes all three seriously: James cites Scripture, Peter recounts experience, and Paul and Barnabas report evidence of God's work.

verses.text: After they were silent, James answered, “Brothers, listen to me.
verses.explainer: After Paul and Barnabas finish, James speaks. As the brother of Jesus and the leader of the Jerusalem church, his opinion carries decisive weight. He addresses the assembly as a judge rendering a verdict.

verses.text: Simeon has reported how God first visited the nations, to take out of them a people for his name.
verses.explainer: James refers to Peter's testimony (using the Hebraic form 'Simeon') about how 'God first visited the Gentiles, to take from them a people for his name.' God is building a people that includes Gentiles -- this is not an accident but a divine plan.

verses.text: This agrees with the words of the prophets. As it is written,
verses.explainer: James turns to Scripture: 'The words of the prophets agree with this.' His argument will combine apostolic experience with prophetic authority. Scripture confirms what God is doing.

verses.text:
‘After these things I will return.
I will again build the tabernacle of David, which has fallen.
I will again build its ruins.
I will set it up,
verses.explainer: James quotes Amos 9:11-12: 'After this I will return and rebuild the dwelling of David that has fallen.' The restoration of David's house -- the Messiah's kingdom -- includes Gentiles. James reads the prophets as predicting Gentile inclusion.

The prophecy continues: 'that the remnant of mankind may seek the Lord, and all the Gentiles who are called by my name.' Gentiles are called by God's name -- they belong to him directly, not through the mediation of Jewish identity.

verses.text: All of God’s works are known to him from eternity.’
verses.explainer: James concludes the quotation with: 'known from of old' -- this has always been God's plan. Gentile inclusion is not a recent improvisation but an ancient purpose now being revealed.

verses.text: “Therefore my judgment is that we don’t trouble those from among the Gentiles who turn to God,
verses.explainer: James gives his judgment: 'We should not trouble those of the Gentiles who turn to God.' 'Not trouble' -- do not add burdens. The verb implies that requiring circumcision would be causing unnecessary difficulty for people God has already accepted.

verses.text: but that we write to them that they abstain from the pollution of idols, from sexual immorality, from what is strangled, and from blood.
verses.explainer: James proposes four restrictions: abstain from things polluted by idols, from sexual immorality, from what has been strangled, and from blood. These are minimal requirements designed to facilitate fellowship between Jewish and Gentile believers.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': 'The four prohibitions', 'explanation': "These four items likely correspond to the 'Noahide laws' that Judaism applied to Gentiles, or to practices associated with pagan temple worship. Idol food, sexual immorality, strangled animals, and blood consumption were all features of Greco-Roman pagan religion. Avoiding them meant a clean break from paganism and showed respect for Jewish sensibilities, enabling shared meals."}

verses.text: For Moses from generations of old has in every city those who preach him, being read in the synagogues every Sabbath.”
verses.explainer: James explains: 'Moses has had in every city those who proclaim him, for he is read every Sabbath in the synagogues.' The Jewish community is everywhere and sensitive to these issues. The four prohibitions show respect for Jewish neighbors and fellow believers.

verses.text: Then it seemed good to the apostles and the elders, with the whole assembly, to choose men out of their company, and send them to Antioch with Paul and Barnabas: Judas called Barsabbas, and Silas, chief men among the brothers.
verses.explainer: The apostles, elders, and the whole church decide to send representatives with Paul and Barnabas: Judas Barsabbas and Silas, 'leading men among the brothers.' The decision is communal, not autocratic.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': 'Church governance model', 'explanation': "The Jerusalem Council's decision-making process involved apostles (authoritative founders), elders (community leaders), and the whole church (the congregation). This three-tiered involvement ensured the decision had broad legitimacy. The delegates sent to deliver the letter were independent of Paul, further establishing the decision's authority."}

verses.text:
They wrote these things by their hand:
“The apostles, the elders, and the brothers, to the brothers who are of the Gentiles in Antioch, Syria, and Cilicia: greetings.
verses.explainer: They write a letter addressed to 'the brothers who are of the Gentiles in Antioch and Syria and Cilicia.' The letter format makes the decision official, portable, and replicable.

verses.text: Because we have heard that some who went out from us have troubled you with words, unsettling your souls, saying, ‘You must be circumcised and keep the law,’ to whom we gave no commandment;
verses.explainer: The letter acknowledges that the men who troubled the Gentiles with circumcision talk 'went out from us' but were not authorized. The Jerusalem church takes responsibility for the confusion and clarifies its position.

verses.text: it seemed good to us, having come to one accord, to choose out men and send them to you with our beloved Barnabas and Paul,
verses.explainer: The council chose delegates 'with one accord' -- the decision is unanimous. This is significant given the sharp debate that preceded it. The community has reached genuine consensus.

verses.text: men who have risked their lives for the name of our Lord Jesus Christ.
verses.explainer: They commend Barnabas and Paul as 'men who have risked their lives for the name of our Lord Jesus Christ.' The council vouches for the missionaries' character and courage.

verses.text: We have sent therefore Judas and Silas, who themselves will also tell you the same things by word of mouth.
verses.explainer: Judas and Silas will confirm the letter verbally. The written and oral communication reinforce each other -- no room for misunderstanding or misrepresentation.

verses.text: For it seemed good to the Holy Spirit, and to us, to lay no greater burden on you than these necessary things:
verses.explainer: 'It has seemed good to the Holy Spirit and to us to lay on you no greater burden than these requirements.' The remarkable phrase 'the Holy Spirit and us' claims divine guidance for the council's decision. The Spirit is a co-author of the ruling.

thenVsNow.then: The formula 'it seemed good to the Holy Spirit and to us' expresses the early church's conviction that their corporate discernment was guided by the Spirit. This was not arrogance but a claim that when the community deliberates faithfully, the Spirit participates in the process.
thenVsNow.now: The question of how church councils and communities can claim Spirit-guidance remains live in every tradition. The Jerusalem Council models a process: gathering diverse voices, considering evidence, consulting Scripture, debating honestly, and seeking consensus. The claim of Spirit-guidance is made humbly, not arrogantly.

verses.text: that you abstain from things sacrificed to idols, from blood, from things strangled, and from sexual immorality, from which if you keep yourselves, it will be well with you. Farewell.”
verses.explainer: The four requirements are listed again: abstain from idol food, blood, strangled things, and sexual immorality. 'If you keep yourselves from these, you will do well.' The tone is pastoral, not legalistic.

verses.text: So, when they were sent off, they came to Antioch. Having gathered the multitude together, they delivered the letter.
verses.explainer: The delegates go to Antioch, gather the congregation, and deliver the letter. The process is public and transparent -- the whole community hears the decision directly.

verses.text: When they had read it, they rejoiced over the encouragement.
verses.explainer: When they read the letter, 'they rejoiced because of its encouragement.' The burden of circumcision has been lifted. Gentile believers are fully accepted without becoming culturally Jewish. The relief is palpable.

verses.text: Judas and Silas, also being prophets themselves, encouraged the brothers with many words, and strengthened them.
verses.explainer: Judas and Silas, who are themselves prophets, encourage and strengthen the brothers with many words. They provide not just a letter but personal presence and encouragement.

verses.text: After they had spent some time there, they were sent back with greetings from the brothers to the apostles.
verses.explainer: After spending some time there, they are sent off in peace by the brothers back to Jerusalem. The visit ends well -- the relationship between Antioch and Jerusalem is strengthened.
Some manuscripts add that Silas decided to remain in Antioch. This may explain how Silas is available when Paul chooses him for the second journey.

verses.text: But Paul and Barnabas stayed in Antioch, teaching and preaching the word of the Lord, with many others also.
verses.explainer: Paul and Barnabas remain in Antioch, teaching and preaching the word of the Lord, along with many others. Antioch continues to be a center of vibrant Christian community and teaching.

verses.text: After some days Paul said to Barnabas, “Let’s return now and visit our brothers in every city in which we proclaimed the word of the Lord, to see how they are doing.”
verses.explainer: After some days, Paul says to Barnabas: 'Let us return and visit the brothers in every city where we proclaimed the word of the Lord, and see how they are.' Paul's pastoral concern drives the second journey -- he wants to check on the churches they planted.

verses.text: Barnabas planned to take John, who was called Mark, with them also.
verses.explainer: Barnabas wants to take John Mark along. He has not given up on the young man who deserted them in Pamphylia. Barnabas, true to his name, wants to give Mark a second chance.

verses.text: But Paul didn’t think that it was a good idea to take with them someone who had withdrawn from them in Pamphylia, and didn’t go with them to do the work.
verses.explainer: Paul disagrees sharply -- he does not think it wise to take someone who 'had withdrawn from them in Pamphylia and had not gone with them to the work.' Paul values reliability; Barnabas values restoration. Both are legitimate priorities.

verses.text: Then the contention grew so sharp that they separated from each other. Barnabas took Mark with him, and sailed away to Cyprus,
verses.explainer: The disagreement is so sharp that they separate. Barnabas takes Mark and sails to Cyprus; Paul chooses Silas and heads overland through Syria and Cilicia. The split is painful but productive -- now there are two missionary teams instead of one.

thenVsNow.then: Luke does not hide the conflict or take sides. Two godly leaders disagree and part ways. The church is not destroyed by the split -- it multiplies. Barnabas's investment in Mark will bear fruit: Mark later becomes valuable to Paul himself (2 Timothy 4:11) and writes a Gospel.
thenVsNow.now: The Paul-Barnabas split normalizes disagreement among Christians without catastrophizing it. Not every conflict requires a winner and a loser. Sometimes both parties are right about different things, and God uses the divergence to accomplish more than unity could have.

verses.text: but Paul chose Silas, and went out, being commended by the brothers to the grace of God.
verses.explainer: Paul chooses Silas and departs, commended by the brothers to the grace of the Lord. The second missionary journey begins. Silas brings both prophetic gifts and Roman citizenship -- valuable assets for the mission ahead.

verses.text: He went through Syria and Cilicia, strengthening the assemblies.
verses.explainer: Paul goes through Syria and Cilicia, strengthening the churches. He begins where his own ministry started -- his home region -- before pushing into new territory.

explainer.summary: Paul recruits Timothy in Lystra, then receives the Macedonian call -- a vision directing the mission to Europe. In Philippi, the first European church is born through three dramatically different conversions: Lydia (a wealthy businesswoman), a slave girl with a spirit of divination, and a Roman jailer. Paul and Silas are beaten and imprisoned, but an earthquake frees them. Instead of escaping, they stay and convert the jailer and his household. Paul then asserts his Roman citizenship, forcing the magistrates to publicly acknowledge their unjust treatment.
themes: The Macedonian call and divine guidance
Roman citizenship as a tool for justice
explainer.contextInBook: Chapter 16 is the beginning of Paul's European mission and contains the first 'we' passage (16:10), indicating that Luke himself has joined the missionary team. Philippi becomes one of Paul's most beloved churches -- the Philippian letter, written later from prison, radiates joy and deep mutual affection.

wordStudies.translatedAs: To cross over, to pass through
wordStudies.meaning: To cross over, to pass through. The Macedonian man's plea -- 'Come over to Macedonia and help us' -- uses language of crossing a boundary. Paul crosses from Asia to Europe, from the eastern to the western Mediterranean. The gospel is making a geographic leap that will reshape world history.

verses.text: He came to Derbe and Lystra: and behold, a certain disciple was there, named Timothy, the son of a Jewess who believed; but his father was a Greek.
verses.explainer: Paul arrives in Derbe and Lystra, where he finds Timothy -- the son of a Jewish mother who is a believer and a Greek father. Timothy is already well spoken of by the believers in Lystra and Iconium.

verses.text: The brothers who were at Lystra and Iconium gave a good testimony about him.
verses.explainer: The brothers in Lystra and Iconium speak well of Timothy. His reputation spans multiple cities. Paul sees potential in this young man and recruits him for the mission team.

verses.text: Paul wanted to have him go out with him, and he took and circumcised him because of the Jews who were in those parts; for they all knew that his father was a Greek.
verses.explainer: Paul circumcises Timothy because of the Jews in those areas, since everyone knows his father is Greek. This is pragmatic, not theological -- after the Jerusalem Council declared circumcision unnecessary for salvation, Paul still uses it as a cultural bridge to facilitate ministry in Jewish communities.

thenVsNow.then: Paul's decision to circumcise Timothy seems contradictory after the Jerusalem Council. But for Paul, the issue was never circumcision itself -- it was requiring circumcision for salvation. Timothy's circumcision was a voluntary cultural accommodation, not a salvation requirement.
thenVsNow.now: The distinction between essential convictions and cultural flexibility remains crucial. Paul's principle: in matters of salvation, never compromise; in matters of cultural practice, freely adapt for the sake of the mission. Knowing which category an issue belongs to requires wisdom.

verses.text: As they went on their way through the cities, they delivered the decrees to them to keep which had been ordained by the apostles and elders who were at Jerusalem.
verses.explainer: As they travel through the cities, they deliver the decisions reached by the apostles and elders in Jerusalem. The council's letter is being distributed -- the churches are being unified around a common standard.

verses.text: So the assemblies were strengthened in the faith, and increased in number daily.
verses.explainer: The churches are 'strengthened in the faith' and grow 'in numbers daily.' The pattern holds: sound teaching plus apostolic encouragement produces both qualitative and quantitative growth.

verses.text: When they had gone through the region of Phrygia and Galatia, they were forbidden by the Holy Spirit to speak the word in Asia.
verses.explainer: They travel through Phrygia and Galatia, 'having been forbidden by the Holy Spirit to speak the word in Asia.' The Spirit blocks them from going west into the province of Asia (where Ephesus is). Divine guidance sometimes comes as a closed door, not an open one.

verses.text: When they had come opposite Mysia, they tried to go into Bithynia, but the Spirit didn’t allow them.
verses.explainer: When they try to go into Bithynia (northeast), 'the Spirit of Jesus did not allow them.' A second door closes. Paul is being funneled toward a specific destination by a process of elimination.

verses.text: Passing by Mysia, they came down to Troas.
verses.explainer: Passing by Mysia, they go down to Troas on the Aegean coast. They have been pushed to the edge of Asia Minor, facing Europe across the sea. They are in position for something they do not yet expect.

verses.text: A vision appeared to Paul in the night. There was a man of Macedonia standing, begging him, and saying, “Come over into Macedonia and help us.”
verses.explainer: During the night, Paul sees a vision: a man from Macedonia standing and pleading, 'Come over to Macedonia and help us.' The Macedonian call is one of the most pivotal moments in Acts -- it directs the gospel westward into Europe.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': 'Macedonia', 'explanation': "The homeland of Alexander the Great, Macedonia was a Roman province occupying modern northern Greece. Its capital was Thessalonica; Philippi was its leading city. Macedonia represented the heart of Greek-speaking Europe. Paul's crossing into Macedonia brought Christianity into the continent that would become its primary home for centuries."}

verses.text: When he had seen the vision, immediately we sought to go out to Macedonia, concluding that the Lord had called us to preach the Good News to them.
verses.explainer: Immediately they seek to go to Macedonia, 'concluding that God had called us to preach the gospel to them.' The switch from 'they' to 'us' marks the beginning of the first 'we passage' -- Luke has joined the team and is now narrating as an eyewitness.

verses.text: Setting sail therefore from Troas, we made a straight course to Samothrace, and the day following to Neapolis;
verses.explainer: They sail from Troas to Samothrace and then to Neapolis, the port city of Philippi. The voyage is swift -- a favorable wind carries them across the Aegean in just two days.

verses.text: and from there to Philippi, which is a city of Macedonia, the foremost of the district, a Roman colony. We were staying some days in this city.
verses.explainer: From Neapolis they travel to Philippi, 'a leading city of the district of Macedonia and a Roman colony.' Luke provides the civic description because Philippi's status matters for the story that follows.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': 'Roman colony', 'explanation': "Philippi was refounded as a Roman colony after the Battle of Philippi (42 BC), where Octavian and Antony defeated Brutus and Cassius. Many residents were retired Roman legionnaires. The city operated under Roman law, used Latin as its official language, and its citizens had the same legal rights as residents of Rome itself. Paul's Roman citizenship will prove critical here."}

verses.text: On the Sabbath day we went outside of the city by a riverside, where we supposed there was a place of prayer, and we sat down, and spoke to the women who had come together.
verses.explainer: On the Sabbath they go outside the city gate to the riverside, where they expect to find a place of prayer. They sit down and speak to the women who have gathered there. There is apparently no synagogue in Philippi -- the Jewish community is too small (fewer than ten Jewish men were required to form one).

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': 'Place of prayer by the river', 'explanation': "When a city lacked enough Jewish men for a synagogue, a 'place of prayer' (proseuche) was established, often near water for ritual washing. The riverside location and the all-female gathering suggest Philippi's Jewish presence was minimal. The first European church begins with a handful of women meeting by a river."}

verses.text: A certain woman named Lydia, a seller of purple, of the city of Thyatira, one who worshiped God, heard us; whose heart the Lord opened to listen to the things which were spoken by Paul.
verses.explainer: A woman named Lydia, a dealer in purple cloth from the city of Thyatira, is listening. She is a God-fearer -- a Gentile who worships the God of Israel. 'The Lord opened her heart to pay attention to what was said by Paul.'

verses.text: When she and her household were baptized, she begged us, saying, “If you have judged me to be faithful to the Lord, come into my house, and stay.” So she persuaded us.
verses.explainer: Lydia and her household are baptized. She then insists: 'If you have judged me to be faithful to the Lord, come to my house and stay.' Her hospitality is assertive -- she 'prevailed upon' them. Her home becomes the first house church in Europe.

thenVsNow.then: Lydia's household baptism and her immediate offer of hospitality established the first Christian community in Europe. A wealthy Gentile businesswoman hosting Jewish missionaries in a Roman military colony -- every category is being crossed.
thenVsNow.now: Lydia represents the intersection of commerce and faith. She used her business success to fund and house the church. The integration of professional life and spiritual commitment that she modeled remains a vital pattern for Christian witness in the marketplace.

verses.text: As we were going to prayer, a certain girl having a spirit of divination met us, who brought her masters much gain by fortune telling.
verses.explainer: A slave girl with a 'spirit of divination' follows Paul and his companions, crying out: 'These men are servants of the Most High God, who proclaim to you the way of salvation.' Her statement is technically accurate but comes from a demonic source.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': 'Spirit of divination', 'explanation': "The Greek says she had a 'spirit of Python' (pneuma pythona) -- a reference to the mythical serpent that guarded the oracle at Delphi. People believed she channeled the Pythian spirit that inspired the famous Delphic oracle. Her owners exploited her condition for profit as a fortune-teller. She was both spiritually oppressed and economically exploited."}

verses.text: Following Paul and us, she cried out, “These men are servants of the Most High God, who proclaim to us a way of salvation!”
verses.explainer: She follows them for many days, repeatedly crying out. Even accurate testimony from the wrong source can be damaging -- it associates the gospel with occult practices.

verses.text:
She was doing this for many days.
But Paul, becoming greatly annoyed, turned and said to the spirit, “I command you in the name of Jesus Christ to come out of her!” It came out that very hour.
verses.explainer: Paul, greatly annoyed, turns and says to the spirit: 'I command you in the name of Jesus Christ to come out of her.' The spirit leaves immediately. Paul's exorcism frees the girl but destroys her owners' revenue stream.

verses.text: But when her masters saw that the hope of their gain was gone, they seized Paul and Silas, and dragged them into the marketplace before the rulers.
verses.explainer: When her owners see that 'their hope of gain was gone,' they seize Paul and Silas and drag them to the marketplace before the rulers. The accusation is economic before it is religious -- they have lost their investment.

thenVsNow.then: The slave girl's owners did not care about her spiritual freedom. They cared about their income stream. The gospel threatened not just religious beliefs but economic systems built on exploitation. When the spirit left, the profit left.
thenVsNow.now: The pattern of economic interests driving religious persecution recurs throughout history. Systems that profit from human exploitation will always resist movements that liberate exploited people. The gospel has always had economic consequences.

verses.text: When they had brought them to the magistrates, they said, “These men, being Jews, are agitating our city,
verses.explainer: The charge: 'These men are Jews and they are disturbing our city.' The accusation plays on anti-Jewish sentiment in this Roman colony. The real issue is money, but the public charge is ethnic and civic disruption.

verses.text: and advocate customs which it is not lawful for us to accept or to observe, being Romans.”
verses.explainer: They claim Paul and Silas 'advocate customs that are not lawful for us as Romans to accept or practice.' In a Roman colony proud of its Roman identity, this is a potent accusation -- the missionaries are portrayed as un-Roman agitators.

verses.text: The multitude rose up together against them, and the magistrates tore their clothes from them, and commanded them to be beaten with rods.
verses.explainer: The crowd joins the attack. The magistrates tear off Paul's and Silas's garments and order them beaten with rods. This is a Roman punishment -- lictors (magistrates' attendants) administered beatings with fasces. Paul and Silas are beaten without trial.

verses.text: When they had laid many stripes on them, they threw them into prison, charging the jailer to keep them safely,
verses.explainer: After inflicting 'many blows,' they throw them into prison, charging the jailer to keep them securely. The violence is severe -- Paul will later call it being 'shamefully treated at Philippi' (1 Thessalonians 2:2).

verses.text: who, having received such a command, threw them into the inner prison, and secured their feet in the stocks.
verses.explainer: The jailer puts them in the inner prison and fastens their feet in the stocks. Maximum security for men already beaten raw. They are in the deepest, darkest part of the prison, unable to move.

verses.text: But about midnight Paul and Silas were praying and singing hymns to God, and the prisoners were listening to them.
verses.explainer: About midnight, Paul and Silas are praying and singing hymns to God, and the other prisoners are listening. Bleeding, chained, in pitch darkness -- and they are singing. The other prisoners have never heard anything like this.

thenVsNow.then: Singing in prison at midnight after a severe beating was not natural resilience or Stoic endurance. It was an act of defiant faith -- worshiping God in the worst possible circumstances. The other prisoners' amazement suggests this behavior was unprecedented.
thenVsNow.now: The Philippian prison scene has inspired persecuted Christians for centuries. The image of worship in chains, joy in suffering, and witness through endurance is the most dramatic illustration of the paradox at the heart of Christian faith: strength through weakness, freedom in bondage, joy through suffering.

verses.text: Suddenly there was a great earthquake, so that the foundations of the prison were shaken; and immediately all the doors were opened, and everyone’s bonds were loosened.
verses.explainer: Suddenly a great earthquake shakes the prison's foundations. Every door opens, and every prisoner's chains are unfastened. The earthquake is selective and purposeful -- it frees everyone without collapsing the building.

verses.text: The jailer, being roused out of sleep and seeing the prison doors open, drew his sword and was about to kill himself, supposing that the prisoners had escaped.
verses.explainer: The jailer wakes, sees the open doors, and draws his sword to kill himself. Under Roman military law, a guard who lost his prisoners faced execution. Suicide was the honorable Roman alternative to disgrace.

verses.text: But Paul cried with a loud voice, saying, “Don’t harm yourself, for we are all here!”
verses.explainer: Paul shouts: 'Do not harm yourself, for we are all here!' In the darkness, Paul cannot see the jailer and the jailer cannot see the prisoners. But Paul speaks up to save the man who imprisoned him. Grace in action.

verses.text: He called for lights, sprang in, fell down trembling before Paul and Silas,
verses.explainer: The jailer calls for lights, rushes in, and falls trembling before Paul and Silas. The earthquake, the open doors, the unchained prisoners who stayed, the singing at midnight -- it all converges on this man.

verses.text: brought them out, and said, “Sirs, what must I do to be saved?”
verses.explainer: He brings them out and asks the question: 'Sirs, what must I do to be saved?' The Philippian jailer's question is the question Acts keeps asking and answering. Salvation is the offer; faith is the response.

verses.text: They said, “Believe in the Lord Jesus Christ, and you will be saved, you and your household.”
verses.explainer: The answer: 'Believe in the Lord Jesus, and you will be saved, you and your household.' The simplest statement of the gospel in Acts. No circumcision, no law-keeping, no elaborate requirements -- believe in Jesus.

verses.text: They spoke the word of the Lord to him, and to all who were in his house.
verses.explainer: They speak the word of the Lord to him and to everyone in his household. The jailer's entire family hears the gospel. Ancient households included extended family, servants, and dependents.

verses.text: He took them the same hour of the night, and washed their stripes, and was immediately baptized, he and all his household.
verses.explainer: That same hour of the night, the jailer washes their wounds and is immediately baptized, he and all his family. The sequence is striking: he washes the missionaries' wounds, then they wash him in baptism. Mutual care and spiritual transformation happen simultaneously.

verses.text: He brought them up into his house, and set food before them, and rejoiced greatly, with all his household, having believed in God.
verses.explainer: He brings them into his house, sets food before them, and 'he rejoiced along with his entire household that he had believed in God.' The chapter that began with joy at Lydia's conversion ends with joy at the jailer's conversion. Philippi is a city of joy.

verses.text: But when it was day, the magistrates sent the sergeants, saying, “Let those men go.”
verses.explainer: When day comes, the magistrates send police with the message: 'Let those men go.' They may have been sobered by the earthquake or simply wanted to end the incident quietly.
