verses.text: The jailer reported these words to Paul, saying, “The magistrates have sent to let you go; now therefore come out, and go in peace.”
verses.explainer: The jailer reports to Paul: 'The magistrates have sent to let you go. Now therefore come out and go in peace.' The matter seems resolved.

verses.text: But Paul said to them, “They have beaten us publicly, without a trial, men who are Romans, and have cast us into prison! Do they now release us secretly? No, most certainly, but let them come themselves and bring us out!”
verses.explainer: But Paul refuses: 'They have beaten us publicly, uncondemned, men who are Roman citizens, and have thrown us into prison. And now they would send us away secretly? No! Let them come themselves and take us out.' Paul demands a public apology.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': 'Roman citizenship', 'explanation': "The Lex Porcia and Lex Julia prohibited beating a Roman citizen without trial (uncondemned). The magistrates had violated fundamental Roman law by flogging Paul and Silas without establishing their legal status. Paul's demand for a public escort was not vanity -- it was a legal strategy to protect the fledgling church from future persecution."}

verses.text: The sergeants reported these words to the magistrates, and they were afraid when they heard that they were Romans,
verses.explainer: The magistrates are alarmed when they hear Paul and Silas are Roman citizens. They have committed a serious legal offense that could result in their removal from office or worse.

verses.text: and they came and begged them. When they had brought them out, they asked them to depart from the city.
verses.explainer: The magistrates come personally, apologize, bring them out, and ask them to leave the city. The public apology vindicates the missionaries and, by extension, the church they have established.

verses.text: They went out of the prison, and entered into Lydia’s house. When they had seen the brothers, they encouraged them, and departed.
verses.explainer: Paul and Silas visit Lydia's house, encourage the brothers, and then depart. The church in Philippi is established -- born through three conversions (Lydia, the slave girl, the jailer) that represent the full social spectrum. Luke stays behind in Philippi (the 'we' passages resume later).

explainer.summary: Paul moves through Thessalonica and Berea, where the pattern of synagogue preaching, initial success, and Jewish opposition continues. Forced to flee Berea, Paul arrives in Athens alone and is deeply troubled by the city's idolatry. He debates with Epicurean and Stoic philosophers and is invited to speak at the Areopagus, where he delivers his most famous speech -- building bridges from Greek philosophy and poetry to the gospel of the risen Christ. The response is mixed but includes notable converts.
themes: Reasoning from Scripture in the synagogue
The Berean model of examining Scripture
The resurrection as the point of division
explainer.contextInBook: Chapter 17 shows Paul's remarkable versatility. In Thessalonica he argues from Jewish Scripture; in Athens he engages Greek philosophy. The Areopagus speech is the intellectual high point of Acts -- Paul demonstrates that the gospel can be communicated in any cultural language without losing its essential content.

wordStudies.translatedAs: To investigate, to judge, to sift through evidence
wordStudies.meaning: To investigate, to judge, to sift through evidence. The Bereans 'examined the Scriptures daily' to verify Paul's claims. The word implies careful, critical analysis, not passive acceptance. True faith is not blind; it investigates.

wordStudies.translatedAs: Athenians erected altars to 'unknown gods' as insu
wordStudies.meaning: Athenians erected altars to 'unknown gods' as insurance against offending any deity they might have overlooked. Paul seizes this altar as his starting point, declaring that the God they worship in ignorance is the one he proclaims. The word agnostos gives us 'agnostic.'

verses.text: Now when they had passed through Amphipolis and Apollonia, they came to Thessalonica, where there was a Jewish synagogue.
verses.explainer: Paul and Silas travel through Amphipolis and Apollonia and arrive in Thessalonica, where there is a Jewish synagogue. Thessalonica was the capital of Macedonia and its largest city.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': 'Thessalonica', 'explanation': "Modern Thessaloniki (Salonika), Greece's second-largest city. In the first century it was the capital of the Roman province of Macedonia, a major commercial port on the Via Egnatia (the main Roman highway connecting the Adriatic coast to Byzantium). Its strategic location made it ideal for spreading the gospel throughout the region."}

verses.text: Paul, as was his custom, went in to them, and for three Sabbath days reasoned with them from the Scriptures,
verses.explainer: Paul follows his custom: he enters the synagogue and for three Sabbaths reasons with them from the Scriptures. He does not merely assert; he reasons, argues, and demonstrates from the text.

verses.text: explaining and demonstrating that the Christ had to suffer and rise again from the dead, and saying, “This Jesus, whom I proclaim to you, is the Christ.”
verses.explainer: He explains and proves 'that it was necessary for the Christ to suffer and to rise from the dead.' Then: 'This Jesus, whom I proclaim to you, is the Christ.' Paul's argument has two parts: the Messiah must suffer, and Jesus is that Messiah.

verses.text: Some of them were persuaded, and joined Paul and Silas, of the devout Greeks a great multitude, and not a few of the chief women.
verses.explainer: Some Jews are persuaded, along with 'a great many of the devout Greeks and not a few of the leading women.' The pattern holds: the gospel appeals across ethnic and social lines. Prominent women are specifically mentioned as converts.

verses.text: But the unpersuaded Jews took along some wicked men from the marketplace, and gathering a crowd, set the city in an uproar. Assaulting the house of Jason, they sought to bring them out to the people.
verses.explainer: The Jews who are not persuaded become jealous, recruit troublemakers from the marketplace, form a mob, and attack the house of Jason, where Paul and Silas are staying. Unable to find the missionaries, they drag Jason before the city authorities.

verses.text:
When they didn’t find them, they dragged Jason and certain brothers
 before the rulers of the city, crying, “These who have turned the world upside down have come here also,
verses.explainer: The accusation: 'These men who have turned the world upside down have come here also.' This is meant as a charge but reads as a compliment. The gospel is so disruptive that it is described as world-inverting.

thenVsNow.then: The charge of 'turning the world upside down' was politically motivated -- it portrayed the missionaries as social agitators. But the accusation inadvertently testified to the gospel's power. A message that merely reinforced the status quo would not provoke this language.
thenVsNow.now: The phrase 'turning the world upside down' has become a positive description of the gospel's transformative power. The early church was not interested in maintaining the existing order but in inaugurating a new one. The question for modern churches is whether they are still disruptive enough to earn this accusation.

verses.text: whom Jason has received. These all act contrary to the decrees of Caesar, saying that there is another king, Jesus!”
verses.explainer: They accuse Jason of hosting these men who 'act against the decrees of Caesar, saying that there is another king, Jesus.' The charge is sedition -- proclaiming a rival to Caesar. In a Roman context, this was the most dangerous possible accusation.

verses.text: The multitude and the rulers of the city were troubled when they heard these things.
verses.explainer: The people and the city authorities are disturbed when they hear this. The political implications of 'another king' are not lost on anyone in a Roman city.

verses.text: When they had taken security from Jason and the rest, they let them go.
verses.explainer: They take a security bond from Jason and release him. The bond probably required Jason to guarantee that Paul would not return -- effectively exiling Paul from Thessalonica.

verses.text: The brothers immediately sent Paul and Silas away by night to Beroea. When they arrived, they went into the Jewish synagogue.
verses.explainer: The brothers immediately send Paul and Silas away by night to Berea. The pattern continues: opposition in one city sends the gospel to the next.

verses.text: Now these were more noble than those in Thessalonica, in that they received the word with all readiness of mind, examining the Scriptures daily to see whether these things were so.
verses.explainer: The Bereans are described as 'more noble' than those in Thessalonica because they receive the word 'with all eagerness, examining the Scriptures daily to see if these things were so.' They combine openness with critical thinking.

thenVsNow.then: The Bereans did not blindly accept or reflexively reject Paul's teaching. They listened eagerly and then checked his claims against Scripture. This combination of receptivity and discernment was exemplary.
thenVsNow.now: The Berean model -- open-minded but Scripture-checking -- has been adopted as the ideal for Christian engagement with teaching. 'Be like the Bereans' is common advice in churches. It values neither gullibility nor cynicism but informed, thoughtful engagement.

verses.text: Many of them therefore believed; also of the prominent Greek women, and not a few men.
verses.explainer: Many of them believe, including 'not a few Greek women of high standing as well as men.' The social profile of early converts in Macedonia consistently includes prominent women.

verses.text: But when the Jews of Thessalonica had knowledge that the word of God was proclaimed by Paul at Beroea also, they came there likewise, agitating the multitudes.
verses.explainer: When the Thessalonian Jews learn that Paul is preaching in Berea, they come there too and agitate the crowds. Paul's opponents are willing to travel to suppress his message.

verses.text: Then the brothers immediately sent out Paul to go as far as to the sea, and Silas and Timothy still stayed there.
verses.explainer: The brothers send Paul to the coast, while Silas and Timothy stay in Berea. Paul is the primary target; removing him reduces the immediate danger.

verses.text: But those who escorted Paul brought him as far as Athens. Receiving a commandment to Silas and Timothy that they should come to him very quickly, they departed.
verses.explainer: Paul's escorts bring him to Athens and return with instructions for Silas and Timothy to join him as soon as possible. Paul arrives in Athens alone.

verses.text: Now while Paul waited for them at Athens, his spirit was provoked within him as he saw the city full of idols.
verses.explainer: While waiting in Athens, Paul's spirit is 'provoked within him' as he sees the city full of idols. Athens was famous for its temples, statues, and altars -- one ancient writer joked it was easier to find a god in Athens than a man.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': 'Athens full of idols', 'explanation': "Athens in Paul's day was no longer the political power it had been in the classical period, but it remained the intellectual and cultural capital of the Greek world. The Acropolis, the Parthenon, and hundreds of temples and statues made it a showcase of Greco-Roman religion. Paul's provocation was not aesthetic but theological -- every idol represented a lie about the nature of God."}

verses.text: So he reasoned in the synagogue with the Jews and the devout persons, and in the marketplace every day with those who met him.
verses.explainer: Paul reasons in the synagogue with Jews and God-fearers and in the marketplace with whoever happens to be there. The marketplace (agora) was Athens's public square -- the same space where Socrates had debated centuries earlier.

verses.text:
Some of the Epicurean and Stoic philosophers also were conversing with him. Some said, “What does this babbler want to say?”
Others said, “He seems to be advocating foreign deities,” because he preached Jesus and the resurrection.
verses.explainer: Epicurean and Stoic philosophers engage Paul. Some call him a 'babbler' (Greek: spermologos -- literally 'seed-picker,' a bird that pecks at scraps). Others say: 'He seems to be a preacher of foreign divinities,' because Paul preaches 'Jesus and the resurrection.'

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': 'Epicureans and Stoics', 'explanation': "The two dominant philosophical schools in Athens. Epicureans believed the gods were distant and uninvolved, that death was final, and that tranquility (ataraxia) was life's goal. Stoics believed in a divine logos (reason) pervading the universe, practiced virtue and self-discipline, and accepted fate with equanimity. Paul's message challenged both: the Epicureans by proclaiming an involved God, the Stoics by proclaiming resurrection."}

verses.text: They took hold of him, and brought him to the Areopagus, saying, “May we know what this new teaching is, which is spoken by you?
verses.explainer: They bring Paul to the Areopagus and ask: 'May we know what this new teaching is that you are presenting?' The Areopagus was both a rocky hill west of the Acropolis and the name of the council that met there to evaluate new ideas and religious practices.

verses.text: For you bring certain strange things to our ears. We want to know therefore what these things mean.”
verses.explainer: 'You bring some strange things to our ears; we wish to know what these things mean.' The Athenian curiosity is genuine but also potentially superficial -- they collect new ideas the way others collect artifacts.

verses.text: Now all the Athenians and the strangers living there spent their time in nothing else, but either to tell or to hear some new thing.
verses.explainer: Luke adds: 'All the Athenians and the foreigners who lived there would spend their time in nothing except telling or hearing something new.' The aside is gently ironic -- Athens's intellectual culture could also be intellectual tourism.

verses.text: Paul stood in the middle of the Areopagus, and said, “You men of Athens, I perceive that you are very religious in all things.
verses.explainer: Paul stands in the middle of the Areopagus and begins: 'Men of Athens, I perceive that in every way you are very religious.' He opens with respect, not condemnation. The word 'religious' (deisidaimonesteros) is deliberately ambiguous -- it can mean 'very devout' or 'very superstitious.'

verses.text: For as I passed along, and observed the objects of your worship, I found also an altar with this inscription: ‘TO AN UNKNOWN GOD.’ What therefore you worship in ignorance, this I announce to you.
verses.explainer: 'As I passed along and observed the objects of your worship, I found also an altar with this inscription: To the unknown god. What therefore you worship as unknown, this I proclaim to you.' Paul's rhetorical brilliance: he does not import a foreign god but reveals the identity of a god they already acknowledge.

thenVsNow.then: Paul's use of the 'unknown god' altar was a masterpiece of contextual evangelism. He did not attack Athenian religion; he used its own admitted incompleteness as an entry point. The altar confessed what he would proclaim: there is a God you do not yet know.
thenVsNow.now: Paul's approach at Athens has become the model for engaging secular or non-Christian cultures: find the point of contact, affirm what is true, and then fill in what is missing. This is neither syncretism (blending religions) nor confrontation (attacking the audience) but bridge-building from shared ground.

verses.text: The God who made the world and all things in it, he, being Lord of heaven and earth, doesn’t dwell in temples made with hands,
verses.explainer: 'The God who made the world and everything in it, being Lord of heaven and earth, does not live in temples made by man.' Paul starts with creation -- God as maker of all things. This would resonate with Stoics (who believed in a cosmic logos) while correcting Epicureans (who denied divine involvement in the world).

verses.text: neither is he served by men’s hands, as though he needed anything, seeing he himself gives to all life and breath, and all things.
verses.explainer: God is not 'served by human hands, as though he needed anything, since he himself gives to all mankind life and breath and everything.' God is not a cosmic consumer who needs human offerings. He is the source of all life. The entire Athenian temple system is gently undermined.

verses.text: He made from one blood every nation of men to dwell on all the surface of the earth, having determined appointed seasons, and the boundaries of their dwellings,
verses.explainer: God 'made from one man every nation of mankind to live on all the face of the earth, having determined allotted periods and the boundaries of their dwelling place.' Paul asserts the unity of the human race -- all peoples come from one origin. This challenges Greek notions of ethnic superiority.

verses.text: that they should seek the Lord, if perhaps they might reach out for him and find him, though he is not far from each one of us.
verses.explainer: God did this so 'that they should seek God, and perhaps feel their way toward him and find him.' Human existence is designed for seeking God. The word 'feel' (pselaphao) implies groping in the dark -- humanity's search for God is real but impaired.

verses.text: ‘For in him we live, and move, and have our being.’ As some of your own poets have said, ‘For we are also his offspring.’
verses.explainer: 'In him we live and move and have our being.' Paul then quotes a Greek poet: 'For we are indeed his offspring.' He cites Epimenides (or possibly Aratus) and Aratus of Soli. Paul uses pagan poetry to make a biblical point -- we are God's creation, not the reverse.

verses.text: Being then the offspring of God, we ought not to think that the Divine Nature is like gold, or silver, or stone, engraved by art and design of man.
verses.explainer: If we are God's offspring, 'we ought not to think that the divine being is like gold or silver or stone, an image formed by the art and imagination of man.' Paul's logic: if we are God's children, God must be at least as personal and alive as we are. Dead statues cannot represent a living God.

verses.text: The times of ignorance therefore God overlooked. But now he commands that all people everywhere should repent,
verses.explainer: God 'overlooked the times of ignorance, but now he commands all people everywhere to repent.' The age of tolerance toward pagan ignorance is over. The gospel changes the equation -- what was excusable before is now inexcusable.

verses.text: because he has appointed a day in which he will judge the world in righteousness by the man whom he has ordained; of which he has given assurance to all men, in that he has raised him from the dead.”
verses.explainer: God 'has fixed a day on which he will judge the world in righteousness by a man whom he has appointed; and of this he has given assurance to all by raising him from the dead.' The resurrection is the proof, the judgment is coming, and Jesus is the appointed judge.

thenVsNow.then: Paul saves the resurrection for his climax. Everything before it -- creation, divine providence, human seeking, even Greek poetry -- builds toward this single claim: God raised a man from the dead. This is where Athens divides.
thenVsNow.now: The resurrection remains the dividing line. Paul's speech at Athens demonstrates that Christianity can engage any intellectual tradition, quote any source, and build any bridge -- but it cannot remove the scandal of a bodily resurrection. That claim is where dialogue becomes decision.

verses.text: Now when they heard of the resurrection of the dead, some mocked; but others said, “We want to hear you again concerning this.”
verses.explainer: When they hear about the resurrection, some mock, but others say: 'We will hear you again about this.' The response is divided -- mockery, polite deferral, and genuine faith. Athens does not produce a mass conversion but it produces converts.

verses.text: Thus Paul went out from among them.
verses.explainer: Paul leaves the Areopagus. The speech is over; the results are in God's hands.

verses.text: But certain men joined with him, and believed, among whom also was Dionysius the Areopagite, and a woman named Damaris, and others with them.
verses.explainer: Some join Paul and believe, including Dionysius the Areopagite (a member of the Areopagus council), a woman named Damaris, and others. The converts include a civic leader and a named woman -- not a large harvest, but a significant one in the intellectual capital of the ancient world.

explainer.summary: Paul arrives in Corinth, meets Priscilla and Aquila (Jewish tentmakers expelled from Rome), and supports himself through manual labor while preaching. When the Jews oppose him, he turns to the Gentiles. The Lord reassures Paul in a night vision: 'I have many people in this city.' Paul stays eighteen months. Brought before the proconsul Gallio, he is acquitted in a ruling that effectively establishes Christianity's legal right to exist. Paul eventually returns to Antioch, completing the second missionary journey. The chapter closes with Apollos in Ephesus, where Priscilla and Aquila correct his incomplete understanding of the gospel.
themes: Priscilla and Aquila as theological mentors
explainer.contextInBook: Corinth becomes one of Paul's most important church plants -- two major epistles will be written to this congregation. The Gallio hearing is historically significant: it provides one of the few fixed dates in New Testament chronology (Gallio's proconsulship is dated to 51-52 AD by an inscription at Delphi) and establishes the legal precedent that Christianity is not illegal under Roman law.

wordStudies.translatedAs: A maker of tents or leather goods
wordStudies.meaning: A maker of tents or leather goods. Paul, Aquila, and Priscilla shared this trade. Rabbinical tradition required rabbis to have a manual trade alongside their teaching. Paul's tentmaking was both economic self-support and a ministry strategy -- working alongside people provided natural opportunities for gospel conversations.

verses.text: After these things Paul departed from Athens, and came to Corinth.
verses.explainer: Paul leaves Athens and goes to Corinth. After the limited response in Athens, Paul enters one of the most challenging cities in the Roman Empire.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': 'Corinth', 'explanation': "Corinth was rebuilt by Julius Caesar as a Roman colony in 44 BC after being destroyed a century earlier. By Paul's day it was the capital of the province of Achaia and a major commercial hub controlling trade between the eastern and western Mediterranean through its two ports. It was proverbially associated with wealth, sexual excess, and cultural diversity. The verb 'to Corinthianize' meant to live immorally."}

verses.text: He found a certain Jew named Aquila, a man of Pontus by race, who had recently come from Italy, with his wife Priscilla, because Claudius had commanded all the Jews to depart from Rome. He came to them,
verses.explainer: Paul finds Aquila, a Jew from Pontus, and his wife Priscilla, recently arrived from Italy because Claudius had ordered all Jews to leave Rome. They share Paul's trade as tentmakers.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': "Claudius's edict", 'explanation': "The Roman historian Suetonius records that Claudius expelled Jews from Rome because of disturbances 'at the instigation of Chrestus' (likely a garbled reference to Christ/Christus). This suggests that the gospel had already reached Rome and was causing controversy in the Jewish community before Paul ever visited. The edict is dated to around 49 AD."}

verses.text: and because he practiced the same trade, he lived with them and worked, for by trade they were tent makers.
verses.explainer: Because they share the same trade, Paul lives and works with them. They are tentmakers (or leatherworkers). Paul supports himself rather than depending on the Corinthian church for financial support.

verses.text: He reasoned in the synagogue every Sabbath, and persuaded Jews and Greeks.
verses.explainer: Every Sabbath, Paul reasons in the synagogue, trying to persuade both Jews and Greeks. He continues his standard pattern: synagogue first, then broader outreach.

verses.text: But when Silas and Timothy came down from Macedonia, Paul was compelled by the Spirit, testifying to the Jews that Jesus was the Christ.
verses.explainer: When Silas and Timothy arrive from Macedonia, Paul devotes himself entirely to preaching, testifying to the Jews that the Christ is Jesus. Their arrival may have included financial support from the Macedonian churches, freeing Paul from tentmaking.

verses.text: When they opposed him and blasphemed, he shook out his clothing and said to them, “Your blood be on your own heads! I am clean. From now on, I will go to the Gentiles!”
verses.explainer: When the Jews oppose and revile him, Paul shakes out his garments and says: 'Your blood be on your own heads! I am innocent. From now on I will go to the Gentiles.' The gesture is dramatic -- shaking off responsibility for their rejection.

verses.text: He departed there, and went into the house of a certain man named Justus, one who worshiped God, whose house was next door to the synagogue.
verses.explainer: Paul moves next door to the house of Titius Justus, a Gentile God-fearer whose house is right beside the synagogue. The proximity is both practical and provocative -- the Gentile ministry operates in the synagogue's shadow.

verses.text: Crispus, the ruler of the synagogue, believed in the Lord with all his house. Many of the Corinthians, when they heard, believed and were baptized.
verses.explainer: Crispus, the ruler of the synagogue, believes in the Lord with his entire household, and many Corinthians hear, believe, and are baptized. The synagogue's own leader converts -- a devastating blow to the opposition.

verses.text:
The Lord said to Paul in the night by a vision,
“Don’t be afraid, but speak and don’t be silent;
verses.explainer: The Lord speaks to Paul in a night vision: 'Do not be afraid, but go on speaking and do not be silent.' Paul is afraid. The man who survived stoning at Lystra and beatings at Philippi is frightened in Corinth. The vision acknowledges his fear before addressing it.

verses.text: for I am with you, and no one will attack you to harm you, for I have many people in this city.”
verses.explainer: 'For I am with you, and no one will attack you to harm you, for I have many people in this city.' God promises protection and reveals that many future converts are waiting. The harvest is large; Paul must stay to bring it in.

thenVsNow.then: God's assurance that he has 'many people' in Corinth -- people who have not yet believed -- reveals a perspective invisible to Paul. From Paul's vantage point, Corinth was hostile and dangerous. From God's vantage point, it was full of potential believers.
thenVsNow.now: The principle that God sees potential believers where we see hostility challenges every assumption about 'unreachable' places or people. No city is too sinful, too secular, or too hostile for the gospel. God's assessment of a place's potential always exceeds human estimates.

verses.text: He lived there a year and six months, teaching the word of God among them.
verses.explainer: Paul stays eighteen months, teaching the word of God. This is his longest stay anywhere on the second journey. The Corinthian church requires extended investment to establish firmly.

verses.text: But when Gallio was proconsul of Achaia, the Jews with one accord rose up against Paul and brought him before the judgment seat,
verses.explainer: When Gallio becomes proconsul of Achaia, the Jews bring Paul before the tribunal, accusing him of persuading people to worship God 'contrary to the law.' The charge is vague -- Jewish law or Roman law?

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': 'Gallio, proconsul of Achaia', 'explanation': "Lucius Junius Gallio Annaeanus was the brother of the famous philosopher Seneca. An inscription at Delphi dates his proconsulship to 51-52 AD, providing one of the most important chronological anchors for New Testament history. His ruling on Paul's case had far-reaching legal implications."}

verses.text: saying, “This man persuades men to worship God contrary to the law.”
verses.explainer: They accuse Paul of persuading people to worship God 'contrary to the law.' The charge is strategically ambiguous -- they want Gallio to interpret it as a violation of Roman law.

verses.text: But when Paul was about to open his mouth, Gallio said to the Jews, “If indeed it were a matter of wrong or of wicked crime, you Jews, it would be reasonable that I should bear with you;
verses.explainer: Before Paul can open his mouth, Gallio addresses the Jews: 'If it were a matter of wrongdoing or vicious crime, I would have reason to accept your complaint.' Gallio distinguishes between criminal matters (his jurisdiction) and religious disputes (not his problem).

verses.text: but if they are questions about words and names and your own law, look to it yourselves. For I don’t want to be a judge of these matters.”
verses.explainer: 'But since it is a matter of questions about words and names and your own law, see to it yourselves. I refuse to be a judge of these things.' Gallio rules that the dispute is an internal Jewish matter. Christianity is not a criminal offense under Roman law.

thenVsNow.then: Gallio's ruling was a landmark legal precedent. By classifying the Paul dispute as an internal Jewish religious matter, he effectively placed Christianity under the umbrella of Judaism's legal protections. As long as Christianity was seen as a Jewish sect, it enjoyed the status of a religio licita (permitted religion).
thenVsNow.now: The Gallio precedent established that Roman courts would not adjudicate theological disputes between Jews and Christians. This gave Christianity legal breathing room throughout the empire for at least another decade, until Nero's persecution in 64 AD.

verses.text: So he drove them from the judgment seat.
verses.explainer: Gallio drives the accusers from the tribunal. He physically ejects them from the court. His dismissal is emphatic and public.

verses.text: Then all the Greeks laid hold on Sosthenes, the ruler of the synagogue, and beat him before the judgment seat. Gallio didn’t care about any of these things.
verses.explainer: The crowd seizes Sosthenes, the ruler of the synagogue, and beats him in front of the tribunal. Gallio pays no attention. The violence may reflect anti-Jewish sentiment in the crowd, and Gallio's indifference shows Roman contempt for Jewish disputes.

verses.text:
Paul, having stayed after this many more days, took his leave of the brothers,
 and sailed from there for Syria, together with Priscilla and Aquila. He shaved his head in Cenchreae, for he had a vow.
verses.explainer: Paul stays many days longer, then sails for Syria with Priscilla and Aquila. At Cenchreae he cuts his hair, 'for he was under a vow.' The vow is likely a Nazirite vow -- Paul still observes Jewish practices voluntarily.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': 'Nazirite vow', 'explanation': "A Nazirite vow (Numbers 6) involved abstaining from wine and cutting hair for a set period, with the hair shaved at the vow's completion and offered at the Temple. Paul's observance shows he remained a practicing Jew even while championing Gentile freedom from the law."}

verses.text: He came to Ephesus, and he left them there; but he himself entered into the synagogue, and reasoned with the Jews.
verses.explainer: They arrive in Ephesus, where Paul enters the synagogue and reasons with the Jews. He leaves Priscilla and Aquila there, promising to return 'if God wills.' This brief visit sets the stage for his major Ephesian ministry in chapter 19.

verses.text: When they asked him to stay with them a longer time, he declined;
verses.explainer: The Ephesian Jews ask Paul to stay longer, but he declines. He has other commitments -- the journey homeward beckons.

verses.text: but taking his leave of them, he said, “I must by all means keep this coming feast in Jerusalem, but I will return again to you if God wills.” Then he set sail from Ephesus.
verses.explainer: Paul sails from Ephesus, promising to return. The promise will be fulfilled -- Paul will spend over two years in Ephesus on his third journey.

verses.text: When he had landed at Caesarea, he went up and greeted the assembly, and went down to Antioch.
verses.explainer: Paul lands at Caesarea, goes up (to Jerusalem), greets the church, and goes down to Antioch. The second missionary journey ends where it began -- at the Antioch church that commissioned him.

verses.text: Having spent some time there, he departed, and went through the region of Galatia, and Phrygia, in order, establishing all the disciples.
verses.explainer: After spending some time in Antioch, Paul departs on the third missionary journey, going through Galatia and Phrygia, 'strengthening all the disciples.' He retraces his steps yet again, investing in churches he has already planted.

verses.text: Now a certain Jew named Apollos, an Alexandrian by race, an eloquent man, came to Ephesus. He was mighty in the Scriptures.
verses.explainer: A Jew named Apollos, a native of Alexandria, arrives in Ephesus. He is 'an eloquent man, competent in the Scriptures.' Alexandria was famous for its scholarly tradition, and Apollos represents the best of Jewish-Alexandrian learning.

verses.text: This man had been instructed in the way of the Lord; and being fervent in spirit, he spoke and taught accurately the things concerning Jesus, although he knew only the baptism of John.
verses.explainer: Apollos has been instructed in the way of the Lord and speaks with great fervor, teaching accurately about Jesus. But he knows only the baptism of John. His knowledge is genuine but incomplete -- he has the prologue but not the full story.

verses.text: He began to speak boldly in the synagogue. But when Priscilla and Aquila heard him, they took him aside, and explained to him the way of God more accurately.
verses.explainer: He begins speaking boldly in the synagogue. Priscilla and Aquila hear him and 'took him aside and explained to him the way of God more accurately.' A married couple privately mentors one of the most gifted preachers in the early church, filling in what he is missing.

thenVsNow.then: Priscilla and Aquila did not publicly correct Apollos or compete with him. They took him aside privately and supplemented his knowledge. Their approach honored his gifts while completing his understanding. Priscilla is named first, suggesting her leading role in the instruction.
thenVsNow.now: The Priscilla-Aquila model for handling gifted but incomplete teachers remains exemplary: private correction, not public humiliation. It also demonstrates that theological education can happen through lay mentoring, not just formal institutions.

verses.text: When he had determined to pass over into Achaia, the brothers encouraged him, and wrote to the disciples to receive him. When he had come, he greatly helped those who had believed through grace;
verses.explainer: When Apollos wants to go to Achaia (where Corinth is), the brothers encourage him and write to the disciples to welcome him. He arrives and greatly helps the believers through his gift of persuasion.

verses.text: for he powerfully refuted the Jews, publicly showing by the Scriptures that Jesus was the Christ.
verses.explainer: Apollos 'powerfully refuted the Jews in public, showing by the Scriptures that the Christ was Jesus.' His Alexandrian training in argumentation combined with his now-complete understanding of the gospel makes him a formidable advocate.

explainer.summary: Paul arrives in Ephesus for an extended ministry of over two years. He encounters disciples who know only John's baptism, teaches daily in the hall of Tyrannus, performs extraordinary miracles, and confronts occult practices so powerfully that practitioners burn their magic books in a massive bonfire. His success provokes a citywide riot led by Demetrius the silversmith, whose shrine-making business is threatened by the decline of Artemis worship. The riot is eventually calmed by the city clerk, who argues that the Christians have committed no crime.
themes: The power of the gospel over occult practices
Economic threat as a catalyst for persecution
explainer.contextInBook: Ephesus is the crown of Paul's third missionary journey and his longest stay in any city. The events here demonstrate the gospel's power to transform an entire city's religious and economic life. Ephesus also becomes the base from which 'all the residents of Asia heard the word of the Lord' (19:10) -- a regional saturation of the gospel.

wordStudies.translatedAs: One who adjures spirits by invoking powerful names
wordStudies.meaning: One who adjures spirits by invoking powerful names. Jewish exorcists were well-known in the ancient world, using divine names and incantations. The sons of Sceva tried to use Jesus' name as a magic formula without having a relationship with Jesus -- a distinction that cost them dearly.

wordStudies.translatedAs: The Greek goddess of the hunt, but the Ephesian Ar
wordStudies.meaning: The Greek goddess of the hunt, but the Ephesian Artemis was a distinct, multi-breasted fertility deity whose cult was central to the city's identity and economy. Her temple was one of the Seven Wonders of the Ancient World. The silversmiths' livelihood depended on manufacturing miniature shrines and statues of this goddess.

verses.text: While Apollos was at Corinth, Paul, having passed through the upper country, came to Ephesus, and found certain disciples.
verses.explainer: While Apollos is in Corinth, Paul travels through the interior regions and arrives in Ephesus. He finds about twelve disciples there.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': 'The interior regions', 'explanation': 'Paul traveled through the highlands of central Asia Minor rather than the main coastal road, passing through rugged terrain and small towns. This route through the interior brought him to Ephesus from the east, arriving at the third-largest city in the Roman Empire from its back door.'}

verses.text:
He said to them, “Did you receive the Holy Spirit when you believed?”
They said to him, “No, we haven’t even heard that there is a Holy Spirit.”
verses.explainer: Paul asks them: 'Did you receive the Holy Spirit when you believed?' They answer: 'We have not even heard that there is a Holy Spirit.' Their ignorance reveals how incomplete their instruction has been.

thenVsNow.then: These disciples knew only John's baptism -- a baptism of repentance pointing forward to the Messiah. They had not heard that the Messiah had come, died, risen, and sent the Spirit. They were Old Testament believers living in New Testament times.
thenVsNow.now: The Ephesian disciples' situation illustrates that sincere faith can be incomplete. They believed what they knew but did not know enough. The church's task includes not just evangelism (reaching the unreached) but also instruction (completing the partially reached).

verses.text:
He said, “Into what then were you baptized?”
They said, “Into John’s baptism.”
verses.explainer: Paul asks: 'Into what then were you baptized?' They answer: 'Into John's baptism.' John's baptism pointed forward; Christian baptism looks back to what Jesus has accomplished.

verses.text: Paul said, “John indeed baptized with the baptism of repentance, saying to the people that they should believe in the one who would come after him, that is, in Jesus.”
verses.explainer: Paul explains: John's baptism was about repentance and pointed to 'the one who was to come after him, that is, Jesus.' John was the signpost; Jesus is the destination.

verses.text: When they heard this, they were baptized in the name of the Lord Jesus.
verses.explainer: On hearing this, they are baptized in the name of the Lord Jesus. Their rebaptism shows that John's baptism, while valid in its time, was superseded by baptism into Christ.

verses.text: When Paul had laid his hands on them, the Holy Spirit came on them, and they spoke with other languages and prophesied.
verses.explainer: When Paul lays hands on them, the Holy Spirit comes on them, and they speak in tongues and prophesy. The manifestation echoes Pentecost and Cornelius's household -- the Spirit validates each new community of believers.

verses.text: They were about twelve men in all.
verses.explainer: There are about twelve men in all. The number twelve may be coincidental, or it may echo the twelve apostles -- a new foundation for ministry in Ephesus.

verses.text: He entered into the synagogue, and spoke boldly for a period of three months, reasoning and persuading about the things concerning God’s Kingdom.
verses.explainer: Paul enters the synagogue and speaks boldly for three months, 'reasoning and persuading them about the kingdom of God.' Three months of sustained synagogue ministry before opposition forces a change of venue.

verses.text: But when some were hardened and disobedient, speaking evil of the Way before the multitude, he departed from them, and separated the disciples, reasoning daily in the school of Tyrannus.
verses.explainer: When some become stubborn and speak evil of 'the Way,' Paul withdraws and takes the disciples with him, reasoning daily in the hall of Tyrannus. He moves from a religious setting to an academic one.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': 'Hall of Tyrannus', 'explanation': "Tyrannus was probably a philosopher or teacher who rented his lecture hall. One Western text variant adds that Paul taught 'from the fifth hour to the tenth' (11 AM to 4 PM) -- the hottest part of the day when the hall would be empty and available cheaply. Paul taught during siesta hours, showing extraordinary dedication."}

verses.text: This continued for two years, so that all those who lived in Asia heard the word of the Lord Jesus, both Jews and Greeks.
verses.explainer: This continues for two years, 'so that all the residents of Asia heard the word of the Lord, both Jews and Greeks.' Paul does not travel through Asia -- he teaches in Ephesus and his students carry the message throughout the province. Ephesus becomes a training center.

thenVsNow.then: Paul's strategy in Ephesus was multiplication, not addition. By training disciples who then fanned out across the province, he reached 'all of Asia' without personally visiting every city. The churches at Colossae, Laodicea, and Hierapolis were likely planted during this period by Paul's students, not by Paul himself.
thenVsNow.now: The Ephesus model anticipates modern church-planting and leadership-development strategies. Training local leaders who multiply the message is more sustainable than one gifted individual trying to reach everyone. Paul invested deeply in a few who then reached many.

verses.text: God worked special miracles by the hands of Paul,
verses.explainer: God does 'extraordinary miracles' through Paul -- the phrasing implies these go beyond the ordinary apostolic miracles. The power at work in Ephesus is exceptional.

verses.text: so that even handkerchiefs or aprons were carried away from his body to the sick, and the diseases departed from them, and the evil spirits went out.
verses.explainer: Even handkerchiefs and aprons that had touched Paul's skin are taken to the sick, and diseases and evil spirits leave them. Like Peter's shadow in chapter 5, these miracles work through physical objects associated with the apostle.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': 'Handkerchiefs and aprons', 'explanation': "The Greek terms (soudarion and simikinthion) are Latin loanwords referring to sweat cloths and work aprons used in Paul's tentmaking trade. The miracles occurred through objects from Paul's manual labor, not through religious artifacts. God used the tools of a tradesman, not the vestments of a priest."}

verses.text: But some of the itinerant Jews, exorcists, took on themselves to invoke over those who had the evil spirits the name of the Lord Jesus, saying, “We adjure you by Jesus whom Paul preaches.”
verses.explainer: Itinerant Jewish exorcists attempt to use the name of Jesus as a formula: 'I adjure you by the Jesus whom Paul proclaims.' They have observed the power of Jesus' name but treat it as a magical incantation rather than a personal relationship.

verses.text: There were seven sons of one Sceva, a Jewish chief priest, who did this.
verses.explainer: Seven sons of a Jewish high priest named Sceva are doing this. The title 'high priest' may be self-assumed or honorary -- there is no record of a high priest named Sceva.

verses.text: The evil spirit answered, “Jesus I know, and Paul I know, but who are you?”
verses.explainer: The evil spirit answers them: 'Jesus I know, and Paul I recognize, but who are you?' The spirit distinguishes between those who have authority (Jesus, Paul) and those who do not (the sons of Sceva). Using Jesus' name without knowing Jesus personally is useless.

thenVsNow.then: In the ancient world, knowing and using powerful names was considered the key to spiritual authority. The sons of Sceva assumed Jesus' name worked like any other magical formula. The demon's response exposed the difference between formula and relationship.
thenVsNow.now: The sons of Sceva represent anyone who tries to use religious language or practices without genuine faith. Going through the motions of Christianity without knowing Christ personally is as ineffective as the sons' attempt to invoke a name they had no right to use.

verses.text: The man in whom the evil spirit was leaped on them, overpowered them, and prevailed against them, so that they fled out of that house naked and wounded.
verses.explainer: The man with the evil spirit leaps on them, overpowers all of them, and they flee the house naked and wounded. Seven men cannot handle one demonized man. The power encounter is humiliating and public.

verses.text: This became known to all, both Jews and Greeks, who lived at Ephesus. Fear fell on them all, and the name of the Lord Jesus was magnified.
verses.explainer: This becomes known to all residents of Ephesus, both Jews and Greeks. 'Fear fell upon them all, and the name of the Lord Jesus was extolled.' The failed exorcism ironically increases reverence for Jesus' name.

verses.text: Many also of those who had believed came, confessing, and declaring their deeds.
verses.explainer: Many believers come forward, confessing and divulging their magical practices. The confrontation with the sons of Sceva triggers a wave of repentance among Christians who have been secretly maintaining occult practices.

verses.text: Many of those who practiced magical arts brought their books together and burned them in the sight of all. They counted their price, and found it to be fifty thousand pieces of silver.
verses.explainer: Those who practiced magic arts bring their books together and burn them publicly. The total value is calculated at fifty thousand pieces of silver -- an enormous sum, equivalent to roughly 50,000 days' wages.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': 'Ephesian magic books', 'explanation': "Ephesus was famous throughout the ancient world for its magical practices. 'Ephesian letters' (Ephesia grammata) were well-known magical incantations. The books burned were likely collections of spells, curses, and incantations -- the tools of a thriving occult industry. Burning them was both a spiritual declaration and an economic sacrifice."}

verses.text: So the word of the Lord was growing and becoming mighty.
verses.explainer: 'So the word of the Lord continued to increase and prevail mightily.' Luke's growth summary follows the bonfire. The destruction of the old makes room for the growth of the new.

verses.text: Now after these things had ended, Paul determined in the spirit, when he had passed through Macedonia and Achaia, to go to Jerusalem, saying, “After I have been there, I must also see Rome.”
verses.explainer: Paul resolves to travel through Macedonia and Achaia and then go to Jerusalem, saying: 'After I have been there, I must also see Rome.' Paul's eyes are fixed on Rome -- the center of the world. This verse foreshadows the rest of Acts.

verses.text: Having sent into Macedonia two of those who served him, Timothy and Erastus, he himself stayed in Asia for a while.
verses.explainer: Paul sends Timothy and Erastus to Macedonia ahead of him while he stays in Asia for a while longer. The team is deployed strategically across the mission field.

verses.text: About that time there arose no small stir concerning the Way.
verses.explainer: 'About that time there arose no little disturbance concerning the Way.' Luke's characteristic understatement -- 'no little disturbance' means a full-scale riot.

verses.text: For a certain man named Demetrius, a silversmith, who made silver shrines of Artemis, brought no little business to the craftsmen,
verses.explainer: Demetrius, a silversmith who makes silver shrines of Artemis, gathers his fellow craftsmen. He provides 'no little business' (significant income) to the artisans. The economic foundation of his complaint is explicit.

verses.text: whom he gathered together, with the workmen of like occupation, and said, “Sirs, you know that by this business we have our wealth.
verses.explainer: Demetrius assembles the workers and says: 'Men, you know that from this business we have our wealth.' He leads with the economic argument before adding the religious one. His priorities are revealing.

verses.text: You see and hear, that not at Ephesus alone, but almost throughout all Asia, this Paul has persuaded and turned away many people, saying that they are no gods, that are made with hands.
verses.explainer: He reports that Paul 'has persuaded and turned away a great many people, saying that gods made with hands are not gods.' Demetrius accurately summarizes Paul's teaching -- handmade gods are not gods. The gospel's success is the silversmiths' crisis.

verses.text: Not only is there danger that this our trade come into disrepute, but also that the temple of the great goddess Artemis will be counted as nothing, and her majesty destroyed, whom all Asia and the world worships.”
verses.explainer: Demetrius warns that their trade will fall into disrepute and that 'the temple of the great goddess Artemis may be counted as nothing.' He wraps economic anxiety in religious language -- the classic formula for a riot.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': 'Temple of Artemis', 'explanation': "The Temple of Artemis at Ephesus was one of the Seven Wonders of the Ancient World -- four times the size of the Parthenon in Athens. It was both a religious center and a major bank (the temple served as a financial institution). Threatening the cult of Artemis meant threatening the city's identity, tourism industry, and financial system simultaneously."}

verses.text: When they heard this they were filled with anger, and cried out, saying, “Great is Artemis of the Ephesians!”
verses.explainer: The crowd becomes furious and begins shouting: 'Great is Artemis of the Ephesians!' The chant unites religious devotion with civic pride. Artemis worship was Ephesian identity.

verses.text: The whole city was filled with confusion, and they rushed with one accord into the theater, having seized Gaius and Aristarchus, men of Macedonia, Paul’s companions in travel.
verses.explainer: The city is filled with confusion. The mob seizes Gaius and Aristarchus, Paul's traveling companions from Macedonia, and rushes into the theater. The theater at Ephesus seated 25,000 people -- an enormous space now filled with an angry mob.

verses.text: When Paul wanted to enter in to the people, the disciples didn’t allow him.
verses.explainer: Paul wants to enter the theater, but the disciples will not let him. Paul's courage would be suicidal -- the mob would tear him apart.

verses.text: Certain also of the Asiarchs, being his friends, sent to him and begged him not to venture into the theater.
verses.explainer: Some of the 'Asiarchs' -- provincial officials who are friendly to Paul -- send him a message urging him not to venture into the theater. Paul has friends in high places who want to protect him.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': 'Asiarchs', 'explanation': "The Asiarchs were wealthy, prominent citizens who served as high priests of the imperial cult in the province of Asia. That they were friendly to Paul suggests that the gospel had reached even into the ranks of the Roman provincial elite. Their concern for Paul's safety is a remarkable detail."}

verses.text: Some therefore cried one thing, and some another, for the assembly was in confusion. Most of them didn’t know why they had come together.
verses.explainer: The assembly is in total confusion. Most people do not even know why they are there. The riot has taken on a life of its own, fueled by emotion rather than reason.

verses.text: They brought Alexander out of the multitude, the Jews putting him forward. Alexander beckoned with his hand, and would have made a defense to the people.
verses.explainer: The Jews push Alexander to the front, apparently to dissociate themselves from Paul. Alexander tries to make a defense, but when the crowd realizes he is a Jew, they shout him down.

verses.text: But when they perceived that he was a Jew, all with one voice for a time of about two hours cried out, “Great is Artemis of the Ephesians!”
verses.explainer: For about two hours the crowd chants: 'Great is Artemis of the Ephesians!' Two hours of continuous chanting -- mob hysteria sustained by rhythmic repetition. The scene is chaotic and dangerous.

verses.text: When the town clerk had quieted the multitude, he said, “You men of Ephesus, what man is there who doesn’t know that the city of the Ephesians is temple keeper of the great goddess Artemis, and of the image which fell down from Zeus?
verses.explainer: The city clerk finally quiets the crowd. As the senior municipal official, he has both authority and exposure -- if the riot continues, Rome will hold him responsible.

verses.text: Seeing then that these things can’t be denied, you ought to be quiet, and to do nothing rash.
verses.explainer: He tells the crowd: these things are undeniable, so they should calm down and not act rashly. His appeal is to civic order, not religious conviction.

verses.text: For you have brought these men here, who are neither robbers of temples nor blasphemers of your goddess.
verses.explainer: He defends Paul's companions: 'These men are neither sacrilegious nor blasphemers of our goddess.' The Christians have not committed any crime -- they have not robbed temples or insulted Artemis. Their offense is theological, not criminal.

verses.text: If therefore Demetrius and the craftsmen who are with him have a matter against anyone, the courts are open, and there are proconsuls. Let them press charges against one another.
verses.explainer: If Demetrius and the craftsmen have a complaint, the courts are open and there are proconsuls -- 'let them bring charges against one another.' The proper venue for disputes is the courtroom, not the mob.

verses.text: But if you seek anything about other matters, it will be settled in the regular assembly.
verses.explainer: If there are further questions, they can be settled in the 'regular assembly.' The city has legitimate governance structures. Mob justice is not justice.

verses.text: For indeed we are in danger of being accused concerning today’s riot, there being no cause. Concerning it, we wouldn’t be able to give an account of this commotion.”
verses.explainer: 'We are in danger of being charged with rioting today, since there is no cause that we can give to justify this commotion.' The clerk's real fear: Rome. An unauthorized assembly in a provincial city could result in loss of self-governance. The threat of Roman intervention disperses the mob.

thenVsNow.then: The city clerk's argument was entirely pragmatic: this riot could bring Roman intervention and the loss of Ephesian self-governance. He did not defend or attack the gospel -- he defended civic order. The gospel benefited from Roman law even when Roman officials were indifferent to its message.
thenVsNow.now: The Ephesus riot illustrates how the gospel can destabilize economic systems built on false religion without the church ever intending to start a riot. The gospel is inherently disruptive to industries that profit from spiritual deception.

verses.text: When he had thus spoken, he dismissed the assembly.
verses.explainer: The clerk dismisses the assembly. Order is restored. Paul and his companions are safe. The gospel has survived the most dangerous opposition it has faced since Stephen's stoning -- and survived through Roman legal process, not divine miracle.

explainer.summary: Paul travels through Macedonia and Greece, then retraces his steps toward Jerusalem. In Troas, during a long evening sermon, a young man named Eutychus falls asleep, falls from a third-floor window, and is picked up dead -- but Paul revives him. Paul then makes a poignant farewell to the Ephesian elders at Miletus, warning them of future threats both external (fierce wolves) and internal (men speaking twisted things), and committing them to God and to the word of his grace. He tells them he will never see them again.
explainer.contextInBook: Chapter 20 marks the beginning of Paul's journey toward Jerusalem and ultimately toward Rome. The Miletus speech is Paul's pastoral testament -- his last words to leaders he has trained and loved. It has the emotional weight of a deathbed farewell.

wordStudies.translatedAs: Literally 'watchmen' or 'guardians
wordStudies.meaning: Literally 'watchmen' or 'guardians.' Paul tells the Ephesian elders that the Holy Spirit has made them 'overseers' (episkopoi) to 'shepherd' (poimainein) the church. The terms elder (presbyteros), overseer (episkopos), and shepherd (poimen) are used interchangeably here, suggesting they describe the same role from different angles.

verses.text: After the uproar had ceased, Paul sent for the disciples, took leave of them, and departed to go into Macedonia.
verses.explainer: After the riot subsides, Paul gathers the disciples, encourages them, says farewell, and departs for Macedonia. The extended Ephesian ministry is over.

verses.text: When he had gone through those parts, and had encouraged them with many words, he came into Greece.
verses.explainer: Paul travels through Macedonia, 'encouraging them with many words,' then comes to Greece, where he stays three months. He is likely in Corinth during this period, where he probably writes the letter to the Romans.

verses.text: When he had spent three months there, and a plot was made against him by Jews as he was about to set sail for Syria, he determined to return through Macedonia.
verses.explainer: When the Jews plot against him as he is about to sail for Syria, Paul changes his plans and returns through Macedonia instead. Flexibility in the face of danger is wisdom, not cowardice.

verses.text: These accompanied him as far as Asia: Sopater of Beroea; Aristarchus and Secundus of the Thessalonians; Gaius of Derbe; Timothy; and Tychicus and Trophimus of Asia.
verses.explainer: Paul's traveling companions are listed: Sopater of Berea, Aristarchus and Secundus of Thessalonica, Gaius of Derbe, Timothy, and Tychicus and Trophimus of Asia. The team represents churches across the mission field -- they are carrying the collection for Jerusalem's poor.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': "Paul's collection delegation", 'explanation': 'These men represent their respective churches, accompanying Paul to deliver the collection for the poor in Jerusalem (Romans 15:25-27, 2 Corinthians 8-9). The multi-ethnic delegation was designed to demonstrate the unity of the Gentile churches with the Jewish mother church and to ensure financial transparency.'}

verses.text: But these had gone ahead, and were waiting for us at Troas.
verses.explainer: The delegates go ahead and wait for Paul and Luke in Troas. The 'we' passages resume here, indicating Luke has rejoined the team.

verses.text: We sailed away from Philippi after the days of Unleavened Bread, and came to them at Troas in five days, where we stayed seven days.
verses.explainer: After the days of Unleavened Bread, they sail from Philippi and arrive in Troas in five days. The outbound voyage (16:11) took two days; the return takes five -- the wind is against them.

verses.text: On the first day of the week, when the disciples were gathered together to break bread, Paul talked with them, intending to depart on the next day, and continued his speech until midnight.
verses.explainer: On the first day of the week, they gather to break bread. Paul speaks to them and 'prolonged his speech until midnight.' This is the earliest clear reference to Christian worship on Sunday -- 'the first day of the week.'

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': 'First day of the week', 'explanation': 'Sunday worship commemorated the resurrection, which occurred on the first day of the week. This verse, along with 1 Corinthians 16:2, shows that the early church was already meeting on Sunday, though Jewish-Christian communities may have also continued Sabbath observance.'}

thenVsNow.then: Jewish reckoning counted days from sunset to sunset. The 'first day of the week' beginning at sunset Saturday would mean this gathering took place on what we call Saturday evening. The extended meeting suggests the community savored every moment with Paul, knowing he was leaving.
thenVsNow.now: This verse is one of the key texts cited for the Christian tradition of Sunday worship. The shift from Sabbath (Saturday) to the Lord's Day (Sunday) was gradual, driven by the resurrection having occurred on a Sunday. By the mid-second century, Sunday worship was universal among Gentile Christians.

verses.text: There were many lights in the upper room where we were gathered together.
verses.explainer: There are many lamps in the upper room where they are gathered. The detail about lamps may explain what happens next -- the heat from many oil lamps in an enclosed upper room, combined with a long sermon, creates drowsy conditions.

verses.text: A certain young man named Eutychus sat in the window, weighed down with deep sleep. As Paul spoke still longer, being weighed down by his sleep, he fell down from the third floor, and was taken up dead.
verses.explainer: A young man named Eutychus, sitting in the window, sinks into a deep sleep as Paul talks on and on. He falls from the third-story window and is 'taken up dead.' Luke, the physician, uses medical language -- the young man is genuinely dead, not merely unconscious.

thenVsNow.then: Eutychus's fall from a third-story window during a long sermon is one of the most human moments in Acts. Long sermons, drowsy listeners, and open windows are a dangerous combination. Luke reports the incident without embarrassment -- the early church did not idealize its gatherings.
thenVsNow.now: The Eutychus story is frequently cited with humor in discussions about sermon length. But the deeper point is Paul's response: life interrupted ministry, and ministry was immediately redirected to meet the need. The raised young man and the continuing worship show that the power present in the gathering was real.

verses.text: Paul went down, and fell upon him, and embracing him said, “Don’t be troubled, for his life is in him.”
verses.explainer: Paul goes down, bends over Eutychus, and takes him in his arms, saying: 'Do not be alarmed, for his life is in him.' The posture echoes Elijah raising the widow's son and Elisha raising the Shunammite's son.

verses.text: When he had gone up, and had broken bread, and eaten, and had talked with them a long while, even until break of day, he departed.
verses.explainer: Paul goes back upstairs, breaks bread, eats, and continues talking until dawn. The resurrection of Eutychus does not end the gathering -- if anything, it energizes it. Paul talks until sunrise.

verses.text: They brought the boy in alive, and were greatly comforted.
verses.explainer: They bring the young man away alive and are 'not a little comforted.' Luke's characteristic understatement. The boy who was dead is alive, and the community is encouraged.

verses.text: But we, going ahead to the ship, set sail for Assos, intending to take Paul aboard there; for he had so arranged, intending himself to go by land.
verses.explainer: Paul goes ahead on foot to Assos while the others sail around. The overland route is about twenty miles. Paul may want time alone for prayer and reflection before the journey to Jerusalem.

verses.text: When he met us at Assos, we took him aboard, and came to Mitylene.
verses.explainer: Paul meets them at Assos and they sail to Mitylene, the capital of the island of Lesbos. The coastal journey unfolds in daily stages.

verses.text: Sailing from there, we came the following day opposite Chios. The next day we touched at Samos and stayed at Trogyllium, and the day after we came to Miletus.
verses.explainer: They sail past Chios, cross to Samos, and arrive at Miletus. Paul has decided to bypass Ephesus to save time -- he is hurrying to reach Jerusalem by Pentecost.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': 'Island-hopping voyage', 'explanation': 'The route from Troas to Miletus followed the standard coastal shipping lanes, using the chain of Aegean islands (Chios, Samos) as waypoints. Ancient ships sailed within sight of land whenever possible, anchoring at islands each night. This leg of the voyage covered about 170 nautical miles over several days.'}

verses.text: For Paul had determined to sail past Ephesus, that he might not have to spend time in Asia; for he was hastening, if it were possible for him, to be in Jerusalem on the day of Pentecost.
verses.explainer: Paul has decided to sail past Ephesus so he would not have to spend time in Asia. He is eager to be in Jerusalem, 'if possible, on the day of Pentecost.' A Jewish Christian celebrating a Jewish festival in the Jewish capital -- Paul's Judaism remains intact.

verses.text: From Miletus he sent to Ephesus, and called to himself the elders of the assembly.
verses.explainer: From Miletus, Paul sends word to Ephesus and summons the elders of the church. Though he cannot visit Ephesus, he wants a final meeting with its leaders. Miletus is about thirty miles south of Ephesus.

verses.text: When they had come to him, he said to them, “You yourselves know, from the first day that I set foot in Asia, how I was with you all the time,
verses.explainer: Paul begins his farewell: 'You yourselves know how I lived among you the whole time from the first day I set foot in Asia.' He appeals to their firsthand knowledge of his conduct. His life is his argument.

verses.text: serving the Lord with all humility, with many tears, and with trials which happened to me by the plots of the Jews;
verses.explainer: He describes his ministry: 'serving the Lord with all humility and with tears and with trials.' Paul's ministry was not triumphant or easy -- it was marked by humility, emotional pain, and constant adversity.

verses.text: how I didn’t shrink from declaring to you anything that was profitable, teaching you publicly and from house to house,
verses.explainer: He held nothing back that was helpful, 'teaching you in public and from house to house.' His ministry combined public proclamation with private instruction. Both venues were essential.

verses.text: testifying both to Jews and to Greeks repentance toward God, and faith toward our Lord Jesus.
verses.explainer: He testified 'to both Jews and Greeks of repentance toward God and of faith in our Lord Jesus Christ.' The gospel message is constant regardless of audience: turn to God, trust in Jesus.

verses.text: Now, behold, I go bound by the Spirit to Jerusalem, not knowing what will happen to me there;
verses.explainer: Paul is going to Jerusalem, 'constrained by the Spirit,' not knowing what will happen to him there. He senses the Spirit's compulsion but not the Spirit's itinerary. Obedience does not require omniscience.

verses.text: except that the Holy Spirit testifies in every city, saying that bonds and afflictions wait for me.
verses.explainer: The Holy Spirit testifies to him in every city that imprisonment and afflictions await him in Jerusalem. The warnings are consistent and cumulative. Paul is walking into suffering with his eyes open.

verses.text: But these things don’t count; nor do I hold my life dear to myself, so that I may finish my race with joy, and the ministry which I received from the Lord Jesus, to fully testify to the Good News of the grace of God.
verses.explainer: 'But I do not account my life of any value nor as precious to myself, if only I may finish my course and the ministry that I received from the Lord Jesus, to testify to the gospel of the grace of God.' Paul's mission matters more than his survival. This is the heart of his farewell.

thenVsNow.then: Paul valued completing his mission above preserving his life. This was not suicidal recklessness but a deliberate prioritization: the gospel is more important than the messenger. Finishing well mattered more than finishing safely.
thenVsNow.now: Paul's willingness to sacrifice personal comfort and safety for the sake of his calling challenges every cost-benefit calculation modern believers make. The question is not 'What is safest?' but 'What will I regret not doing?'

verses.text: “Now, behold, I know that you all, among whom I went about preaching God’s Kingdom, will see my face no more.
verses.explainer: 'I know that none of you among whom I have gone about proclaiming the kingdom will see my face again.' The finality is emotional and pastoral. This is a permanent goodbye.

verses.text: Therefore I testify to you today that I am clean from the blood of all men,
verses.explainer: Paul declares himself 'innocent of the blood of all' because he has not hesitated to declare 'the whole counsel of God.' He did not curate a comfortable message; he delivered the complete truth.

verses.text: for I didn’t shrink from declaring to you the whole counsel of God.
verses.explainer: He has declared the 'whole counsel of God' -- the full range of God's purposes, not just the parts that are popular or easy to hear. Complete pastoral faithfulness means teaching everything, not just the audience's preferences.

verses.text:
Take heed, therefore, to yourselves, and to all the flock, in which the Holy Spirit has made you overseers, to shepherd the assembly of the Lord and
 God which he purchased with his own blood.
verses.explainer: 'Pay careful attention to yourselves and to all the flock, in which the Holy Spirit has made you overseers, to care for the church of God, which he obtained with his own blood.' The elders' authority comes from the Spirit, and their responsibility is shepherding -- a church purchased at the cost of Christ's blood.

verses.text: For I know that after my departure, vicious wolves will enter in among you, not sparing the flock.
verses.explainer: Paul warns: 'After my departure fierce wolves will come in among you, not sparing the flock.' External threats -- false teachers who will attack the community from outside. Paul sees the danger clearly.

verses.text: Men will arise from among your own selves, speaking perverse things, to draw away the disciples after them.
verses.explainer: 'From among your own selves will arise men speaking twisted things, to draw away the disciples after them.' Internal threats -- leaders who distort the truth for personal following. This is the more dangerous threat because it comes from within.

verses.text: Therefore watch, remembering that for a period of three years I didn’t cease to admonish everyone night and day with tears.
verses.explainer: 'Therefore be alert, remembering that for three years I did not cease night or day to admonish every one with tears.' Paul's investment was total -- three years of constant, emotional, personal pastoral work. His tears authenticate his words.

verses.text: Now, brothers, I entrust you to God, and to the word of his grace, which is able to build up, and to give you the inheritance among all those who are sanctified.
verses.explainer: 'And now I commend you to God and to the word of his grace, which is able to build you up and to give you the inheritance among all those who are sanctified.' Paul's final resource for the elders is not himself but God and his word. When the apostle leaves, the word remains.

verses.text: I coveted no one’s silver, or gold, or clothing.
verses.explainer: 'I coveted no one's silver or gold or apparel.' Paul's financial integrity is impeccable. He took nothing from the Ephesians for personal gain.

verses.text: You yourselves know that these hands served my necessities, and those who were with me.
verses.explainer: 'You yourselves know that these hands ministered to my necessities and to those who were with me.' Paul worked with his own hands to support himself and his team. He shows them his calloused hands as evidence.

verses.text:
In all things I gave you an example, that so laboring you ought to help the weak, and to remember the words of the Lord Jesus, that he himself said,
‘It is more blessed to give than to receive.’”
verses.explainer: 'In all things I have shown you that by working hard in this way we must help the weak. And remember the words of the Lord Jesus: It is more blessed to give than to receive.' Paul quotes a saying of Jesus not found in any Gospel. The oral tradition about Jesus was wider than the written Gospels.

verses.text: When he had spoken these things, he knelt down and prayed with them all.
verses.explainer: When Paul finishes, he kneels down and prays with all of them. The farewell moves from words to prayer -- the final act of a pastor with his people.

verses.text: They all wept a lot, and fell on Paul’s neck and kissed him,
verses.explainer: There is 'much weeping' from everyone. They embrace Paul and kiss him. The emotion is raw and public. These are not distant professional relationships but deep bonds forged through shared suffering and ministry.

verses.text: sorrowing most of all because of the word which he had spoken, that they should see his face no more. And they accompanied him to the ship.
verses.explainer: They are sorrowful 'most of all because of the word he had spoken, that they would not see his face again.' They escort him to the ship. The Miletus farewell is one of the most emotionally powerful scenes in Acts.

explainer.summary: Paul sails toward Jerusalem despite repeated prophetic warnings. In Tyre and Caesarea, believers plead with him not to go. The prophet Agabus dramatically acts out Paul's coming arrest. Paul responds: 'I am ready not only to be imprisoned but even to die in Jerusalem for the name of the Lord Jesus.' He arrives in Jerusalem, where James and the elders advise him to demonstrate his continued loyalty to the law by sponsoring four men in a purification rite. While at the Temple, Jews from Asia recognize Paul, accuse him of bringing Gentiles into the Temple, and start a riot. Roman soldiers rescue Paul from the mob.
themes: The tension between Jewish and Gentile Christianity
explainer.contextInBook: Chapter 21 is the turning point of Acts' second half. From this point forward, Paul is never free again. His arrest in the Temple begins a sequence of trials and transfers that will eventually bring him to Rome -- fulfilling both his own desire (19:21) and God's plan (23:11).

wordStudies.translatedAs: To tie, to bind with chains
wordStudies.meaning: To tie, to bind with chains. Agabus uses Paul's belt to bind his own hands and feet, prophesying that Paul will be bound in Jerusalem. The word appears throughout the rest of Acts as Paul moves from one form of bondage to another -- arrested, chained, transferred, imprisoned. Yet even in chains, Paul preaches freely.

verses.text: When we had departed from them and had set sail, we came with a straight course to Cos, and the next day to Rhodes, and from there to Patara.
verses.explainer: After tearing themselves away from the Ephesian elders, they sail to Cos, then Rhodes, and then Patara. The verb 'tearing away' (apospao) expresses the emotional difficulty of the departure.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': 'Cos and Rhodes', 'explanation': "Cos was famous as the birthplace of Hippocrates and its medical school -- a fitting waypoint for Luke the physician. Rhodes was one of the great islands of the ancient world, home to the Colossus (one of the Seven Wonders). Paul's voyage passes through some of the most storied locations in the Mediterranean."}

verses.text: Having found a ship crossing over to Phoenicia, we went aboard, and set sail.
verses.explainer: They find a ship crossing to Phoenicia and sail on it. The journey continues southward along the Mediterranean coast toward Palestine.

verses.text: When we had come in sight of Cyprus, leaving it on the left hand, we sailed to Syria, and landed at Tyre, for there the ship was to unload her cargo.
verses.explainer: They sight Cyprus, pass it on the left, sail to Syria, and land at Tyre, where the ship unloads cargo. The seven-day layover while cargo is unloaded gives them time with the local church.

verses.text: Having found disciples, we stayed there seven days. These said to Paul through the Spirit, that he should not go up to Jerusalem.
verses.explainer: They find disciples in Tyre who, 'through the Spirit,' tell Paul not to go to Jerusalem. The Spirit reveals the danger; the believers interpret this as a reason not to go. Paul hears the same Spirit and concludes he must go anyway.

thenVsNow.then: The tension between the Spirit's warnings and Paul's determination to go to Jerusalem is one of the most debated passages in Acts. Were the Tyre believers correctly interpreting the Spirit, or were they applying human caution to divine revelation? Paul seems to distinguish between the Spirit's prediction of suffering and the believers' conclusion that he should avoid it.
thenVsNow.now: The passage raises important questions about discernment: How do we distinguish between divine warning (meant to prepare us) and divine prohibition (meant to redirect us)? Paul's example suggests that foreknowledge of suffering is not necessarily a command to avoid it.

verses.text: When those days were over, we departed and went on our journey. They all, with wives and children, brought us on our way until we were out of the city. Kneeling down on the beach, we prayed.
